INSTANCE_ID="i-django-prod-001"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Finding EIP for instance: $INSTANCE_ID"

EIP_INFO=$(aws ec2 describe-addresses \
  --filters "Name=instance-id,Values=$INSTANCE_ID" \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'Addresses[0].[PublicIp,AllocationId]' \
  --output text)

if [ -n "$EIP_INFO" ] && [ "$EIP_INFO" != "None" ]; then
    PUBLIC_IP=$(echo $EIP_INFO | awk '{print $1}')
    ALLOC_ID=$(echo $EIP_INFO | awk '{print $2}')
    echo "✓ Found EIP:"
    echo "  Public IP: $PUBLIC_IP"
    echo "  Allocation ID: $ALLOC_ID"
else
    echo "✗ No EIP associated with this instance"
fi
Finding EIP for instance: i-django-prod-001 ✓ Found EIP: Public IP: 52.87.123.45 Allocation ID: eipalloc-prod123456789
# Get EIP for specific instance from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost instance_eip = { 'instance_id': 'i-django-prod-001', 'public_ip': '52.87.123.45', 'allocation_id': 'eipalloc-prod123456789' } print(f"EIP for Django Instance") print(f" Instance: {instance_eip['instance_id']}") print(f" Public IP: {instance_eip['public_ip']}") print(f" Allocation: {instance_eip['allocation_id']}") print(f"\n✓ Instance has static public IP")
Example 4: Filter EIPs by Environment Tag

List Elastic IPs filtered by environment tag for production/staging separation.

#!/bin/bash
# Filter EIPs by environment
REGION="us-east-1"
ENVIRONMENT="Production"

echo "Listing $ENVIRONMENT environment EIPs..."

aws ec2 describe-addresses \
  --filters "Name=tag:Environment,Values=$ENVIRONMENT" \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'Addresses[*].[PublicIp,InstanceId,Tags[?Key==`Name`].Value|[0]]' \
  --output table
Listing Production environment EIPs... ---------------------------------------------------------- | DescribeAddresses | +----------------+---------------------+------------------+ | 52.87.123.45 | i-django-prod-001 | anihpj-prod-eip | | 52.87.123.48 | i-bastion-001 | anihpj-bastion | +----------------+---------------------+------------------+
# Filter EIPs by environment from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost production_eips = [ {'ip': '52.87.123.45', 'name': 'anihpj-prod-eip', 'instance': 'i-django-prod-001'}, {'ip': '52.87.123.48', 'name': 'anihpj-bastion', 'instance': 'i-bastion-001'} ] print(f"Production Environment EIPs: {len(production_eips)}") for eip in production_eips: print(f" {eip['ip']} - {eip['name']} ({eip['instance']})") print(f"\n✓ Production EIP inventory complete")
Example 5: Generate EIP Report for Audit

Generate a comprehensive EIP report for security audit and compliance.

#!/bin/bash
# Generate EIP audit report
REGION="us-east-1"
REPORT_FILE="eip_audit_report.json"

echo "Generating EIP audit report..."

aws ec2 describe-addresses \
  --region $REGION \
  --output json | jq '{
    report_date: now | strftime("%Y-%m-%d"),
    total_eips: (.Addresses | length),
    associated: (.Addresses | map(select(.AssociationId != null)) | length),
    unassociated: (.Addresses | map(select(.AssociationId == null)) | length),
    monthly_waste: ((.Addresses | map(select(.AssociationId == null)) | length) * 3.60),
    eips: [.Addresses[] | {
      ip: .PublicIp,
      allocation_id: .AllocationId,
      associated: (.AssociationId != null),
      instance: .InstanceId,
      name: (.Tags // [] | map(select(.Key=="Name")) | .[0].Value // "unnamed")
    }]
  }' > $REPORT_FILE

cat $REPORT_FILE
echo ""
echo "✓ Report saved to $REPORT_FILE"
Generating EIP audit report... { "report_date": "2026-01-13", "total_eips": 6, "associated": 4, "unassociated": 2, "monthly_waste": 7.20, "eips": [ {"ip": "52.87.123.45", "allocation_id": "eipalloc-prod123", "associated": true, "instance": "i-django-prod-001", "name": "anihpj-prod-eip"}, {"ip": "52.87.123.46", "allocation_id": "eipalloc-nat1", "associated": true, "instance": null, "name": "anihpj-nat-az1"}, {"ip": "52.87.123.60", "allocation_id": "eipalloc-unused1", "associated": false, "instance": null, "name": "anihpj-old-staging"} ] } ✓ Report saved to eip_audit_report.json
# Generate EIP audit report from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone audit_report = { 'report_date': timezone.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d'), 'total_eips': 6, 'associated': 4, 'unassociated': 2, 'monthly_waste': 7.20, 'compliance_status': 'Review Required' } print(f"EIP Audit Report - {audit_report['report_date']}") print(f"=" * 40) print(f"Total EIPs: {audit_report['total_eips']}") print(f"Associated: {audit_report['associated']}") print(f"Unassociated: {audit_report['unassociated']}") print(f"Monthly Waste: ${audit_report['monthly_waste']}") print(f"Status: {audit_report['compliance_status']}") print(f"\n✓ Audit report generated")

release-address

Release an Elastic IP address back to AWS's public IP pool. Essential for cost optimization - unassociated EIPs incur charges. Use after decommissioning Django servers, cleaning up test environments, or when static IPs are no longer needed.

Syntax
aws ec2 release-address \
  --allocation-id  \
  [--public-ip ] \
  [--network-border-group ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --allocation-id (string): [Required for VPC] The allocation ID of the EIP
  • --public-ip (string): The public IP address (EC2-Classic only, deprecated)
  • --network-border-group (string): The network border group for the EIP
  • --region (string): AWS region where the EIP exists
Return Value
  • None: Successful release returns no output (exit code 0)
  • Error: Returns error if EIP is still associated or doesn't exist
Example 1: Release Unused EIP After Server Decommission

Release an EIP after decommissioning a Django server to stop charges.

#!/bin/bash
# Release EIP after server decommission
ALLOC_ID="eipalloc-unused123456"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Releasing EIP: $ALLOC_ID"

# First verify it's not associated
ASSOC=$(aws ec2 describe-addresses \
  --allocation-ids $ALLOC_ID \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'Addresses[0].AssociationId' \
  --output text)

if [ "$ASSOC" != "None" ] && [ -n "$ASSOC" ]; then
    echo "⚠️  EIP is still associated. Disassociate first."
    exit 1
fi

# Release the EIP
aws ec2 release-address \
  --allocation-id $ALLOC_ID \
  --region $REGION

if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "✓ EIP released successfully"
    echo "✓ Monthly savings: \$3.60"
else
    echo "✗ Failed to release EIP"
fi
Releasing EIP: eipalloc-unused123456 ✓ EIP released successfully ✓ Monthly savings: $3.60
# Release EIP after server decommission from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost released_eip = { 'allocation_id': 'eipalloc-unused123456', 'public_ip': '52.87.123.60', 'was_associated': False, 'reason': 'Server decommissioned' } print(f"EIP Release") print(f" Allocation: {released_eip['allocation_id']}") print(f" IP Address: {released_eip['public_ip']}") print(f" Reason: {released_eip['reason']}") print(f"\n✓ EIP released - $3.60/month saved")
Example 2: Bulk Release All Unassociated EIPs

Release all unassociated EIPs for cost cleanup.

#!/bin/bash
# Bulk release unassociated EIPs
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Finding and releasing all unassociated EIPs..."

UNASSOCIATED=$(aws ec2 describe-addresses \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'Addresses[?AssociationId==`null`].AllocationId' \
  --output text)

if [ -z "$UNASSOCIATED" ]; then
    echo "✓ No unassociated EIPs to release"
    exit 0
fi

COUNT=0
for ALLOC_ID in $UNASSOCIATED; do
    echo "Releasing: $ALLOC_ID"
    aws ec2 release-address \
      --allocation-id $ALLOC_ID \
      --region $REGION
    if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
        ((COUNT++))
    fi
done

SAVINGS=$(echo "$COUNT * 3.60" | bc)
echo ""
echo "✓ Released $COUNT EIPs"
echo "✓ Monthly savings: \$$SAVINGS"
Finding and releasing all unassociated EIPs... Releasing: eipalloc-unused123456 Releasing: eipalloc-unused789012 ✓ Released 2 EIPs ✓ Monthly savings: $7.20
# Bulk release unassociated EIPs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost released_eips = [ 'eipalloc-unused123456', 'eipalloc-unused789012' ] count = len(released_eips) savings = count * 3.60 print(f"Bulk EIP Release") for alloc_id in released_eips: print(f" ✓ Released: {alloc_id}") print(f"\nTotal released: {count}") print(f"Monthly savings: ${savings}")
Example 3: Safe Release with Confirmation

Release EIP with confirmation showing details before release.

#!/bin/bash
# Safe EIP release with confirmation
ALLOC_ID="eipalloc-old-staging"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "=== EIP Release Confirmation ==="

# Get EIP details
EIP_DETAILS=$(aws ec2 describe-addresses \
  --allocation-ids $ALLOC_ID \
  --region $REGION \
  --output json)

PUBLIC_IP=$(echo $EIP_DETAILS | jq -r '.Addresses[0].PublicIp')
NAME=$(echo $EIP_DETAILS | jq -r '.Addresses[0].Tags[]? | select(.Key=="Name").Value // "unnamed"')

echo "EIP to release:"
echo "  IP Address: $PUBLIC_IP"
echo "  Name: $NAME"
echo "  Allocation: $ALLOC_ID"
echo ""

read -p "Are you sure you want to release this EIP? (yes/no): " CONFIRM

if [ "$CONFIRM" == "yes" ]; then
    aws ec2 release-address \
      --allocation-id $ALLOC_ID \
      --region $REGION
    echo "✓ EIP released"
else
    echo "Release cancelled"
fi
=== EIP Release Confirmation === EIP to release: IP Address: 52.87.123.60 Name: anihpj-old-staging Allocation: eipalloc-old-staging Are you sure you want to release this EIP? (yes/no): yes ✓ EIP released
# Safe release with confirmation from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost eip_to_release = { 'allocation_id': 'eipalloc-old-staging', 'public_ip': '52.87.123.60', 'name': 'anihpj-old-staging', 'confirmed': True } print(f"EIP Release Confirmation") print(f" IP: {eip_to_release['public_ip']}") print(f" Name: {eip_to_release['name']}") if eip_to_release['confirmed']: print(f"\n✓ EIP released successfully") else: print(f"\n✗ Release cancelled")
Example 4: Disassociate and Release in One Flow

Disassociate EIP from instance then release it.

#!/bin/bash
# Disassociate and release EIP
ALLOC_ID="eipalloc-decomm123"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Disassociating and releasing EIP..."

# Get association ID
ASSOC_ID=$(aws ec2 describe-addresses \
  --allocation-ids $ALLOC_ID \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'Addresses[0].AssociationId' \
  --output text)

# Disassociate if associated
if [ "$ASSOC_ID" != "None" ] && [ -n "$ASSOC_ID" ]; then
    echo "Disassociating: $ASSOC_ID"
    aws ec2 disassociate-address \
      --association-id $ASSOC_ID \
      --region $REGION
    echo "✓ Disassociated"
fi

# Wait briefly
sleep 2

# Release the EIP
aws ec2 release-address \
  --allocation-id $ALLOC_ID \
  --region $REGION

echo "✓ EIP released successfully"
Disassociating and releasing EIP... Disassociating: eipassoc-decomm123 ✓ Disassociated ✓ EIP released successfully
# Disassociate and release EIP from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost eip_cleanup = { 'allocation_id': 'eipalloc-decomm123', 'association_id': 'eipassoc-decomm123', 'disassociated': True, 'released': True } print(f"EIP Cleanup Flow") print(f" 1. Disassociate: ✓") print(f" 2. Release: ✓") print(f"\n✓ EIP fully cleaned up")
Example 5: Release EIPs by Tag (Environment Cleanup)

Release all EIPs tagged for a specific environment during cleanup.

#!/bin/bash
# Release EIPs by environment tag
ENVIRONMENT="Development"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Releasing all $ENVIRONMENT EIPs..."

# Get unassociated EIPs for this environment
EIPS=$(aws ec2 describe-addresses \
  --filters "Name=tag:Environment,Values=$ENVIRONMENT" \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'Addresses[?AssociationId==`null`].[AllocationId,PublicIp]' \
  --output text)

if [ -z "$EIPS" ]; then
    echo "No unassociated $ENVIRONMENT EIPs found"
    exit 0
fi

echo "EIPs to release:"
echo "$EIPS"
echo ""

while read ALLOC_ID PUBLIC_IP; do
    echo "Releasing $PUBLIC_IP ($ALLOC_ID)..."
    aws ec2 release-address \
      --allocation-id $ALLOC_ID \
      --region $REGION
done <<< "$EIPS"

echo "✓ Environment cleanup complete"
Releasing all Development EIPs... EIPs to release: eipalloc-dev123 52.87.123.70 eipalloc-dev456 52.87.123.71 Releasing 52.87.123.70 (eipalloc-dev123)... Releasing 52.87.123.71 (eipalloc-dev456)... ✓ Environment cleanup complete
# Release EIPs by environment tag from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost environment = 'Development' released_eips = [ {'allocation_id': 'eipalloc-dev123', 'ip': '52.87.123.70'}, {'allocation_id': 'eipalloc-dev456', 'ip': '52.87.123.71'} ] print(f"{environment} Environment EIP Cleanup") for eip in released_eips: print(f" ✓ Released: {eip['ip']}") print(f"\nTotal: {len(released_eips)} EIPs released") print(f"Monthly savings: ${len(released_eips) * 3.60}")

create-volume

Create an Amazon EBS volume for persistent block storage. Essential for Django deployments requiring data persistence beyond instance lifecycle - database storage, media files, logs, and backups. Supports gp3, gp2, io1, io2, st1, and sc1 volume types.

Syntax
aws ec2 create-volume \
  --availability-zone  \
  --size  \
  [--volume-type ] \
  [--iops ] \
  [--throughput ] \
  [--encrypted] \
  [--kms-key-id ] \
  [--snapshot-id ] \
  [--tag-specifications ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --availability-zone (string): [Required] The AZ where the volume will be created (must match instance AZ)
  • --size (integer): Size in GiB (1-16384 for gp2/gp3, up to 64 TiB for io2)
  • --volume-type (string): gp3 (default), gp2, io1, io2, st1, sc1
  • --iops (integer): Provisioned IOPS (io1/io2: up to 64000, gp3: up to 16000)
  • --throughput (integer): Throughput in MiB/s (gp3 only: 125-1000)
  • --encrypted (flag): Enable EBS encryption
  • --kms-key-id (string): KMS key for encryption
  • --snapshot-id (string): Create volume from snapshot
  • --tag-specifications (list): Tags to apply to the volume
Return Value
  • VolumeId (String): The ID of the created volume
  • Size (Integer): Size in GiB
  • VolumeType (String): The volume type
  • State (String): Volume state (creating, available, in-use)
  • AvailabilityZone (String): The AZ of the volume
  • Encrypted (Boolean): Whether the volume is encrypted
Example 1: Create GP3 Volume for Django Media Storage

Create a gp3 volume for storing Django media files uploaded by users.

#!/bin/bash
# Create GP3 volume for Django media storage
AZ="us-east-1a"
SIZE=100  # 100 GiB for media files
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Creating EBS volume for Django media storage..."

VOLUME_ID=$(aws ec2 create-volume \
  --availability-zone $AZ \
  --size $SIZE \
  --volume-type gp3 \
  --throughput 250 \
  --iops 4000 \
  --encrypted \
  --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=volume,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=anihpj-django-media},{Key=Environment,Value=Production},{Key=Application,Value=anihpj}]' \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'VolumeId' \
  --output text)

echo "✓ Volume created: $VOLUME_ID"

# Wait for volume to be available
echo "Waiting for volume to be available..."
aws ec2 wait volume-available --volume-ids $VOLUME_ID --region $REGION

echo "✓ Volume is ready for attachment"
echo "  Size: ${SIZE} GiB"
echo "  Type: gp3"
echo "  IOPS: 4000"
echo "  Throughput: 250 MiB/s"
Creating EBS volume for Django media storage... ✓ Volume created: vol-0abc123django456 Waiting for volume to be available... ✓ Volume is ready for attachment Size: 100 GiB Type: gp3 IOPS: 4000 Throughput: 250 MiB/s
# Create GP3 volume for Django media storage from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost volume = { 'volume_id': 'vol-0abc123django456', 'size_gib': 100, 'volume_type': 'gp3', 'iops': 4000, 'throughput': 250, 'encrypted': True, 'state': 'available' } print(f"Django Media Volume Created") print(f" Volume ID: {volume['volume_id']}") print(f" Size: {volume['size_gib']} GiB") print(f" Type: {volume['volume_type']}") print(f" IOPS: {volume['iops']}") print(f" Encrypted: ✓") print(f"\n✓ Ready for attachment to Django instance")
Example 2: Create High-IOPS Volume for Database

Create an io2 volume for PostgreSQL database requiring high IOPS.

#!/bin/bash
# Create high-IOPS volume for PostgreSQL
AZ="us-east-1a"
SIZE=500  # 500 GiB for database
IOPS=20000  # High IOPS for database
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Creating high-IOPS EBS volume for PostgreSQL..."

RESULT=$(aws ec2 create-volume \
  --availability-zone $AZ \
  --size $SIZE \
  --volume-type io2 \
  --iops $IOPS \
  --encrypted \
  --kms-key-id alias/anihpj-db-key \
  --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=volume,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=anihpj-postgres-data},{Key=Environment,Value=Production},{Key=Application,Value=anihpj-db}]' \
  --region $REGION \
  --output json)

VOLUME_ID=$(echo $RESULT | jq -r '.VolumeId')
STATE=$(echo $RESULT | jq -r '.State')

echo "✓ Database volume created"
echo "  Volume ID: $VOLUME_ID"
echo "  Size: ${SIZE} GiB"
echo "  Type: io2 (Block Express)"
echo "  IOPS: $IOPS"
echo "  State: $STATE"
echo "  Encryption: KMS (anihpj-db-key)"
Creating high-IOPS EBS volume for PostgreSQL... ✓ Database volume created Volume ID: vol-0db123postgres456 Size: 500 GiB Type: io2 (Block Express) IOPS: 20000 State: creating Encryption: KMS (anihpj-db-key)
# Create high-IOPS volume for PostgreSQL from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost db_volume = { 'volume_id': 'vol-0db123postgres456', 'size_gib': 500, 'volume_type': 'io2', 'iops': 20000, 'encrypted': True, 'kms_key': 'alias/anihpj-db-key', 'purpose': 'PostgreSQL data' } print(f"PostgreSQL Database Volume") print(f" Volume: {db_volume['volume_id']}") print(f" Size: {db_volume['size_gib']} GiB") print(f" IOPS: {db_volume['iops']}") print(f" KMS: {db_volume['kms_key']}") print(f"\n✓ High-performance database storage ready")
Example 3: Create Volume from Snapshot (Restore Backup)

Create a volume from a snapshot to restore Django data from backup.

#!/bin/bash
# Create volume from snapshot (restore)
SNAPSHOT_ID="snap-0backup123456789"
AZ="us-east-1a"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Restoring volume from snapshot..."
echo "  Snapshot: $SNAPSHOT_ID"

VOLUME_ID=$(aws ec2 create-volume \
  --snapshot-id $SNAPSHOT_ID \
  --availability-zone $AZ \
  --volume-type gp3 \
  --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=volume,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=anihpj-media-restored},{Key=RestoredFrom,Value=snap-0backup123456789},{Key=RestoredDate,Value=2026-01-13}]' \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'VolumeId' \
  --output text)

echo "✓ Volume created from snapshot: $VOLUME_ID"

# Wait for restoration
aws ec2 wait volume-available --volume-ids $VOLUME_ID --region $REGION
echo "✓ Volume restoration complete and ready"
Restoring volume from snapshot... Snapshot: snap-0backup123456789 ✓ Volume created from snapshot: vol-0restored456789 ✓ Volume restoration complete and ready
# Create volume from snapshot from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone restore = { 'snapshot_id': 'snap-0backup123456789', 'new_volume_id': 'vol-0restored456789', 'restore_date': timezone.now(), 'state': 'available' } print(f"Volume Restored from Backup") print(f" Source Snapshot: {restore['snapshot_id']}") print(f" New Volume: {restore['new_volume_id']}") print(f" State: {restore['state']}") print(f"\n✓ Data restoration complete")
Example 4: Create Log Volume with Cold Storage (sc1)

Create a cold storage volume for Django log archival.

#!/bin/bash
# Create cold storage volume for logs
AZ="us-east-1a"
SIZE=1000  # 1 TiB for log archive
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Creating cold storage volume for log archival..."

VOLUME_ID=$(aws ec2 create-volume \
  --availability-zone $AZ \
  --size $SIZE \
  --volume-type sc1 \
  --encrypted \
  --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=volume,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=anihpj-logs-archive},{Key=StorageTier,Value=Cold},{Key=RetentionDays,Value=365}]' \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'VolumeId' \
  --output text)

echo "✓ Cold storage volume created: $VOLUME_ID"
echo "  Size: 1 TiB"
echo "  Type: sc1 (Cold HDD)"
echo "  Cost: ~\$0.015/GiB-month"
echo "  Monthly cost: ~\$15"
Creating cold storage volume for log archival... ✓ Cold storage volume created: vol-0logs123archive456 Size: 1 TiB Type: sc1 (Cold HDD) Cost: ~$0.015/GiB-month Monthly cost: ~$15
# Create cold storage for logs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost log_volume = { 'volume_id': 'vol-0logs123archive456', 'size_gib': 1000, 'volume_type': 'sc1', 'monthly_cost': 15.00, 'purpose': 'Log archival' } print(f"Log Archive Volume Created") print(f" Volume: {log_volume['volume_id']}") print(f" Size: {log_volume['size_gib']} GiB (1 TiB)") print(f" Type: sc1 (Cold HDD)") print(f" Cost: ${log_volume['monthly_cost']}/month") print(f"\n✓ Cost-effective log storage ready")
Example 5: Create Multiple Volumes for Multi-Tier Django

Create volumes for a complete Django deployment (app, db, media, logs).

#!/bin/bash
# Create volumes for multi-tier Django deployment
AZ="us-east-1a"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Creating volumes for anihpj Django multi-tier deployment..."
echo ""

# App volume (50 GiB gp3)
APP_VOL=$(aws ec2 create-volume \
  --availability-zone $AZ \
  --size 50 \
  --volume-type gp3 \
  --encrypted \
  --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=volume,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=anihpj-app-data},{Key=Tier,Value=Application}]' \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'VolumeId' --output text)
echo "✓ App volume: $APP_VOL (50 GiB gp3)"

# Database volume (200 GiB io2)
DB_VOL=$(aws ec2 create-volume \
  --availability-zone $AZ \
  --size 200 \
  --volume-type io2 \
  --iops 10000 \
  --encrypted \
  --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=volume,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=anihpj-db-data},{Key=Tier,Value=Database}]' \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'VolumeId' --output text)
echo "✓ DB volume: $DB_VOL (200 GiB io2, 10000 IOPS)"

# Media volume (500 GiB gp3)
MEDIA_VOL=$(aws ec2 create-volume \
  --availability-zone $AZ \
  --size 500 \
  --volume-type gp3 \
  --throughput 500 \
  --encrypted \
  --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=volume,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=anihpj-media},{Key=Tier,Value=Media}]' \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'VolumeId' --output text)
echo "✓ Media volume: $MEDIA_VOL (500 GiB gp3, 500 MiB/s)"

echo ""
echo "✓ All volumes created for anihpj deployment"
Creating volumes for anihpj Django multi-tier deployment... ✓ App volume: vol-0app123abc (50 GiB gp3) ✓ DB volume: vol-0db456def (200 GiB io2, 10000 IOPS) ✓ Media volume: vol-0media789ghi (500 GiB gp3, 500 MiB/s) ✓ All volumes created for anihpj deployment
# Create volumes for multi-tier Django from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost volumes = [ {'name': 'anihpj-app-data', 'id': 'vol-0app123abc', 'size': 50, 'type': 'gp3', 'tier': 'Application'}, {'name': 'anihpj-db-data', 'id': 'vol-0db456def', 'size': 200, 'type': 'io2', 'tier': 'Database'}, {'name': 'anihpj-media', 'id': 'vol-0media789ghi', 'size': 500, 'type': 'gp3', 'tier': 'Media'} ] print(f"anihpj Multi-Tier Storage") print(f"=" * 40) total_size = sum(v['size'] for v in volumes) for vol in volumes: print(f" {vol['tier']}: {vol['id']}") print(f" Size: {vol['size']} GiB | Type: {vol['type']}") print(f"\nTotal Storage: {total_size} GiB") print(f"✓ All tiers provisioned")

attach-volume

Attach an EBS volume to a running EC2 instance. The volume must be in the same Availability Zone as the instance. Essential for connecting persistent storage to Django servers for media files, databases, and application data.

Syntax
aws ec2 attach-volume \
  --volume-id  \
  --instance-id  \
  --device  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --volume-id (string): [Required] The ID of the EBS volume to attach
  • --instance-id (string): [Required] The ID of the instance to attach to
  • --device (string): [Required] The device name (/dev/sdf through /dev/sdp, or /dev/xvdf through /dev/xvdp)
  • --region (string): AWS region
Return Value
  • VolumeId (String): The ID of the volume
  • InstanceId (String): The ID of the instance
  • Device (String): The device name
  • State (String): Attachment state (attaching, attached)
  • AttachTime (Timestamp): Time of attachment
Example 1: Attach Media Volume to Django Instance

Attach EBS volume for Django media file storage.

#!/bin/bash
# Attach media volume to Django instance
VOLUME_ID="vol-0abc123django456"
INSTANCE_ID="i-django-prod-001"
DEVICE="/dev/sdf"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Attaching volume to Django instance..."
echo "  Volume: $VOLUME_ID"
echo "  Instance: $INSTANCE_ID"
echo "  Device: $DEVICE"

aws ec2 attach-volume \
  --volume-id $VOLUME_ID \
  --instance-id $INSTANCE_ID \
  --device $DEVICE \
  --region $REGION

# Wait for attachment
echo "Waiting for attachment to complete..."
aws ec2 wait volume-in-use --volume-ids $VOLUME_ID --region $REGION

echo "✓ Volume attached successfully"
echo ""
echo "Next steps on the instance:"
echo "  1. sudo lsblk                     # Verify device"
echo "  2. sudo mkfs -t xfs /dev/xvdf     # Format (if new)"
echo "  3. sudo mkdir /var/www/media      # Create mount point"
echo "  4. sudo mount /dev/xvdf /var/www/media"
Attaching volume to Django instance... Volume: vol-0abc123django456 Instance: i-django-prod-001 Device: /dev/sdf { "VolumeId": "vol-0abc123django456", "InstanceId": "i-django-prod-001", "Device": "/dev/sdf", "State": "attaching", "AttachTime": "2026-01-13T14:30:00.000Z" } Waiting for attachment to complete... ✓ Volume attached successfully Next steps on the instance: 1. sudo lsblk # Verify device 2. sudo mkfs -t xfs /dev/xvdf # Format (if new) 3. sudo mkdir /var/www/media # Create mount point 4. sudo mount /dev/xvdf /var/www/media
# Attach media volume to Django instance from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone attachment = { 'volume_id': 'vol-0abc123django456', 'instance_id': 'i-django-prod-001', 'device': '/dev/sdf', 'state': 'attached', 'attach_time': timezone.now() } print(f"Volume Attachment") print(f" Volume: {attachment['volume_id']}") print(f" Instance: {attachment['instance_id']}") print(f" Device: {attachment['device']}") print(f" State: {attachment['state']}") print(f"\n✓ Media storage connected to Django")
Example 2: Attach Database Volume with Verification

Attach PostgreSQL database volume with pre and post verification.

#!/bin/bash
# Attach database volume with verification
VOLUME_ID="vol-0db123postgres456"
INSTANCE_ID="i-django-db-001"
DEVICE="/dev/sdg"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "=== Database Volume Attachment ==="

# Verify volume is available
STATE=$(aws ec2 describe-volumes \
  --volume-ids $VOLUME_ID \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'Volumes[0].State' \
  --output text)

if [ "$STATE" != "available" ]; then
    echo "✗ Volume is $STATE, must be 'available'"
    exit 1
fi

echo "✓ Volume is available"

# Verify AZ match
VOL_AZ=$(aws ec2 describe-volumes \
  --volume-ids $VOLUME_ID \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'Volumes[0].AvailabilityZone' \
  --output text)

INST_AZ=$(aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'Reservations[0].Instances[0].Placement.AvailabilityZone' \
  --output text)

if [ "$VOL_AZ" != "$INST_AZ" ]; then
    echo "✗ AZ mismatch: Volume in $VOL_AZ, Instance in $INST_AZ"
    exit 1
fi

echo "✓ AZ match confirmed: $VOL_AZ"

# Perform attachment
aws ec2 attach-volume \
  --volume-id $VOLUME_ID \
  --instance-id $INSTANCE_ID \
  --device $DEVICE \
  --region $REGION

aws ec2 wait volume-in-use --volume-ids $VOLUME_ID --region $REGION

echo "✓ Database volume attached to $DEVICE"
=== Database Volume Attachment === ✓ Volume is available ✓ AZ match confirmed: us-east-1a ✓ Database volume attached to /dev/sdg
# Attach database volume with verification from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost db_attachment = { 'volume_id': 'vol-0db123postgres456', 'instance_id': 'i-django-db-001', 'volume_az': 'us-east-1a', 'instance_az': 'us-east-1a', 'az_match': True, 'state': 'attached' } print(f"Database Volume Attachment") print(f" Volume: {db_attachment['volume_id']}") print(f" Instance: {db_attachment['instance_id']}") print(f" AZ Match: ✓") print(f" State: {db_attachment['state']}") print(f"\n✓ PostgreSQL storage ready")
Example 3: Attach Volume and Configure Mount

Complete workflow: attach volume, format, mount, and add to fstab.

#!/bin/bash
# Complete volume attachment and mount workflow
VOLUME_ID="vol-0media123456"
INSTANCE_ID="i-django-prod-001"
DEVICE="/dev/sdf"
MOUNT_POINT="/var/www/media"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Complete volume setup for Django media..."

# Step 1: Attach volume
echo "Step 1: Attaching volume..."
aws ec2 attach-volume \
  --volume-id $VOLUME_ID \
  --instance-id $INSTANCE_ID \
  --device $DEVICE \
  --region $REGION

aws ec2 wait volume-in-use --volume-ids $VOLUME_ID --region $REGION
echo "✓ Volume attached"

# Step 2: SSH and configure (commands for reference)
echo ""
echo "Step 2: Run on instance via SSH:"
echo "---"
cat << 'EOF'
# Check if new volume (no filesystem)
sudo file -s /dev/xvdf

# Format if new (ONLY for new volumes!)
sudo mkfs -t xfs /dev/xvdf

# Create mount point
sudo mkdir -p /var/www/media

# Mount the volume
sudo mount /dev/xvdf /var/www/media

# Add to fstab for persistence
UUID=$(sudo blkid -s UUID -o value /dev/xvdf)
echo "UUID=$UUID /var/www/media xfs defaults,nofail 0 2" | sudo tee -a /etc/fstab

# Set permissions for Django
sudo chown www-data:www-data /var/www/media
sudo chmod 755 /var/www/media

# Verify
df -h /var/www/media
EOF
Complete volume setup for Django media... Step 1: Attaching volume... ✓ Volume attached Step 2: Run on instance via SSH: --- # Check if new volume (no filesystem) sudo file -s /dev/xvdf # Format if new (ONLY for new volumes!) sudo mkfs -t xfs /dev/xvdf # Create mount point sudo mkdir -p /var/www/media # Mount the volume sudo mount /dev/xvdf /var/www/media # Add to fstab for persistence UUID=$(sudo blkid -s UUID -o value /dev/xvdf) echo "UUID=$UUID /var/www/media xfs defaults,nofail 0 2" | sudo tee -a /etc/fstab # Set permissions for Django sudo chown www-data:www-data /var/www/media sudo chmod 755 /var/www/media # Verify df -h /var/www/media
# Complete volume attachment workflow from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost mount_config = { 'volume_id': 'vol-0media123456', 'device': '/dev/xvdf', 'mount_point': '/var/www/media', 'filesystem': 'xfs', 'owner': 'www-data', 'permissions': '755', 'fstab_entry': True } print(f"Volume Mount Configuration") print(f" Device: {mount_config['device']}") print(f" Mount: {mount_config['mount_point']}") print(f" FS: {mount_config['filesystem']}") print(f" Owner: {mount_config['owner']}") print(f" fstab: ✓") print(f"\n✓ Django media storage configured")
Example 4: Attach Multiple Volumes to Instance

Attach multiple volumes for different Django data types.

#!/bin/bash
# Attach multiple volumes to Django instance
INSTANCE_ID="i-django-prod-001"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Attaching multiple volumes to Django instance..."

# Define volumes and devices
declare -A VOLUMES
VOLUMES["vol-0app123"]="/dev/sdf:app"
VOLUMES["vol-0media456"]="/dev/sdg:media"
VOLUMES["vol-0logs789"]="/dev/sdh:logs"

for VOL_ID in "${!VOLUMES[@]}"; do
    IFS=':' read -r DEVICE PURPOSE <<< "${VOLUMES[$VOL_ID]}"
    
    echo "Attaching $PURPOSE volume ($VOL_ID) to $DEVICE..."
    
    aws ec2 attach-volume \
      --volume-id $VOL_ID \
      --instance-id $INSTANCE_ID \
      --device $DEVICE \
      --region $REGION >/dev/null
    
    echo "✓ $PURPOSE volume attached"
done

# Wait for all volumes
echo ""
echo "Waiting for all volumes..."
aws ec2 wait volume-in-use \
  --volume-ids vol-0app123 vol-0media456 vol-0logs789 \
  --region $REGION

echo "✓ All volumes attached and ready"
Attaching multiple volumes to Django instance... Attaching app volume (vol-0app123) to /dev/sdf... ✓ app volume attached Attaching media volume (vol-0media456) to /dev/sdg... ✓ media volume attached Attaching logs volume (vol-0logs789) to /dev/sdh... ✓ logs volume attached Waiting for all volumes... ✓ All volumes attached and ready
# Attach multiple volumes from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost attachments = [ {'volume': 'vol-0app123', 'device': '/dev/sdf', 'purpose': 'app'}, {'volume': 'vol-0media456', 'device': '/dev/sdg', 'purpose': 'media'}, {'volume': 'vol-0logs789', 'device': '/dev/sdh', 'purpose': 'logs'} ] print(f"Multi-Volume Attachment") for att in attachments: print(f" ✓ {att['purpose']}: {att['volume']} → {att['device']}") print(f"\nTotal: {len(attachments)} volumes attached") print(f"✓ Django instance storage ready")
Example 5: Attach Restored Volume (Disaster Recovery)

Attach a volume restored from snapshot for disaster recovery.

#!/bin/bash
# Attach restored volume for DR
RESTORED_VOLUME="vol-0restored789"
INSTANCE_ID="i-django-dr-001"
DEVICE="/dev/sdf"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "=== Disaster Recovery: Attach Restored Volume ==="

# Verify volume is from snapshot
SNAPSHOT=$(aws ec2 describe-volumes \
  --volume-ids $RESTORED_VOLUME \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'Volumes[0].SnapshotId' \
  --output text)

echo "Restored from snapshot: $SNAPSHOT"

# Attach to DR instance
aws ec2 attach-volume \
  --volume-id $RESTORED_VOLUME \
  --instance-id $INSTANCE_ID \
  --device $DEVICE \
  --region $REGION

aws ec2 wait volume-in-use --volume-ids $RESTORED_VOLUME --region $REGION

echo "✓ Restored volume attached"
echo "✓ Data recovery ready - mount and verify"
=== Disaster Recovery: Attach Restored Volume === Restored from snapshot: snap-0backup123456789 { "VolumeId": "vol-0restored789", "InstanceId": "i-django-dr-001", "Device": "/dev/sdf", "State": "attaching" } ✓ Restored volume attached ✓ Data recovery ready - mount and verify
# Attach restored volume for DR from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost dr_recovery = { 'restored_volume': 'vol-0restored789', 'source_snapshot': 'snap-0backup123456789', 'dr_instance': 'i-django-dr-001', 'device': '/dev/sdf', 'state': 'attached' } print(f"Disaster Recovery Volume") print(f" Snapshot: {dr_recovery['source_snapshot']}") print(f" Volume: {dr_recovery['restored_volume']}") print(f" DR Instance: {dr_recovery['dr_instance']}") print(f"\n✓ Data recovery volume ready")

describe-volumes

List and describe EBS volumes in your account. Returns volume IDs, sizes, types, states, attachment information, and encryption status. Essential for inventory management, monitoring volume usage, and identifying unattached volumes for cost optimization.

Syntax
aws ec2 describe-volumes \
  [--volume-ids ] \
  [--filters Name=,Values=] \
  [--query ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --volume-ids (list): Specific volume IDs to describe
  • --filters (list): Filter volumes. Filters: attachment.instance-id, attachment.status, availability-zone, size, status, volume-id, volume-type, tag:key=value
  • --query (string): JMESPath query to filter output
  • --region (string): AWS region to query
Return Value
  • Volumes (Array): List of EBS volume objects
  • VolumeId (String): The volume ID
  • Size (Integer): Size in GiB
  • VolumeType (String): Volume type (gp2, gp3, io1, io2, st1, sc1)
  • State (String): Volume state (creating, available, in-use, deleting)
  • Attachments (Array): Attachment information including InstanceId and Device
  • Encrypted (Boolean): Whether the volume is encrypted
Example 1: List All Volumes with Status

List all EBS volumes showing their size, type, and attachment status.

#!/bin/bash
# List all EBS volumes
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "EBS Volume Inventory"
echo "===================="

aws ec2 describe-volumes \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'Volumes[*].[VolumeId,Size,VolumeType,State,Attachments[0].InstanceId,Tags[?Key==`Name`].Value|[0]]' \
  --output table
EBS Volume Inventory ==================== ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | DescribeVolumes | +-----------------------+------+--------+---------+--------------------+------------------+ | vol-0abc123django456 | 100 | gp3 | in-use | i-django-prod-001 | anihpj-media | | vol-0db123postgres456 | 500 | io2 | in-use | i-django-db-001 | anihpj-postgres | | vol-0logs123archive | 1000 | sc1 | in-use | i-django-prod-001 | anihpj-logs | | vol-0unused789 | 50 | gp2 | available| None | anihpj-old-test | +-----------------------+------+--------+---------+--------------------+------------------+
# List all EBS volumes from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost volumes = [ {'id': 'vol-0abc123django456', 'size': 100, 'type': 'gp3', 'state': 'in-use', 'instance': 'i-django-prod-001'}, {'id': 'vol-0db123postgres456', 'size': 500, 'type': 'io2', 'state': 'in-use', 'instance': 'i-django-db-001'}, {'id': 'vol-0logs123archive', 'size': 1000, 'type': 'sc1', 'state': 'in-use', 'instance': 'i-django-prod-001'}, {'id': 'vol-0unused789', 'size': 50, 'type': 'gp2', 'state': 'available', 'instance': None} ] print(f"EBS Volume Inventory: {len(volumes)} volumes") print(f"=" * 50) total_size = sum(v['size'] for v in volumes) attached = sum(1 for v in volumes if v['state'] == 'in-use') for vol in volumes: status = "✓" if vol['state'] == 'in-use' else "○" print(f" {status} {vol['id']} ({vol['size']} GiB {vol['type']})") print(f"\nTotal: {total_size} GiB | Attached: {attached}/{len(volumes)}")
Example 2: Find Unattached Volumes (Cost Waste)

Identify volumes not attached to any instance to avoid unnecessary costs.

#!/bin/bash
# Find unattached volumes
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Finding unattached EBS volumes (cost waste)..."
echo ""

UNATTACHED=$(aws ec2 describe-volumes \
  --filters "Name=status,Values=available" \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'Volumes[*].[VolumeId,Size,VolumeType,Tags[?Key==`Name`].Value|[0]]' \
  --output text)

if [ -z "$UNATTACHED" ]; then
    echo "✓ No unattached volumes found"
else
    echo "⚠️  Unattached volumes (still incurring costs):"
    echo "$UNATTACHED"
    echo ""
    
    # Calculate cost
    TOTAL_SIZE=$(aws ec2 describe-volumes \
      --filters "Name=status,Values=available" \
      --region $REGION \
      --query 'sum(Volumes[*].Size)' \
      --output text)
    
    MONTHLY=$(echo "$TOTAL_SIZE * 0.08" | bc)
    echo "Total unattached: $TOTAL_SIZE GiB"
    echo "Estimated monthly cost: \$$MONTHLY (gp3 rate)"
fi
Finding unattached EBS volumes (cost waste)... ⚠️ Unattached volumes (still incurring costs): vol-0unused789 50 gp2 anihpj-old-test vol-0orphan123 100 gp3 anihpj-staging-old Total unattached: 150 GiB Estimated monthly cost: $12.00 (gp3 rate)
# Find unattached volumes from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost unattached = [ {'id': 'vol-0unused789', 'size': 50, 'type': 'gp2', 'name': 'anihpj-old-test'}, {'id': 'vol-0orphan123', 'size': 100, 'type': 'gp3', 'name': 'anihpj-staging-old'} ] total_size = sum(v['size'] for v in unattached) monthly_cost = total_size * 0.08 print(f"⚠️ Unattached Volumes (Wasted Cost)") print(f"=" * 40) for vol in unattached: print(f" {vol['id']} - {vol['size']} GiB ({vol['name']})") print(f"\nTotal Size: {total_size} GiB") print(f"Monthly Waste: ${monthly_cost:.2f}") print(f"\nAction: Delete or attach these volumes")
Example 3: Get Volumes for Specific Instance

List all volumes attached to a specific Django instance.

#!/bin/bash
# Get volumes for specific instance
INSTANCE_ID="i-django-prod-001"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Volumes attached to: $INSTANCE_ID"
echo ""

aws ec2 describe-volumes \
  --filters "Name=attachment.instance-id,Values=$INSTANCE_ID" \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'Volumes[*].{
    VolumeId: VolumeId,
    Size: Size,
    Type: VolumeType,
    Device: Attachments[0].Device,
    Name: Tags[?Key==`Name`].Value|[0]
  }' \
  --output table
Volumes attached to: i-django-prod-001 ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | DescribeVolumes | +-----------------------+----------+--------+------+-----------------------------+ | VolumeId | Device | Size | Type | Name | +-----------------------+----------+--------+------+-----------------------------+ | vol-0root123 | /dev/xvda| 50 | gp3 | anihpj-prod-root | | vol-0abc123django456 | /dev/sdf | 100 | gp3 | anihpj-media | | vol-0logs123archive | /dev/sdg | 1000 | sc1 | anihpj-logs | +-----------------------+----------+--------+------+-----------------------------+
# Get volumes for specific instance from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost instance_volumes = [ {'id': 'vol-0root123', 'device': '/dev/xvda', 'size': 50, 'type': 'gp3', 'name': 'anihpj-prod-root'}, {'id': 'vol-0abc123django456', 'device': '/dev/sdf', 'size': 100, 'type': 'gp3', 'name': 'anihpj-media'}, {'id': 'vol-0logs123archive', 'device': '/dev/sdg', 'size': 1000, 'type': 'sc1', 'name': 'anihpj-logs'} ] print(f"Instance: i-django-prod-001") print(f"Attached Volumes: {len(instance_volumes)}") print(f"-" * 40) total = sum(v['size'] for v in instance_volumes) for vol in instance_volumes: print(f" {vol['device']} → {vol['id']} ({vol['size']} GiB)") print(f"\nTotal Storage: {total} GiB")
Example 4: Filter Volumes by Type and Size

Find high-performance io2 volumes above a certain size for audit.

#!/bin/bash
# Find io2 volumes (expensive) over 100 GiB
REGION="us-east-1"
MIN_SIZE=100

echo "High-performance io2 volumes >= ${MIN_SIZE} GiB"
echo ""

aws ec2 describe-volumes \
  --filters "Name=volume-type,Values=io2" \
  --region $REGION \
  --query "Volumes[?Size>=\`$MIN_SIZE\`].[VolumeId,Size,Iops,Attachments[0].InstanceId,Tags[?Key==\`Name\`].Value|[0]]" \
  --output table
High-performance io2 volumes >= 100 GiB ------------------------------------------------------------------------ | DescribeVolumes | +-----------------------+------+-------+------------------+--------------+ | vol-0db123postgres456 | 500 | 20000 | i-django-db-001 | anihpj-postgres | | vol-0db456replica | 500 | 15000 | i-django-db-002 | anihpj-replica | +-----------------------+------+-------+------------------+--------------+
# Filter volumes by type and size from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost io2_volumes = [ {'id': 'vol-0db123postgres456', 'size': 500, 'iops': 20000, 'instance': 'i-django-db-001'}, {'id': 'vol-0db456replica', 'size': 500, 'iops': 15000, 'instance': 'i-django-db-002'} ] print(f"High-Performance io2 Volumes (≥100 GiB)") for vol in io2_volumes: cost = (vol['size'] * 0.125) + (vol['iops'] * 0.065) print(f" {vol['id']}: {vol['size']} GiB @ {vol['iops']} IOPS") print(f" Monthly: ~${cost:.2f}") print(f"\n✓ High-cost storage audit complete")
Example 5: Generate Volume Report for Django Infrastructure

Generate comprehensive volume report with cost estimates.

#!/bin/bash
# Generate volume cost report
REGION="us-east-1"
REPORT_FILE="volume_report.json"

echo "Generating EBS volume cost report..."

aws ec2 describe-volumes \
  --region $REGION \
  --output json | jq '{
    report_date: now | strftime("%Y-%m-%d"),
    total_volumes: (.Volumes | length),
    total_size_gib: (.Volumes | map(.Size) | add),
    attached_count: ([.Volumes[] | select(.State=="in-use")] | length),
    unattached_count: ([.Volumes[] | select(.State=="available")] | length),
    by_type: (.Volumes | group_by(.VolumeType) | map({
      type: .[0].VolumeType,
      count: length,
      total_size: (map(.Size) | add)
    })),
    volumes: [.Volumes[] | {
      id: .VolumeId,
      name: (.Tags // [] | map(select(.Key=="Name")) | .[0].Value // "unnamed"),
      size: .Size,
      type: .VolumeType,
      state: .State,
      encrypted: .Encrypted
    }]
  }' > $REPORT_FILE

cat $REPORT_FILE | jq '.total_volumes, .total_size_gib, .attached_count, .unattached_count'
echo ""
echo "✓ Full report saved to $REPORT_FILE"
Generating EBS volume cost report... 6 1700 5 1 ✓ Full report saved to volume_report.json
# Generate volume cost report from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone report = { 'report_date': timezone.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d'), 'total_volumes': 6, 'total_size_gib': 1700, 'attached_count': 5, 'unattached_count': 1, 'by_type': { 'gp3': {'count': 3, 'size': 200}, 'io2': {'count': 2, 'size': 1000}, 'sc1': {'count': 1, 'size': 500} } } print(f"EBS Volume Report - {report['report_date']}") print(f"=" * 40) print(f"Total Volumes: {report['total_volumes']}") print(f"Total Storage: {report['total_size_gib']} GiB") print(f"Attached: {report['attached_count']}") print(f"Unattached: {report['unattached_count']}") print(f"\n✓ Report generated")

create-snapshot

Create a point-in-time snapshot of an EBS volume for backup and disaster recovery. Snapshots are stored in S3 and can be used to create new volumes in any AZ. Essential for Django data protection, migration, and creating AMIs.

Syntax
aws ec2 create-snapshot \
  --volume-id  \
  [--description ] \
  [--tag-specifications ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --volume-id (string): [Required] The ID of the EBS volume to snapshot
  • --description (string): Description of the snapshot
  • --tag-specifications (list): Tags to apply to the snapshot
  • --region (string): AWS region
Return Value
  • SnapshotId (String): The ID of the created snapshot
  • VolumeId (String): The source volume ID
  • VolumeSize (Integer): Size in GiB
  • State (String): Snapshot state (pending, completed, error)
  • StartTime (Timestamp): When the snapshot started
  • Progress (String): Completion percentage
Example 1: Create Backup Snapshot of Django Media Volume

Create a snapshot of the Django media volume for backup.

#!/bin/bash
# Create backup snapshot of Django media
VOLUME_ID="vol-0abc123django456"
REGION="us-east-1"
DATE=$(date +%Y-%m-%d)

echo "Creating snapshot of Django media volume..."

SNAPSHOT_ID=$(aws ec2 create-snapshot \
  --volume-id $VOLUME_ID \
  --description "anihpj Django media backup - $DATE" \
  --tag-specifications "ResourceType=snapshot,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=anihpj-media-backup-$DATE},{Key=Environment,Value=Production},{Key=Application,Value=anihpj},{Key=BackupType,Value=Daily}]" \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'SnapshotId' \
  --output text)

echo "✓ Snapshot created: $SNAPSHOT_ID"
echo "  Volume: $VOLUME_ID"
echo "  Date: $DATE"
echo ""
echo "Snapshot is being created in the background..."
echo "Check status: aws ec2 describe-snapshots --snapshot-ids $SNAPSHOT_ID"
Creating snapshot of Django media volume... ✓ Snapshot created: snap-0backup123456789 Volume: vol-0abc123django456 Date: 2026-01-13 Snapshot is being created in the background... Check status: aws ec2 describe-snapshots --snapshot-ids snap-0backup123456789
# Create backup snapshot from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone snapshot = { 'snapshot_id': 'snap-0backup123456789', 'volume_id': 'vol-0abc123django456', 'description': 'anihpj Django media backup - 2026-01-13', 'state': 'pending', 'created': timezone.now() } print(f"Snapshot Created") print(f" ID: {snapshot['snapshot_id']}") print(f" Volume: {snapshot['volume_id']}") print(f" State: {snapshot['state']}") print(f"\n✓ Backup initiated")
Example 2: Create Database Snapshot with Wait

Create a snapshot of PostgreSQL database volume and wait for completion.

#!/bin/bash
# Create database snapshot and wait
VOLUME_ID="vol-0db123postgres456"
REGION="us-east-1"
DATE=$(date +%Y-%m-%d-%H%M)

echo "Creating PostgreSQL database snapshot..."
echo "⚠️  For consistency, consider stopping writes during snapshot"

SNAPSHOT_ID=$(aws ec2 create-snapshot \
  --volume-id $VOLUME_ID \
  --description "anihpj PostgreSQL backup - $DATE" \
  --tag-specifications "ResourceType=snapshot,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=anihpj-postgres-$DATE},{Key=Environment,Value=Production},{Key=Database,Value=PostgreSQL},{Key=BackupType,Value=Manual}]" \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'SnapshotId' \
  --output text)

echo "Snapshot: $SNAPSHOT_ID"
echo "Waiting for completion..."

aws ec2 wait snapshot-completed --snapshot-ids $SNAPSHOT_ID --region $REGION

echo "✓ Database snapshot completed"
echo "  Ready for disaster recovery or migration"
Creating PostgreSQL database snapshot... ⚠️ For consistency, consider stopping writes during snapshot Snapshot: snap-0postgres123456 Waiting for completion... ✓ Database snapshot completed Ready for disaster recovery or migration
# Create database snapshot with wait from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone db_snapshot = { 'snapshot_id': 'snap-0postgres123456', 'volume_id': 'vol-0db123postgres456', 'database': 'PostgreSQL', 'state': 'completed', 'size_gib': 500, 'completed_at': timezone.now() } print(f"Database Snapshot Complete") print(f" Snapshot: {db_snapshot['snapshot_id']}") print(f" Size: {db_snapshot['size_gib']} GiB") print(f" Database: {db_snapshot['database']}") print(f"\n✓ Recovery point created")
Example 3: Snapshot All Volumes for Instance

Create snapshots of all volumes attached to a Django instance.

#!/bin/bash
# Snapshot all volumes for an instance
INSTANCE_ID="i-django-prod-001"
REGION="us-east-1"
DATE=$(date +%Y-%m-%d)

echo "Creating snapshots for all volumes of $INSTANCE_ID..."

# Get all volume IDs for the instance
VOLUMES=$(aws ec2 describe-volumes \
  --filters "Name=attachment.instance-id,Values=$INSTANCE_ID" \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'Volumes[*].[VolumeId,Tags[?Key==`Name`].Value|[0]]' \
  --output text)

echo "$VOLUMES" | while read VOL_ID VOL_NAME; do
    echo "Snapshotting: $VOL_NAME ($VOL_ID)"
    
    aws ec2 create-snapshot \
      --volume-id $VOL_ID \
      --description "Instance backup $INSTANCE_ID - $VOL_NAME - $DATE" \
      --tag-specifications "ResourceType=snapshot,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=backup-$VOL_NAME-$DATE},{Key=SourceInstance,Value=$INSTANCE_ID}]" \
      --region $REGION \
      --query 'SnapshotId' \
      --output text
done

echo ""
echo "✓ All volumes snapshot initiated"
Creating snapshots for all volumes of i-django-prod-001... Snapshotting: anihpj-prod-root (vol-0root123) snap-0root123456 Snapshotting: anihpj-media (vol-0abc123django456) snap-0media123456 Snapshotting: anihpj-logs (vol-0logs123archive) snap-0logs123456 ✓ All volumes snapshot initiated
# Snapshot all volumes for instance from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost instance_snapshots = [ {'snapshot': 'snap-0root123456', 'volume': 'vol-0root123', 'name': 'anihpj-prod-root'}, {'snapshot': 'snap-0media123456', 'volume': 'vol-0abc123django456', 'name': 'anihpj-media'}, {'snapshot': 'snap-0logs123456', 'volume': 'vol-0logs123archive', 'name': 'anihpj-logs'} ] print(f"Instance Backup: i-django-prod-001") print(f"Snapshots Created: {len(instance_snapshots)}") for snap in instance_snapshots: print(f" ✓ {snap['name']} → {snap['snapshot']}") print(f"\n✓ Full instance backup initiated")
Example 4: Pre-Deployment Backup

Create a backup snapshot before deploying Django updates.

#!/bin/bash
# Pre-deployment backup
VOLUME_ID="vol-0abc123django456"
REGION="us-east-1"
DEPLOY_VERSION="v2.5.0"
DATE=$(date +%Y-%m-%d-%H%M)

echo "=== Pre-Deployment Backup ==="
echo "Version: $DEPLOY_VERSION"

SNAPSHOT_ID=$(aws ec2 create-snapshot \
  --volume-id $VOLUME_ID \
  --description "Pre-deployment backup before $DEPLOY_VERSION - $DATE" \
  --tag-specifications "ResourceType=snapshot,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=pre-deploy-$DEPLOY_VERSION},{Key=DeployVersion,Value=$DEPLOY_VERSION},{Key=BackupType,Value=PreDeploy},{Key=Environment,Value=Production}]" \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'SnapshotId' \
  --output text)

echo "✓ Pre-deployment snapshot: $SNAPSHOT_ID"
echo ""
echo "Rollback command if needed:"
echo "  aws ec2 create-volume --snapshot-id $SNAPSHOT_ID --availability-zone us-east-1a"
=== Pre-Deployment Backup === Version: v2.5.0 ✓ Pre-deployment snapshot: snap-0predeploy123 Rollback command if needed: aws ec2 create-volume --snapshot-id snap-0predeploy123 --availability-zone us-east-1a
# Pre-deployment backup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost pre_deploy_backup = { 'snapshot_id': 'snap-0predeploy123', 'volume_id': 'vol-0abc123django456', 'deploy_version': 'v2.5.0', 'backup_type': 'PreDeploy', 'can_rollback': True } print(f"Pre-Deployment Backup") print(f" Version: {pre_deploy_backup['deploy_version']}") print(f" Snapshot: {pre_deploy_backup['snapshot_id']}") print(f" Rollback Ready: ✓") print(f"\n✓ Safe to proceed with deployment")
Example 5: Automated Daily Backup Script

Create automated daily backups with retention tagging.

#!/bin/bash
# Automated daily backup script
REGION="us-east-1"
RETENTION_DAYS=7
DATE=$(date +%Y-%m-%d)
DELETE_DATE=$(date -d "+$RETENTION_DAYS days" +%Y-%m-%d)

echo "=== Daily Backup - $DATE ==="

# Tag for volumes to backup
VOLUMES=$(aws ec2 describe-volumes \
  --filters "Name=tag:BackupEnabled,Values=true" \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'Volumes[*].VolumeId' \
  --output text)

for VOL_ID in $VOLUMES; do
    VOL_NAME=$(aws ec2 describe-volumes \
      --volume-ids $VOL_ID \
      --region $REGION \
      --query 'Volumes[0].Tags[?Key==`Name`].Value|[0]' \
      --output text)
    
    echo "Backing up: $VOL_NAME"
    
    aws ec2 create-snapshot \
      --volume-id $VOL_ID \
      --description "Daily backup - $VOL_NAME - $DATE" \
      --tag-specifications "ResourceType=snapshot,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=daily-$VOL_NAME-$DATE},{Key=BackupType,Value=Daily},{Key=DeleteAfter,Value=$DELETE_DATE},{Key=AutoBackup,Value=true}]" \
      --region $REGION \
      --output text
done

echo ""
echo "✓ Daily backups complete (retain for $RETENTION_DAYS days)"
=== Daily Backup - 2026-01-13 === Backing up: anihpj-media snap-0daily-media-20260113 Backing up: anihpj-postgres snap-0daily-db-20260113 ✓ Daily backups complete (retain for 7 days)
# Automated daily backup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone from datetime import timedelta backup_job = { 'date': timezone.now().date(), 'retention_days': 7, 'delete_after': timezone.now().date() + timedelta(days=7), 'volumes_backed_up': ['anihpj-media', 'anihpj-postgres'], 'snapshots_created': 2 } print(f"Daily Backup Job - {backup_job['date']}") print(f"Retention: {backup_job['retention_days']} days") print(f"Delete After: {backup_job['delete_after']}") print(f"Volumes: {len(backup_job['volumes_backed_up'])}") for vol in backup_job['volumes_backed_up']: print(f" ✓ {vol}") print(f"\n✓ Automated backup complete")

describe-snapshots

List and describe EBS snapshots in your account. Returns snapshot IDs, sizes, states, creation times, and source volumes. Essential for backup inventory management, identifying old snapshots for cleanup, and verifying backup status for Django infrastructure.

Syntax
aws ec2 describe-snapshots \
  [--snapshot-ids ] \
  [--owner-ids ] \
  [--filters Name=,Values=] \
  [--query ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --snapshot-ids (list): Specific snapshot IDs to describe
  • --owner-ids (list): AWS account IDs that own the snapshots (use 'self' for your own)
  • --filters (list): Filter snapshots. Filters: description, owner-id, progress, snapshot-id, start-time, status, volume-id, volume-size, tag:key=value
  • --query (string): JMESPath query to filter output
  • --region (string): AWS region to query
Return Value
  • Snapshots (Array): List of snapshot objects
  • SnapshotId (String): The snapshot ID
  • VolumeId (String): The source volume ID
  • VolumeSize (Integer): Size in GiB
  • State (String): Snapshot state (pending, completed, error)
  • StartTime (Timestamp): When the snapshot was initiated
  • Progress (String): Completion percentage
Example 1: List All Snapshots for Django Infrastructure

List all snapshots owned by your account with their status.

#!/bin/bash
# List all snapshots
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "EBS Snapshot Inventory"
echo "======================"

aws ec2 describe-snapshots \
  --owner-ids self \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'Snapshots[*].[SnapshotId,VolumeSize,State,StartTime,Tags[?Key==`Name`].Value|[0]]' \
  --output table
EBS Snapshot Inventory ====================== ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | DescribeSnapshots | +-----------------------+------+-----------+----------------------+----------------------+ | snap-0backup123456789 | 100 | completed | 2026-01-13T10:30:00Z | anihpj-media-backup | | snap-0postgres123456 | 500 | completed | 2026-01-13T08:00:00Z | anihpj-postgres-daily| | snap-0predeploy123 | 50 | completed | 2026-01-12T14:00:00Z | pre-deploy-v2.5.0 | | snap-0pending789 | 100 | pending | 2026-01-13T14:30:00Z | anihpj-media-new | +-----------------------+------+-----------+----------------------+----------------------+
# List all snapshots from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost snapshots = [ {'id': 'snap-0backup123456789', 'size': 100, 'state': 'completed', 'name': 'anihpj-media-backup'}, {'id': 'snap-0postgres123456', 'size': 500, 'state': 'completed', 'name': 'anihpj-postgres-daily'}, {'id': 'snap-0predeploy123', 'size': 50, 'state': 'completed', 'name': 'pre-deploy-v2.5.0'}, {'id': 'snap-0pending789', 'size': 100, 'state': 'pending', 'name': 'anihpj-media-new'} ] print(f"Snapshot Inventory: {len(snapshots)} snapshots") print(f"=" * 50) completed = sum(1 for s in snapshots if s['state'] == 'completed') total_size = sum(s['size'] for s in snapshots) for snap in snapshots: status = "✓" if snap['state'] == 'completed' else "⏳" print(f" {status} {snap['id']} ({snap['size']} GiB)") print(f"\nCompleted: {completed}/{len(snapshots)} | Total Size: {total_size} GiB")
Example 2: Check Snapshot Progress

Monitor the progress of pending snapshots.

#!/bin/bash
# Check snapshot progress
SNAPSHOT_ID="snap-0pending789"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Checking snapshot progress: $SNAPSHOT_ID"

while true; do
    RESULT=$(aws ec2 describe-snapshots \
      --snapshot-ids $SNAPSHOT_ID \
      --region $REGION \
      --query 'Snapshots[0].[State,Progress]' \
      --output text)
    
    STATE=$(echo $RESULT | awk '{print $1}')
    PROGRESS=$(echo $RESULT | awk '{print $2}')
    
    echo "State: $STATE | Progress: $PROGRESS"
    
    if [ "$STATE" == "completed" ]; then
        echo "✓ Snapshot completed!"
        break
    elif [ "$STATE" == "error" ]; then
        echo "✗ Snapshot failed!"
        break
    fi
    
    sleep 30
done
Checking snapshot progress: snap-0pending789 State: pending | Progress: 25% State: pending | Progress: 50% State: pending | Progress: 75% State: completed | Progress: 100% ✓ Snapshot completed!
# Check snapshot progress from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost import time snapshot_progress = [ {'state': 'pending', 'progress': '25%'}, {'state': 'pending', 'progress': '50%'}, {'state': 'pending', 'progress': '75%'}, {'state': 'completed', 'progress': '100%'} ] print(f"Snapshot Progress Monitor") for check in snapshot_progress: print(f" State: {check['state']} | Progress: {check['progress']}") print(f"\n✓ Snapshot completed successfully")
Example 3: Find Old Snapshots for Cleanup

Identify snapshots older than retention period for cost optimization.

#!/bin/bash
# Find old snapshots for cleanup
REGION="us-east-1"
RETENTION_DAYS=30
CUTOFF_DATE=$(date -d "-$RETENTION_DAYS days" +%Y-%m-%d)

echo "Finding snapshots older than $RETENTION_DAYS days..."
echo "Cutoff date: $CUTOFF_DATE"
echo ""

OLD_SNAPSHOTS=$(aws ec2 describe-snapshots \
  --owner-ids self \
  --region $REGION \
  --query "Snapshots[?StartTime<='${CUTOFF_DATE}'].[SnapshotId,VolumeSize,StartTime,Tags[?Key==\`Name\`].Value|[0]]" \
  --output text)

if [ -z "$OLD_SNAPSHOTS" ]; then
    echo "✓ No old snapshots found"
else
    echo "Old snapshots found:"
    echo "$OLD_SNAPSHOTS"
    
    # Calculate storage cost
    TOTAL_SIZE=$(aws ec2 describe-snapshots \
      --owner-ids self \
      --region $REGION \
      --query "sum(Snapshots[?StartTime<='${CUTOFF_DATE}'].VolumeSize)" \
      --output text)
    
    MONTHLY=$(echo "$TOTAL_SIZE * 0.05" | bc)
    echo ""
    echo "Total old snapshot storage: $TOTAL_SIZE GiB"
    echo "Monthly cost: \$$MONTHLY"
fi
Finding snapshots older than 30 days... Cutoff date: 2025-12-14 Old snapshots found: snap-0old123 100 2025-11-15 anihpj-old-backup snap-0old456 200 2025-10-20 anihpj-staging-old Total old snapshot storage: 300 GiB Monthly cost: $15.00
# Find old snapshots for cleanup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone from datetime import timedelta retention_days = 30 cutoff = timezone.now() - timedelta(days=retention_days) old_snapshots = [ {'id': 'snap-0old123', 'size': 100, 'created': '2025-11-15'}, {'id': 'snap-0old456', 'size': 200, 'created': '2025-10-20'} ] total_size = sum(s['size'] for s in old_snapshots) monthly_cost = total_size * 0.05 print(f"Old Snapshots (>{retention_days} days)") for snap in old_snapshots: print(f" {snap['id']} - {snap['size']} GiB ({snap['created']})") print(f"\nTotal: {total_size} GiB | Cost: ${monthly_cost}/month") print(f"Action: Delete or verify still needed")
Example 4: Get Snapshots for Specific Volume

List all snapshots for a specific Django volume.

#!/bin/bash
# Get snapshots for specific volume
VOLUME_ID="vol-0abc123django456"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Snapshots for volume: $VOLUME_ID"
echo ""

aws ec2 describe-snapshots \
  --owner-ids self \
  --filters "Name=volume-id,Values=$VOLUME_ID" \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'sort_by(Snapshots, &StartTime)[*].{
    ID: SnapshotId,
    Date: StartTime,
    Size: VolumeSize,
    State: State,
    Name: Tags[?Key==`Name`].Value|[0]
  }' \
  --output table
Snapshots for volume: vol-0abc123django456 ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | DescribeSnapshots | +-----------------------+----------------------+------+-----------+-----------+ | ID | Date | Size | State | Name | +-----------------------+----------------------+------+-----------+-----------+ | snap-0media20260110 | 2026-01-10T00:00:00Z | 100 | completed | daily-1-10| | snap-0media20260111 | 2026-01-11T00:00:00Z | 100 | completed | daily-1-11| | snap-0media20260112 | 2026-01-12T00:00:00Z | 100 | completed | daily-1-12| | snap-0media20260113 | 2026-01-13T00:00:00Z | 100 | completed | daily-1-13| +-----------------------+----------------------+------+-----------+-----------+
# Get snapshots for specific volume from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost volume_snapshots = [ {'id': 'snap-0media20260110', 'date': '2026-01-10', 'size': 100}, {'id': 'snap-0media20260111', 'date': '2026-01-11', 'size': 100}, {'id': 'snap-0media20260112', 'date': '2026-01-12', 'size': 100}, {'id': 'snap-0media20260113', 'date': '2026-01-13', 'size': 100} ] print(f"Volume: vol-0abc123django456") print(f"Snapshots: {len(volume_snapshots)}") print(f"-" * 40) for snap in volume_snapshots: print(f" {snap['date']}: {snap['id']}") print(f"\n✓ Volume backup history retrieved")
Example 5: Generate Snapshot Audit Report

Generate comprehensive snapshot report for audit and compliance.

#!/bin/bash
# Generate snapshot audit report
REGION="us-east-1"
REPORT_FILE="snapshot_audit.json"

echo "Generating snapshot audit report..."

aws ec2 describe-snapshots \
  --owner-ids self \
  --region $REGION \
  --output json | jq '{
    report_date: now | strftime("%Y-%m-%d"),
    total_snapshots: (.Snapshots | length),
    total_size_gib: (.Snapshots | map(.VolumeSize) | add),
    storage_cost_monthly: ((.Snapshots | map(.VolumeSize) | add) * 0.05),
    completed: ([.Snapshots[] | select(.State=="completed")] | length),
    pending: ([.Snapshots[] | select(.State=="pending")] | length),
    by_age: {
      last_7_days: ([.Snapshots[] | select(.StartTime > (now - 604800 | strftime("%Y-%m-%d")))] | length),
      last_30_days: ([.Snapshots[] | select(.StartTime > (now - 2592000 | strftime("%Y-%m-%d")))] | length),
      older: ([.Snapshots[] | select(.StartTime < (now - 2592000 | strftime("%Y-%m-%d")))] | length)
    },
    recent: [.Snapshots | sort_by(.StartTime) | reverse | .[:5][] | {
      id: .SnapshotId,
      size: .VolumeSize,
      date: .StartTime,
      name: (.Tags // [] | map(select(.Key=="Name")) | .[0].Value // "unnamed")
    }]
  }' > $REPORT_FILE

cat $REPORT_FILE | jq '{total_snapshots, total_size_gib, storage_cost_monthly}'
echo ""
echo "✓ Full report saved to $REPORT_FILE"
Generating snapshot audit report... { "total_snapshots": 15, "total_size_gib": 2500, "storage_cost_monthly": 125 } ✓ Full report saved to snapshot_audit.json
# Generate snapshot audit report from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone audit = { 'report_date': timezone.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d'), 'total_snapshots': 15, 'total_size_gib': 2500, 'storage_cost_monthly': 125.00, 'completed': 14, 'pending': 1, 'by_age': { 'last_7_days': 5, 'last_30_days': 10, 'older': 5 } } print(f"Snapshot Audit Report - {audit['report_date']}") print(f"=" * 40) print(f"Total Snapshots: {audit['total_snapshots']}") print(f"Total Size: {audit['total_size_gib']} GiB") print(f"Monthly Cost: ${audit['storage_cost_monthly']}") print(f"Completed: {audit['completed']} | Pending: {audit['pending']}") print(f"\n✓ Audit report generated")

modify-instance-attribute

Modify attributes of an EC2 instance. Use to change security groups, instance type, EBS optimization, termination protection, and more. Instance must be stopped for some modifications. Essential for Django server configuration changes without replacing instances.

Syntax
aws ec2 modify-instance-attribute \
  --instance-id  \
  [--instance-type ] \
  [--groups ] \
  [--source-dest-check | --no-source-dest-check] \
  [--disable-api-termination | --no-disable-api-termination] \
  [--ebs-optimized | --no-ebs-optimized] \
  [--user-data ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --instance-id (string): [Required] The ID of the instance to modify
  • --instance-type (string): The instance type (requires stopped instance)
  • --groups (list): Security group IDs (VPC instances only)
  • --source-dest-check (flag): Enable source/destination checking
  • --no-source-dest-check (flag): Disable for NAT instances
  • --disable-api-termination (flag): Enable termination protection
  • --ebs-optimized (flag): Enable EBS optimization
  • --user-data (blob): Base64-encoded user data (requires stopped instance)
Return Value
  • None: Successful modification returns no output (exit code 0)
  • Error: Returns error if instance state doesn't allow modification
Example 1: Change Security Groups on Django Instance

Update security groups attached to a running Django instance.

#!/bin/bash
# Change security groups on instance
INSTANCE_ID="i-django-prod-001"
REGION="us-east-1"

# New security groups: web + app + monitoring
SG_WEB="sg-web123456"
SG_APP="sg-app789012"
SG_MONITOR="sg-monitor345678"

echo "Updating security groups for: $INSTANCE_ID"

aws ec2 modify-instance-attribute \
  --instance-id $INSTANCE_ID \
  --groups $SG_WEB $SG_APP $SG_MONITOR \
  --region $REGION

echo "✓ Security groups updated"
echo "  Applied: $SG_WEB, $SG_APP, $SG_MONITOR"

# Verify
aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'Reservations[0].Instances[0].SecurityGroups[*].GroupId' \
  --output text
Updating security groups for: i-django-prod-001 ✓ Security groups updated Applied: sg-web123456, sg-app789012, sg-monitor345678 sg-web123456 sg-app789012 sg-monitor345678
# Change security groups from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost sg_update = { 'instance_id': 'i-django-prod-001', 'old_groups': ['sg-default123'], 'new_groups': ['sg-web123456', 'sg-app789012', 'sg-monitor345678'], 'success': True } print(f"Security Group Update") print(f" Instance: {sg_update['instance_id']}") print(f" Previous: {len(sg_update['old_groups'])} groups") print(f" New: {len(sg_update['new_groups'])} groups") for sg in sg_update['new_groups']: print(f" ✓ {sg}") print(f"\n✓ Security configuration updated")
Example 2: Enable Termination Protection

Protect production Django server from accidental termination.

#!/bin/bash
# Enable termination protection
INSTANCE_ID="i-django-prod-001"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Enabling termination protection for: $INSTANCE_ID"

aws ec2 modify-instance-attribute \
  --instance-id $INSTANCE_ID \
  --disable-api-termination \
  --region $REGION

echo "✓ Termination protection enabled"
echo "  Instance cannot be terminated via API/Console"
echo "  Must disable protection before termination"

# Verify
PROTECTED=$(aws ec2 describe-instance-attribute \
  --instance-id $INSTANCE_ID \
  --attribute disableApiTermination \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'DisableApiTermination.Value' \
  --output text)

echo "  Protected: $PROTECTED"
Enabling termination protection for: i-django-prod-001 ✓ Termination protection enabled Instance cannot be terminated via API/Console Must disable protection before termination Protected: true
# Enable termination protection from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost protection = { 'instance_id': 'i-django-prod-001', 'termination_protected': True, 'environment': 'Production' } print(f"Termination Protection") print(f" Instance: {protection['instance_id']}") print(f" Environment: {protection['environment']}") print(f" Protected: ✓") print(f"\n✓ Production server protected from accidental termination")
Example 3: Change Instance Type (Vertical Scaling)

Upgrade Django instance to larger type for increased traffic.

#!/bin/bash
# Change instance type (vertical scaling)
INSTANCE_ID="i-django-prod-001"
NEW_TYPE="m5.xlarge"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "=== Vertical Scaling: $INSTANCE_ID ==="
echo "New instance type: $NEW_TYPE"
echo ""

# Step 1: Stop the instance
echo "Step 1: Stopping instance..."
aws ec2 stop-instances --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID --region $REGION >/dev/null
aws ec2 wait instance-stopped --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID --region $REGION
echo "✓ Instance stopped"

# Step 2: Change instance type
echo "Step 2: Changing instance type..."
aws ec2 modify-instance-attribute \
  --instance-id $INSTANCE_ID \
  --instance-type "{\"Value\": \"$NEW_TYPE\"}" \
  --region $REGION
echo "✓ Instance type changed to $NEW_TYPE"

# Step 3: Start the instance
echo "Step 3: Starting instance..."
aws ec2 start-instances --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID --region $REGION >/dev/null
aws ec2 wait instance-running --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID --region $REGION
echo "✓ Instance running"

echo ""
echo "✓ Vertical scaling complete"
=== Vertical Scaling: i-django-prod-001 === New instance type: m5.xlarge Step 1: Stopping instance... ✓ Instance stopped Step 2: Changing instance type... ✓ Instance type changed to m5.xlarge Step 3: Starting instance... ✓ Instance running ✓ Vertical scaling complete
# Change instance type (vertical scaling) from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost scaling = { 'instance_id': 'i-django-prod-001', 'old_type': 't3.medium', 'new_type': 'm5.xlarge', 'steps': ['stop', 'modify', 'start'], 'completed': True } print(f"Vertical Scaling Complete") print(f" Instance: {scaling['instance_id']}") print(f" Old Type: {scaling['old_type']}") print(f" New Type: {scaling['new_type']}") print(f"\n✓ Django server upgraded for higher capacity")
Example 4: Disable Source/Dest Check for NAT Instance

Configure instance as NAT for private subnet traffic.

#!/bin/bash
# Disable source/dest check for NAT instance
INSTANCE_ID="i-nat-instance-001"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Configuring NAT instance: $INSTANCE_ID"
echo "Disabling source/destination check..."

aws ec2 modify-instance-attribute \
  --instance-id $INSTANCE_ID \
  --no-source-dest-check \
  --region $REGION

echo "✓ Source/destination check disabled"
echo "  Instance can now route traffic as NAT"

# Verify
CHECK=$(aws ec2 describe-instance-attribute \
  --instance-id $INSTANCE_ID \
  --attribute sourceDestCheck \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'SourceDestCheck.Value' \
  --output text)

echo "  Source/Dest Check: $CHECK"
Configuring NAT instance: i-nat-instance-001 Disabling source/destination check... ✓ Source/destination check disabled Instance can now route traffic as NAT Source/Dest Check: false
# Disable source/dest check for NAT from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost nat_config = { 'instance_id': 'i-nat-instance-001', 'source_dest_check': False, 'role': 'NAT Instance', 'purpose': 'Route private subnet traffic to internet' } print(f"NAT Instance Configuration") print(f" Instance: {nat_config['instance_id']}") print(f" Role: {nat_config['role']}") print(f" Source/Dest Check: Disabled") print(f"\n✓ Ready to route Django private subnet traffic")
Example 5: Enable EBS Optimization

Enable dedicated EBS bandwidth for improved storage performance.

#!/bin/bash
# Enable EBS optimization
INSTANCE_ID="i-django-db-001"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Enabling EBS optimization for: $INSTANCE_ID"

# Check current state
CURRENT=$(aws ec2 describe-instance-attribute \
  --instance-id $INSTANCE_ID \
  --attribute ebsOptimized \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'EbsOptimized.Value' \
  --output text)

echo "Current EBS Optimized: $CURRENT"

if [ "$CURRENT" == "true" ]; then
    echo "✓ Already EBS optimized"
    exit 0
fi

# Stop instance (required for this change)
echo "Stopping instance..."
aws ec2 stop-instances --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID --region $REGION >/dev/null
aws ec2 wait instance-stopped --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID --region $REGION

# Enable EBS optimization
aws ec2 modify-instance-attribute \
  --instance-id $INSTANCE_ID \
  --ebs-optimized \
  --region $REGION

# Start instance
echo "Starting instance..."
aws ec2 start-instances --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID --region $REGION >/dev/null
aws ec2 wait instance-running --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID --region $REGION

echo "✓ EBS optimization enabled"
echo "  Dedicated bandwidth for EBS I/O"
Enabling EBS optimization for: i-django-db-001 Current EBS Optimized: false Stopping instance... Starting instance... ✓ EBS optimization enabled Dedicated bandwidth for EBS I/O
# Enable EBS optimization from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost ebs_opt = { 'instance_id': 'i-django-db-001', 'ebs_optimized': True, 'benefit': 'Dedicated EBS bandwidth', 'use_case': 'Database server with io2 volumes' } print(f"EBS Optimization Enabled") print(f" Instance: {ebs_opt['instance_id']}") print(f" EBS Optimized: ✓") print(f" Benefit: {ebs_opt['benefit']}") print(f"\n✓ PostgreSQL database I/O performance improved")

describe-instance-status

Get status checks and scheduled events for EC2 instances. Returns system status (AWS infrastructure) and instance status (guest OS) checks. Essential for monitoring Django server health, detecting hardware issues, and identifying scheduled maintenance.

Syntax
aws ec2 describe-instance-status \
  [--instance-ids ] \
  [--filters Name=,Values=] \
  [--include-all-instances | --no-include-all-instances] \
  [--query ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --instance-ids (list): Specific instance IDs to describe
  • --filters (list): Filter results. Filters: availability-zone, event.code, event.description, instance-state-name, instance-status.reachability, instance-status.status, system-status.reachability, system-status.status
  • --include-all-instances (flag): Include stopped instances (default: only running)
  • --query (string): JMESPath query to filter output
  • --region (string): AWS region to query
Return Value
  • InstanceStatuses (Array): List of instance status objects
  • InstanceId (String): The instance ID
  • InstanceState (Object): Current state (running, stopped, etc.)
  • SystemStatus (Object): AWS infrastructure status checks
  • InstanceStatus (Object): Guest OS status checks
  • Events (Array): Scheduled events (maintenance, retirement)
Example 1: Check Django Instance Health Status

Get comprehensive health status of Django production server.

#!/bin/bash
# Check instance health status
INSTANCE_ID="i-django-prod-001"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Health Status: $INSTANCE_ID"
echo "============================="

aws ec2 describe-instance-status \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'InstanceStatuses[0].{
    InstanceId: InstanceId,
    State: InstanceState.Name,
    SystemStatus: SystemStatus.Status,
    InstanceStatus: InstanceStatus.Status,
    AZ: AvailabilityZone
  }' \
  --output table
Health Status: i-django-prod-001 ============================= ----------------------------------------------------------------- | DescribeInstanceStatus | +-------------------+------------+---------------+---------------+ | InstanceId | State | InstanceStatus| SystemStatus | +-------------------+------------+---------------+---------------+ | i-django-prod-001 | running | ok | ok | +-------------------+------------+---------------+---------------+
# Check instance health status from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost health = { 'instance_id': 'i-django-prod-001', 'state': 'running', 'system_status': 'ok', 'instance_status': 'ok', 'az': 'us-east-1a' } print(f"Django Instance Health") print(f" Instance: {health['instance_id']}") print(f" State: {health['state']}") print(f" System: {health['system_status']} ✓") print(f" Instance: {health['instance_status']} ✓") print(f"\n✓ All health checks passing")
Example 2: Find Instances with Failed Status Checks

Identify instances with impaired status for troubleshooting.

#!/bin/bash
# Find instances with failed checks
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Checking for instances with failed status..."

# Check for impaired system status
IMPAIRED=$(aws ec2 describe-instance-status \
  --filters "Name=system-status.status,Values=impaired" \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'InstanceStatuses[*].[InstanceId,SystemStatus.Status]' \
  --output text)

if [ -n "$IMPAIRED" ]; then
    echo "⚠️  Instances with impaired SYSTEM status:"
    echo "$IMPAIRED"
else
    echo "✓ No instances with impaired system status"
fi

# Check for impaired instance status
INSTANCE_IMPAIRED=$(aws ec2 describe-instance-status \
  --filters "Name=instance-status.status,Values=impaired" \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'InstanceStatuses[*].[InstanceId,InstanceStatus.Status]' \
  --output text)

if [ -n "$INSTANCE_IMPAIRED" ]; then
    echo "⚠️  Instances with impaired INSTANCE status:"
    echo "$INSTANCE_IMPAIRED"
else
    echo "✓ No instances with impaired instance status"
fi
Checking for instances with failed status... ✓ No instances with impaired system status ✓ No instances with impaired instance status
# Find instances with failed checks from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost status_check = { 'impaired_system': [], 'impaired_instance': [], 'all_healthy': True } if status_check['all_healthy']: print(f"Health Check Results") print(f" System Status: All OK ✓") print(f" Instance Status: All OK ✓") print(f"\n✓ All Django instances healthy") else: print(f"⚠️ Issues detected - investigate required")
Example 3: Check for Scheduled Maintenance Events

List any scheduled events affecting Django infrastructure.

#!/bin/bash
# Check for scheduled events
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Checking for scheduled maintenance events..."
echo ""

EVENTS=$(aws ec2 describe-instance-status \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'InstanceStatuses[?Events!=`null`].{
    InstanceId: InstanceId,
    EventCode: Events[0].Code,
    EventDesc: Events[0].Description,
    NotBefore: Events[0].NotBefore,
    NotAfter: Events[0].NotAfter
  }' \
  --output json)

if [ "$EVENTS" == "[]" ]; then
    echo "✓ No scheduled events"
else
    echo "⚠️  Scheduled events found:"
    echo "$EVENTS" | jq -r '.[] | "  \(.InstanceId): \(.EventCode) - \(.EventDesc) (Before: \(.NotBefore))"'
fi
Checking for scheduled maintenance events... ⚠️ Scheduled events found: i-django-staging: instance-reboot - Scheduled maintenance (Before: 2026-01-20T00:00:00Z)
