# Check for scheduled events from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost scheduled_events = [ { 'instance_id': 'i-django-staging', 'event_code': 'instance-reboot', 'description': 'Scheduled maintenance', 'not_before': '2026-01-20', 'not_after': '2026-01-21' } ] if scheduled_events: print(f"⚠️ Scheduled Events") for event in scheduled_events: print(f" {event['instance_id']}: {event['event_code']}") print(f" Scheduled: {event['not_before']}") else: print(f"✓ No scheduled events")
Example 4: Monitor All Django Instances

Get status of all running Django instances.

#!/bin/bash
# Monitor all Django instances
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Django Infrastructure Status"
echo "============================"

aws ec2 describe-instance-status \
  --filters "Name=tag:Application,Values=anihpj" \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'InstanceStatuses[*].[InstanceId,InstanceState.Name,SystemStatus.Status,InstanceStatus.Status]' \
  --output table

# Summary
TOTAL=$(aws ec2 describe-instance-status \
  --filters "Name=tag:Application,Values=anihpj" \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'length(InstanceStatuses)' \
  --output text)

HEALTHY=$(aws ec2 describe-instance-status \
  --filters "Name=tag:Application,Values=anihpj" "Name=system-status.status,Values=ok" "Name=instance-status.status,Values=ok" \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'length(InstanceStatuses)' \
  --output text)

echo ""
echo "Summary: $HEALTHY/$TOTAL instances healthy"
Django Infrastructure Status ============================ ---------------------------------------------------------- | DescribeInstanceStatus | +--------------------+---------+--------------+-----------+ | i-django-prod-001 | running | ok | ok | | i-django-prod-002 | running | ok | ok | | i-django-db-001 | running | ok | ok | | i-bastion-001 | running | ok | ok | +--------------------+---------+--------------+-----------+ Summary: 4/4 instances healthy
# Monitor all Django instances from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost django_instances = [ {'id': 'i-django-prod-001', 'state': 'running', 'system': 'ok', 'instance': 'ok'}, {'id': 'i-django-prod-002', 'state': 'running', 'system': 'ok', 'instance': 'ok'}, {'id': 'i-django-db-001', 'state': 'running', 'system': 'ok', 'instance': 'ok'}, {'id': 'i-bastion-001', 'state': 'running', 'system': 'ok', 'instance': 'ok'} ] healthy = sum(1 for i in django_instances if i['system'] == 'ok' and i['instance'] == 'ok') print(f"Django Infrastructure Status") print(f"=" * 40) for inst in django_instances: status = "✓" if inst['system'] == 'ok' and inst['instance'] == 'ok' else "⚠️" print(f" {status} {inst['id']}: {inst['state']}") print(f"\nHealthy: {healthy}/{len(django_instances)}")
Example 5: Health Check Script with Alerting

Automated health check with notification trigger.

#!/bin/bash
# Health check with alerting
REGION="us-east-1"
SNS_TOPIC="arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789:anihpj-alerts"

echo "Running health check..."

# Get all instance statuses
STATUSES=$(aws ec2 describe-instance-status \
  --filters "Name=tag:Application,Values=anihpj" \
  --region $REGION \
  --output json)

# Check for any issues
ISSUES=$(echo $STATUSES | jq '[.InstanceStatuses[] | select(.SystemStatus.Status != "ok" or .InstanceStatus.Status != "ok")]')
ISSUE_COUNT=$(echo $ISSUES | jq 'length')

if [ "$ISSUE_COUNT" -gt 0 ]; then
    echo "⚠️  $ISSUE_COUNT instance(s) with issues!"
    
    # Send SNS alert
    MESSAGE="Django Infrastructure Alert: $ISSUE_COUNT instance(s) failing health checks"
    aws sns publish \
      --topic-arn $SNS_TOPIC \
      --message "$MESSAGE" \
      --subject "⚠️ Django Health Check Failed" \
      --region $REGION
    
    echo "Alert sent to SNS"
    echo "$ISSUES" | jq -r '.[] | "  \(.InstanceId): System=\(.SystemStatus.Status), Instance=\(.InstanceStatus.Status)"'
else
    echo "✓ All instances healthy"
fi
Running health check... ✓ All instances healthy
# Health check with alerting from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost health_check = { 'total_instances': 4, 'healthy': 4, 'issues': 0, 'alert_sent': False } print(f"Health Check Results") print(f" Total: {health_check['total_instances']}") print(f" Healthy: {health_check['healthy']}") print(f" Issues: {health_check['issues']}") if health_check['issues'] == 0: print(f"\n✓ All Django instances healthy") else: print(f"\n⚠️ Alert sent to SNS")

create-image

Create an Amazon Machine Image (AMI) from a running or stopped EC2 instance. Captures the root volume and any attached EBS volumes. Essential for creating reusable Django server templates, disaster recovery, and auto-scaling launch templates.

Syntax
aws ec2 create-image \
  --instance-id  \
  --name  \
  [--description ] \
  [--no-reboot] \
  [--tag-specifications ] \
  [--block-device-mappings ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --instance-id (string): [Required] The ID of the instance to create an image from
  • --name (string): [Required] A unique name for the AMI
  • --description (string): Description of the AMI
  • --no-reboot (flag): Don't reboot instance before creating (may affect consistency)
  • --tag-specifications (list): Tags to apply to the AMI and snapshots
  • --block-device-mappings (list): Block device configuration for the AMI
Return Value
  • ImageId (String): The ID of the created AMI
Example 1: Create Django Golden AMI

Create a golden AMI from fully configured Django production server.

#!/bin/bash
# Create Django golden AMI
INSTANCE_ID="i-django-prod-001"
REGION="us-east-1"
DATE=$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M)
AMI_NAME="anihpj-django-golden-$DATE"

echo "Creating golden AMI from Django production server..."
echo "Instance: $INSTANCE_ID"

AMI_ID=$(aws ec2 create-image \
  --instance-id $INSTANCE_ID \
  --name "$AMI_NAME" \
  --description "anihpj Django production golden image - Nginx, Gunicorn, Python 3.11" \
  --tag-specifications "ResourceType=image,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=$AMI_NAME},{Key=Application,Value=anihpj},{Key=Type,Value=GoldenAMI},{Key=Environment,Value=Production}]" "ResourceType=snapshot,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=$AMI_NAME-snapshot},{Key=Application,Value=anihpj}]" \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'ImageId' \
  --output text)

echo "✓ AMI creation initiated: $AMI_ID"
echo "  Name: $AMI_NAME"
echo ""
echo "Check status: aws ec2 describe-images --image-ids $AMI_ID"
Creating golden AMI from Django production server... Instance: i-django-prod-001 ✓ AMI creation initiated: ami-0django123golden456 Name: anihpj-django-golden-20260113-1430 Check status: aws ec2 describe-images --image-ids ami-0django123golden456
# Create Django golden AMI from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone ami = { 'ami_id': 'ami-0django123golden456', 'name': 'anihpj-django-golden-20260113', 'source_instance': 'i-django-prod-001', 'type': 'GoldenAMI', 'created': timezone.now() } print(f"Golden AMI Created") print(f" AMI ID: {ami['ami_id']}") print(f" Name: {ami['name']}") print(f" Source: {ami['source_instance']}") print(f" Type: {ami['type']}") print(f"\n✓ Ready for auto-scaling launch template")
Example 2: Create AMI Without Reboot (Live)

Create AMI without rebooting for minimal downtime (use with caution).

#!/bin/bash
# Create AMI without reboot
INSTANCE_ID="i-django-prod-001"
REGION="us-east-1"
DATE=$(date +%Y%m%d)

echo "Creating AMI without reboot (live snapshot)..."
echo "⚠️  Warning: Filesystem consistency not guaranteed"

AMI_ID=$(aws ec2 create-image \
  --instance-id $INSTANCE_ID \
  --name "anihpj-django-live-$DATE" \
  --description "Live backup - no reboot" \
  --no-reboot \
  --tag-specifications "ResourceType=image,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=anihpj-live-$DATE},{Key=NoReboot,Value=true},{Key=ConsistencyWarning,Value=Check before production use}]" \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'ImageId' \
  --output text)

echo "✓ AMI created: $AMI_ID"
echo "  ⚠️  Created without reboot - verify before using in production"
Creating AMI without reboot (live snapshot)... ⚠️ Warning: Filesystem consistency not guaranteed ✓ AMI created: ami-0live123456789 ⚠️ Created without reboot - verify before using in production
# Create AMI without reboot from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost live_ami = { 'ami_id': 'ami-0live123456789', 'no_reboot': True, 'consistency_guaranteed': False, 'use_case': 'Quick backup during peak hours' } print(f"Live AMI Created") print(f" AMI: {live_ami['ami_id']}") print(f" No Reboot: ✓") print(f" Consistency: ⚠️ Not guaranteed") print(f"\n⚠️ Verify before production use")
Example 3: Create Pre-Deployment Backup AMI

Create AMI before deploying new Django version for rollback capability.

#!/bin/bash
# Pre-deployment backup AMI
INSTANCE_ID="i-django-prod-001"
DEPLOY_VERSION="v2.5.0"
REGION="us-east-1"
DATE=$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M)

echo "=== Pre-Deployment Backup ==="
echo "Creating AMI before deploying $DEPLOY_VERSION"

AMI_ID=$(aws ec2 create-image \
  --instance-id $INSTANCE_ID \
  --name "anihpj-pre-deploy-$DEPLOY_VERSION-$DATE" \
  --description "Backup before deploying $DEPLOY_VERSION" \
  --tag-specifications "ResourceType=image,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=anihpj-pre-deploy-$DEPLOY_VERSION},{Key=PreDeploy,Value=true},{Key=TargetVersion,Value=$DEPLOY_VERSION},{Key=RollbackTo,Value=$DEPLOY_VERSION}]" \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'ImageId' \
  --output text)

echo "✓ Pre-deployment AMI: $AMI_ID"
echo ""
echo "Rollback command if needed:"
echo "  aws ec2 run-instances --image-id $AMI_ID --instance-type t3.large ..."
=== Pre-Deployment Backup === Creating AMI before deploying v2.5.0 ✓ Pre-deployment AMI: ami-0predeploy-v250 Rollback command if needed: aws ec2 run-instances --image-id ami-0predeploy-v250 --instance-type t3.large ...
# Pre-deployment backup AMI from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost predeploy = { 'ami_id': 'ami-0predeploy-v250', 'target_version': 'v2.5.0', 'source_version': 'v2.4.0', 'can_rollback': True } print(f"Pre-Deployment Backup") print(f" AMI: {predeploy['ami_id']}") print(f" Current: {predeploy['source_version']}") print(f" Deploying: {predeploy['target_version']}") print(f" Rollback Ready: ✓") print(f"\n✓ Safe to proceed with deployment")
Example 4: Create AMI and Wait for Completion

Create AMI and wait until it's ready for use.

#!/bin/bash
# Create AMI and wait for completion
INSTANCE_ID="i-django-prod-001"
REGION="us-east-1"
DATE=$(date +%Y%m%d)

echo "Creating AMI and waiting for completion..."

AMI_ID=$(aws ec2 create-image \
  --instance-id $INSTANCE_ID \
  --name "anihpj-django-$DATE" \
  --description "Django server image" \
  --tag-specifications "ResourceType=image,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=anihpj-django-$DATE},{Key=Application,Value=anihpj}]" \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'ImageId' \
  --output text)

echo "AMI ID: $AMI_ID"
echo "Waiting for AMI to be available..."

aws ec2 wait image-available --image-ids $AMI_ID --region $REGION

echo "✓ AMI is now available and ready for use"

# Get AMI details
aws ec2 describe-images \
  --image-ids $AMI_ID \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'Images[0].{State: State, RootDeviceType: RootDeviceType, Architecture: Architecture}' \
  --output table
Creating AMI and waiting for completion... AMI ID: ami-0django20260113 Waiting for AMI to be available... ✓ AMI is now available and ready for use ------------------------------------------ | DescribeImages | +----------------+---------+--------------+ | Architecture | State |RootDeviceType| +----------------+---------+--------------+ | x86_64 | available| ebs | +----------------+---------+--------------+
# Create AMI and wait for completion from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost ami_ready = { 'ami_id': 'ami-0django20260113', 'state': 'available', 'architecture': 'x86_64', 'root_device': 'ebs' } print(f"AMI Ready") print(f" AMI: {ami_ready['ami_id']}") print(f" State: {ami_ready['state']}") print(f" Arch: {ami_ready['architecture']}") print(f"\n✓ Ready for launching new instances")
Example 5: Create AMI for Auto Scaling Launch Template

Create optimized AMI for use in auto-scaling launch templates.

#!/bin/bash
# Create AMI for auto-scaling
INSTANCE_ID="i-django-prod-001"
REGION="us-east-1"
VERSION=$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M)

echo "Creating AMI for Auto Scaling..."

AMI_ID=$(aws ec2 create-image \
  --instance-id $INSTANCE_ID \
  --name "anihpj-asg-$VERSION" \
  --description "Auto Scaling optimized Django AMI" \
  --tag-specifications "ResourceType=image,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=anihpj-asg-$VERSION},{Key=Purpose,Value=AutoScaling},{Key=Application,Value=anihpj},{Key=Version,Value=$VERSION}]" \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'ImageId' \
  --output text)

echo "✓ AMI created: $AMI_ID"

# Wait for AMI
aws ec2 wait image-available --image-ids $AMI_ID --region $REGION

# Update launch template (example)
echo ""
echo "Update launch template with:"
echo "  aws ec2 create-launch-template-version \\"
echo "    --launch-template-id lt-0xxxx \\"
echo "    --source-version 1 \\"
echo "    --launch-template-data '{\"ImageId\":\"$AMI_ID\"}'"
Creating AMI for Auto Scaling... ✓ AMI created: ami-0asg20260113 Update launch template with: aws ec2 create-launch-template-version \ --launch-template-id lt-0xxxx \ --source-version 1 \ --launch-template-data '{"ImageId":"ami-0asg20260113"}'
# Create AMI for auto-scaling from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost asg_ami = { 'ami_id': 'ami-0asg20260113', 'purpose': 'AutoScaling', 'launch_template': 'lt-0django-asg', 'ready': True } print(f"Auto Scaling AMI Created") print(f" AMI: {asg_ami['ami_id']}") print(f" Purpose: {asg_ami['purpose']}") print(f" Launch Template: {asg_ami['launch_template']}") print(f"\n✓ Update launch template to use new AMI")

describe-images

List and describe Amazon Machine Images (AMIs). Returns AMI IDs, names, states, creation dates, and configurations. Essential for finding the right AMI for Django deployments, managing custom AMI inventory, and tracking AMI versions for auto-scaling.

Syntax
aws ec2 describe-images \
  [--image-ids ] \
  [--owners ] \
  [--executable-users ] \
  [--filters Name=,Values=] \
  [--query ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --image-ids (list): Specific AMI IDs to describe
  • --owners (list): AMI owners ('self', 'amazon', or account IDs)
  • --executable-users (list): Users with explicit launch permissions
  • --filters (list): Filter AMIs. Filters: architecture, description, image-id, name, owner-id, platform, state, tag:key=value
  • --query (string): JMESPath query to filter output
  • --region (string): AWS region to query
Return Value
  • Images (Array): List of AMI objects
  • ImageId (String): The AMI ID
  • Name (String): The AMI name
  • State (String): AMI state (available, pending, failed)
  • CreationDate (String): When the AMI was created
  • Architecture (String): CPU architecture (x86_64, arm64)
  • RootDeviceType (String): Root device type (ebs, instance-store)
Example 1: List All Custom Django AMIs

List all custom AMIs created for Django deployments.

#!/bin/bash
# List all custom AMIs
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Custom Django AMI Inventory"
echo "============================"

aws ec2 describe-images \
  --owners self \
  --filters "Name=tag:Application,Values=anihpj" \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'sort_by(Images, &CreationDate)[*].[ImageId,Name,State,CreationDate]' \
  --output table
Custom Django AMI Inventory ============================ ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | DescribeImages | +--------------------------+------------------------------+-----------+-------------+ | ami-0django20260110 | anihpj-django-20260110 | available | 2026-01-10 | | ami-0django20260111 | anihpj-django-20260111 | available | 2026-01-11 | | ami-0django123golden456 | anihpj-django-golden-20260113| available | 2026-01-13 | | ami-0asg20260113 | anihpj-asg-20260113 | available | 2026-01-13 | +--------------------------+------------------------------+-----------+-------------+
# List all custom Django AMIs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost amis = [ {'id': 'ami-0django20260110', 'name': 'anihpj-django-20260110', 'state': 'available'}, {'id': 'ami-0django20260111', 'name': 'anihpj-django-20260111', 'state': 'available'}, {'id': 'ami-0django123golden456', 'name': 'anihpj-django-golden', 'state': 'available'}, {'id': 'ami-0asg20260113', 'name': 'anihpj-asg-20260113', 'state': 'available'} ] print(f"Django AMI Inventory: {len(amis)} AMIs") print(f"=" * 50) for ami in amis: print(f" ✓ {ami['id']}: {ami['name']}") print(f"\n✓ All AMIs available")
Example 2: Find Latest Amazon Linux 2023 AMI

Find the latest Amazon Linux 2023 AMI for launching new Django servers.

#!/bin/bash
# Find latest Amazon Linux 2023 AMI
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Finding latest Amazon Linux 2023 AMI..."

LATEST_AMI=$(aws ec2 describe-images \
  --owners amazon \
  --filters "Name=name,Values=al2023-ami-2023*-x86_64" \
            "Name=state,Values=available" \
            "Name=architecture,Values=x86_64" \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'sort_by(Images, &CreationDate)[-1].[ImageId,Name,CreationDate]' \
  --output text)

AMI_ID=$(echo $LATEST_AMI | awk '{print $1}')
AMI_NAME=$(echo $LATEST_AMI | awk '{print $2}')
CREATED=$(echo $LATEST_AMI | awk '{print $3}')

echo "✓ Latest Amazon Linux 2023 AMI:"
echo "  AMI ID: $AMI_ID"
echo "  Name: $AMI_NAME"
echo "  Created: $CREATED"
Finding latest Amazon Linux 2023 AMI... ✓ Latest Amazon Linux 2023 AMI: AMI ID: ami-0abcd1234efgh5678 Name: al2023-ami-2023.4.20260110.0-kernel-6.1-x86_64 Created: 2026-01-10T00:00:00.000Z
# Find latest Amazon Linux 2023 AMI from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost latest_ami = { 'ami_id': 'ami-0abcd1234efgh5678', 'name': 'al2023-ami-2023.4.20260110.0', 'os': 'Amazon Linux 2023', 'architecture': 'x86_64' } print(f"Latest Amazon Linux 2023") print(f" AMI: {latest_ami['ami_id']}") print(f" OS: {latest_ami['os']}") print(f" Arch: {latest_ami['architecture']}") print(f"\n✓ Use for new Django instances")
Example 3: Check AMI Status After Creation

Verify AMI creation completed successfully.

#!/bin/bash
# Check AMI status
AMI_ID="ami-0django123golden456"
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Checking AMI status: $AMI_ID"

aws ec2 describe-images \
  --image-ids $AMI_ID \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'Images[0].{
    ImageId: ImageId,
    Name: Name,
    State: State,
    CreationDate: CreationDate,
    Architecture: Architecture,
    RootDeviceType: RootDeviceType,
    BlockDevices: BlockDeviceMappings[*].Ebs.SnapshotId
  }' \
  --output yaml
Checking AMI status: ami-0django123golden456 ImageId: ami-0django123golden456 Name: anihpj-django-golden-20260113 State: available CreationDate: '2026-01-13T14:30:00.000Z' Architecture: x86_64 RootDeviceType: ebs BlockDevices: - snap-0root123456789
# Check AMI status from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost ami_status = { 'ami_id': 'ami-0django123golden456', 'name': 'anihpj-django-golden-20260113', 'state': 'available', 'architecture': 'x86_64', 'snapshots': ['snap-0root123456789'] } print(f"AMI Status Check") print(f" AMI: {ami_status['ami_id']}") print(f" State: {ami_status['state']} ✓") print(f" Arch: {ami_status['architecture']}") print(f"\n✓ AMI ready for use")
Example 4: Find Golden AMI by Tag

Find the latest golden AMI for production deployments.

#!/bin/bash
# Find golden AMI by tag
REGION="us-east-1"

echo "Finding latest golden AMI..."

GOLDEN_AMI=$(aws ec2 describe-images \
  --owners self \
  --filters "Name=tag:Type,Values=GoldenAMI" \
            "Name=tag:Application,Values=anihpj" \
            "Name=state,Values=available" \
  --region $REGION \
  --query 'sort_by(Images, &CreationDate)[-1].[ImageId,Name,CreationDate]' \
  --output text)

AMI_ID=$(echo $GOLDEN_AMI | awk '{print $1}')
AMI_NAME=$(echo $GOLDEN_AMI | awk '{print $2}')

echo "✓ Latest Golden AMI:"
echo "  AMI ID: $AMI_ID"
echo "  Name: $AMI_NAME"
echo ""
echo "Use for production deployments and auto-scaling"
Finding latest golden AMI... ✓ Latest Golden AMI: AMI ID: ami-0django123golden456 Name: anihpj-django-golden-20260113 Use for production deployments and auto-scaling
# Find golden AMI by tag from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost golden_ami = { 'ami_id': 'ami-0django123golden456', 'name': 'anihpj-django-golden-20260113', 'type': 'GoldenAMI', 'environment': 'Production' } print(f"Latest Golden AMI") print(f" AMI: {golden_ami['ami_id']}") print(f" Name: {golden_ami['name']}") print(f" Type: {golden_ami['type']}") print(f"\n✓ Ready for production deployments")
Example 5: Generate AMI Audit Report

Generate comprehensive AMI inventory report.

#!/bin/bash
# Generate AMI audit report
REGION="us-east-1"
REPORT_FILE="ami_audit.json"

echo "Generating AMI audit report..."

aws ec2 describe-images \
  --owners self \
  --region $REGION \
  --output json | jq '{
    report_date: now | strftime("%Y-%m-%d"),
    total_amis: (.Images | length),
    available: ([.Images[] | select(.State=="available")] | length),
    pending: ([.Images[] | select(.State=="pending")] | length),
    by_application: (.Images | group_by(.Tags[]? | select(.Key=="Application").Value // "untagged") | map({
      app: .[0].Tags[]? | select(.Key=="Application").Value // "untagged",
      count: length
    })),
    recent: [.Images | sort_by(.CreationDate) | reverse | .[:5][] | {
      id: .ImageId,
      name: .Name,
      created: .CreationDate,
      state: .State
    }]
  }' > $REPORT_FILE

echo "Report Summary:"
cat $REPORT_FILE | jq '{total_amis, available, pending}'
echo ""
echo "✓ Full report saved to $REPORT_FILE"
Generating AMI audit report... Report Summary: { "total_amis": 12, "available": 10, "pending": 2 } ✓ Full report saved to ami_audit.json
# Generate AMI audit report from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone audit = { 'report_date': timezone.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d'), 'total_amis': 12, 'available': 10, 'pending': 2, 'by_application': { 'anihpj': 8, 'anihpj-staging': 3, 'untagged': 1 } } print(f"AMI Audit Report - {audit['report_date']}") print(f"=" * 40) print(f"Total AMIs: {audit['total_amis']}") print(f"Available: {audit['available']}") print(f"Pending: {audit['pending']}") print(f"\nBy Application:") for app, count in audit['by_application'].items(): print(f" {app}: {count}") print(f"\n✓ Audit report complete")

AWS Lambda

Serverless compute for Django background tasks and event-driven processing

📋 Lambda Overview

AWS Lambda is a serverless compute service that runs code in response to events without provisioning servers. For the anihpj Django application, Lambda handles background tasks like image processing, email sending, scheduled jobs (cron), webhook handlers, and API endpoints via API Gateway. Integrates seamlessly with SQS, S3, DynamoDB, and other AWS services to process JobPost, Skills, User, Author, and Location data asynchronously.

Core Lambda Operations for Django Deployment

create-function - Deploy new Lambda function
update-function-code - Update function code
invoke - Execute function directly
list-functions - List all functions
get-function - Get function details
delete-function - Remove function
add-permission - Grant invoke permissions
create-event-source-mapping - Connect triggers

create-function

Create a new Lambda function from deployment package (ZIP) or container image. Define runtime, handler, memory, timeout, IAM role, and environment variables. Essential for deploying Django background tasks and serverless API endpoints.

Syntax
aws lambda create-function \
  --function-name  \
  --runtime  \
  --role  \
  --handler  \
  [--code S3Bucket=,S3Key= | --zip-file fileb://] \
  [--description ] \
  [--timeout ] \
  [--memory-size ] \
  [--environment Variables={=}] \
  [--vpc-config SubnetIds=,SecurityGroupIds=] \
  [--layers ] \
  [--tags ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string): [Required] Name of the function (1-64 chars)
  • --runtime (string): [Required] Runtime: python3.12, python3.11, nodejs20.x, etc.
  • --role (string): [Required] IAM execution role ARN
  • --handler (string): [Required] Handler: filename.function_name
  • --code (structure): Deployment package location (S3 or local ZIP)
  • --timeout (integer): Execution timeout in seconds (1-900, default 3)
  • --memory-size (integer): Memory in MB (128-10240, default 128)
  • --environment (structure): Environment variables
  • --vpc-config (structure): VPC configuration for private resources
  • --layers (list): Layer ARNs to include
Return Value
  • FunctionName (String): The function name
  • FunctionArn (String): The function ARN
  • Runtime (String): The runtime environment
  • State (String): Function state (Pending, Active, Failed)
  • CodeSha256 (String): SHA256 hash of deployment package
Example 1: Create Django Background Task Function

Create a Lambda function for processing Django background tasks like email sending.

#!/bin/bash
# Create Django background task Lambda
FUNCTION_NAME="anihpj-email-sender"
REGION="us-east-1"
ROLE_ARN="arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpj-lambda-role"

echo "Creating Lambda function: $FUNCTION_NAME"

aws lambda create-function \
  --function-name $FUNCTION_NAME \
  --runtime python3.12 \
  --role $ROLE_ARN \
  --handler email_handler.send_email \
  --zip-file fileb://deployment.zip \
  --timeout 30 \
  --memory-size 256 \
  --environment "Variables={DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings,DB_HOST=anihpj-db.cluster-xxx.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com}" \
  --tags "Application=anihpj,Environment=Production" \
  --description "Sends emails for anihpj Django application" \
  --region $REGION

echo "✓ Lambda function created successfully"
Creating Lambda function: anihpj-email-sender { "FunctionName": "anihpj-email-sender", "FunctionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:anihpj-email-sender", "Runtime": "python3.12", "Role": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpj-lambda-role", "Handler": "email_handler.send_email", "CodeSize": 5242880, "Timeout": 30, "MemorySize": 256, "State": "Active" } ✓ Lambda function created successfully
# Create Django background task Lambda from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost lambda_function = { 'function_name': 'anihpj-email-sender', 'runtime': 'python3.12', 'handler': 'email_handler.send_email', 'memory': 256, 'timeout': 30, 'state': 'Active' } print(f"Lambda Function Created") print(f" Name: {lambda_function['function_name']}") print(f" Runtime: {lambda_function['runtime']}") print(f" Handler: {lambda_function['handler']}") print(f" Memory: {lambda_function['memory']} MB") print(f" Timeout: {lambda_function['timeout']}s") print(f"\n✓ Ready to process Django email tasks")
Example 2: Create Function from S3 Package

Create Lambda function with deployment package stored in S3.

#!/bin/bash
# Create function from S3 deployment package
FUNCTION_NAME="anihpj-image-processor"
REGION="us-east-1"
ROLE_ARN="arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpj-lambda-role"
S3_BUCKET="anihpj-deployments"
S3_KEY="lambda/image-processor-v1.0.0.zip"

aws lambda create-function \
  --function-name $FUNCTION_NAME \
  --runtime python3.12 \
  --role $ROLE_ARN \
  --handler image_processor.resize_image \
  --code S3Bucket=$S3_BUCKET,S3Key=$S3_KEY \
  --timeout 60 \
  --memory-size 512 \
  --environment "Variables={S3_MEDIA_BUCKET=anihpj-media,THUMBNAIL_SIZE=150}" \
  --region $REGION

echo "✓ Image processor Lambda created from S3"
{ "FunctionName": "anihpj-image-processor", "FunctionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:anihpj-image-processor", "Runtime": "python3.12", "Handler": "image_processor.resize_image", "CodeSize": 15728640, "Timeout": 60, "MemorySize": 512, "State": "Pending" } ✓ Image processor Lambda created from S3
# Create function from S3 package from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost s3_deployment = { 'function_name': 'anihpj-image-processor', 'source': 's3://anihpj-deployments/lambda/image-processor-v1.0.0.zip', 'state': 'Pending' } print(f"Lambda Created from S3") print(f" Function: {s3_deployment['function_name']}") print(f" Source: {s3_deployment['source']}") print(f"\n✓ Deployment from S3 initiated")
Example 3: Create VPC-Connected Function

Create Lambda in VPC to access private Django database.

#!/bin/bash
# Create VPC-connected Lambda for DB access
FUNCTION_NAME="anihpj-db-sync"
REGION="us-east-1"
ROLE_ARN="arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpj-lambda-vpc-role"
SUBNET_IDS="subnet-private-1a,subnet-private-1b"
SG_IDS="sg-lambda-db-access"

aws lambda create-function \
  --function-name $FUNCTION_NAME \
  --runtime python3.12 \
  --role $ROLE_ARN \
  --handler db_sync.sync_data \
  --zip-file fileb://db_sync.zip \
  --timeout 300 \
  --memory-size 1024 \
  --vpc-config SubnetIds=$SUBNET_IDS,SecurityGroupIds=$SG_IDS \
  --environment "Variables={DB_HOST=anihpj-db.internal,DB_NAME=anihpj_prod,DB_USER=lambda_user}" \
  --region $REGION

echo "✓ VPC Lambda created with private DB access"
{ "FunctionName": "anihpj-db-sync", "Runtime": "python3.12", "Timeout": 300, "MemorySize": 1024, "VpcConfig": { "SubnetIds": ["subnet-private-1a", "subnet-private-1b"], "SecurityGroupIds": ["sg-lambda-db-access"], "VpcId": "vpc-anihpj-prod" }, "State": "Pending" } ✓ VPC Lambda created with private DB access
# Create VPC-connected Lambda from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost vpc_lambda = { 'function_name': 'anihpj-db-sync', 'vpc_id': 'vpc-anihpj-prod', 'subnets': ['subnet-private-1a', 'subnet-private-1b'], 'can_access_db': True } print(f"VPC Lambda Created") print(f" Function: {vpc_lambda['function_name']}") print(f" VPC: {vpc_lambda['vpc_id']}") print(f" DB Access: ✓") print(f"\n✓ Can access private Django database")
Example 4: Create Function with Layers

Create Lambda with shared dependency layers for Django libraries.

#!/bin/bash
# Create function with dependency layers
FUNCTION_NAME="anihpj-report-generator"
REGION="us-east-1"
ROLE_ARN="arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpj-lambda-role"
DJANGO_LAYER="arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:django-deps:5"
PANDAS_LAYER="arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:pandas:3"

aws lambda create-function \
  --function-name $FUNCTION_NAME \
  --runtime python3.12 \
  --role $ROLE_ARN \
  --handler report_generator.generate \
  --zip-file fileb://report_generator.zip \
  --timeout 120 \
  --memory-size 2048 \
  --layers $DJANGO_LAYER $PANDAS_LAYER \
  --environment "Variables={REPORT_BUCKET=anihpj-reports}" \
  --region $REGION

echo "✓ Lambda created with Django and Pandas layers"
{ "FunctionName": "anihpj-report-generator", "Runtime": "python3.12", "MemorySize": 2048, "Layers": [ {"Arn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:django-deps:5"}, {"Arn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:pandas:3"} ], "State": "Active" } ✓ Lambda created with Django and Pandas layers
# Create function with layers from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost layered_function = { 'function_name': 'anihpj-report-generator', 'layers': ['django-deps:5', 'pandas:3'], 'memory': 2048 } print(f"Lambda with Layers") print(f" Function: {layered_function['function_name']}") print(f" Layers: {', '.join(layered_function['layers'])}") print(f"\n✓ Shared dependencies loaded from layers")
Example 5: Create Scheduled Task Function

Create Lambda for Django scheduled tasks (cron jobs).

#!/bin/bash
# Create scheduled task Lambda
FUNCTION_NAME="anihpj-daily-cleanup"
REGION="us-east-1"
ROLE_ARN="arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpj-lambda-role"

aws lambda create-function \
  --function-name $FUNCTION_NAME \
  --runtime python3.12 \
  --role $ROLE_ARN \
  --handler cleanup.run_daily \
  --zip-file fileb://cleanup.zip \
  --timeout 900 \
  --memory-size 512 \
  --environment "Variables={DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings,CLEANUP_DAYS=30}" \
  --tags "Application=anihpj,TaskType=Scheduled,Schedule=Daily" \
  --description "Daily cleanup: expired sessions, old logs, temp files" \
  --region $REGION

echo "✓ Scheduled task Lambda created"
echo "  Next: Create EventBridge rule to trigger daily"
{ "FunctionName": "anihpj-daily-cleanup", "Runtime": "python3.12", "Timeout": 900, "MemorySize": 512, "Description": "Daily cleanup: expired sessions, old logs, temp files", "State": "Active" } ✓ Scheduled task Lambda created Next: Create EventBridge rule to trigger daily
# Create scheduled task Lambda from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost scheduled_task = { 'function_name': 'anihpj-daily-cleanup', 'task_type': 'Scheduled', 'schedule': 'Daily', 'timeout': 900, 'tasks': ['expired sessions', 'old logs', 'temp files'] } print(f"Scheduled Task Lambda") print(f" Function: {scheduled_task['function_name']}") print(f" Schedule: {scheduled_task['schedule']}") print(f" Tasks: {', '.join(scheduled_task['tasks'])}") print(f"\n✓ Ready for EventBridge scheduling")

update-function-code

Updates a Lambda function's deployment package with new code from ZIP file or S3. Use for deploying new versions of Django background tasks, fixing bugs, or adding features without recreating the function.

Syntax
aws lambda update-function-code \
  --function-name  \
  [--zip-file fileb://] \
  [--s3-bucket ] \
  [--s3-key ] \
  [--s3-object-version ] \
  [--publish] \
  [--dry-run | --no-dry-run] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string): [Required] Function name or ARN
  • --zip-file (blob): Local ZIP file path (fileb://path)
  • --s3-bucket (string): S3 bucket with deployment package
  • --s3-key (string): S3 object key for deployment package
  • --s3-object-version (string): S3 object version ID
  • --publish (boolean): Publish a new version after update
  • --dry-run (boolean): Validate without updating
Return Value
  • FunctionName (String): The function name
  • CodeSha256 (String): SHA256 hash of new code
  • Version (String): Published version number
  • LastUpdateStatus (String): Update status
Example 1: Update from Local ZIP
#!/bin/bash
# Update Django Lambda with new code
FUNCTION_NAME="anihpj-email-sender"

echo "Updating function: $FUNCTION_NAME"

aws lambda update-function-code \
  --function-name $FUNCTION_NAME \
  --zip-file fileb://deployment.zip \
  --publish \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Code updated and published"
Updating function: anihpj-email-sender { "FunctionName": "anihpj-email-sender", "CodeSha256": "abc123def456...", "Version": "5", "LastUpdateStatus": "Successful" } ✓ Code updated and published
# Update Lambda code from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost update_result = { 'function': 'anihpj-email-sender', 'new_version': '5', 'status': 'Successful' } print(f"Code Updated: {update_result['function']}") print(f"New Version: {update_result['new_version']}")
Example 2: Update from S3
#!/bin/bash
# Update from S3 deployment package
aws lambda update-function-code \
  --function-name anihpj-image-processor \
  --s3-bucket anihpj-deployments \
  --s3-key lambda/image-processor-v2.0.0.zip \
  --publish \
  --region us-east-1
{ "FunctionName": "anihpj-image-processor", "CodeSha256": "xyz789ghi012...", "Version": "3", "LastUpdateStatus": "InProgress" }
# Update from S3 from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Updating from S3...") print(f"Source: s3://anihpj-deployments/lambda/image-processor-v2.0.0.zip") print(f"✓ Deployment initiated")
Example 3: Dry Run Validation
#!/bin/bash
# Validate code before deploying
aws lambda update-function-code \
  --function-name anihpj-report-generator \
  --zip-file fileb://new_code.zip \
  --dry-run \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Validation passed - ready to deploy"
{ "FunctionName": "anihpj-report-generator", "CodeSha256": "preview123...", "DryRunResult": "Success" } ✓ Validation passed - ready to deploy
# Dry run validation from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Dry Run: Validation passed") print(f"✓ Code package is valid")
Example 4: CI/CD Pipeline Update
#!/bin/bash
# Automated deployment script
VERSION=$(git describe --tags)
FUNCTION_NAME="anihpj-api-handler"
S3_BUCKET="anihpj-deployments"
S3_KEY="lambda/api-handler-${VERSION}.zip"

# Upload to S3 first
aws s3 cp deployment.zip s3://$S3_BUCKET/$S3_KEY

# Update Lambda
aws lambda update-function-code \
  --function-name $FUNCTION_NAME \
  --s3-bucket $S3_BUCKET \
  --s3-key $S3_KEY \
  --publish

echo "✓ Deployed version: $VERSION"
upload: ./deployment.zip to s3://anihpj-deployments/lambda/api-handler-v1.2.3.zip { "FunctionName": "anihpj-api-handler", "Version": "12", "LastUpdateStatus": "Successful" } ✓ Deployed version: v1.2.3
# CI/CD deployment from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost deployment = { 'function': 'anihpj-api-handler', 'version': 'v1.2.3', 'lambda_version': '12' } print(f"CI/CD Deployment Complete") print(f" Git Tag: {deployment['version']}") print(f" Lambda Version: {deployment['lambda_version']}")
Example 5: Rollback to Previous Version
#!/bin/bash
# Rollback by deploying previous S3 version
FUNCTION_NAME="anihpj-email-sender"
PREVIOUS_VERSION="v1.0.5"

aws lambda update-function-code \
  --function-name $FUNCTION_NAME \
  --s3-bucket anihpj-deployments \
  --s3-key lambda/email-sender-${PREVIOUS_VERSION}.zip \
  --publish \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Rolled back to $PREVIOUS_VERSION"
{ "FunctionName": "anihpj-email-sender", "Version": "6", "LastUpdateStatus": "Successful" } ✓ Rolled back to v1.0.5
# Rollback deployment from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Rollback Complete") print(f" Function: anihpj-email-sender") print(f" Restored: v1.0.5") print(f"✓ Previous version now active")

update-function-configuration

Modifies a Lambda function's configuration without changing code. Update memory, timeout, environment variables, VPC settings, and runtime. Essential for tuning Django Lambda performance.

Syntax
aws lambda update-function-configuration \
  --function-name  \
  [--role ] \
  [--handler ] \
  [--description ] \
  [--timeout ] \
  [--memory-size ] \
  [--runtime ] \
  [--environment Variables={=}] \
  [--vpc-config SubnetIds=,SecurityGroupIds=] \
  [--layers ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string): [Required] Function name or ARN
  • --timeout (integer): Execution timeout (1-900 seconds)
  • --memory-size (integer): Memory allocation (128-10240 MB)
  • --runtime (string): Runtime environment
  • --environment (structure): Environment variables
  • --vpc-config (structure): VPC configuration
  • --layers (list): Layer ARNs
Return Value
  • FunctionName (String): The function name
  • MemorySize (Integer): Updated memory size
  • Timeout (Integer): Updated timeout
  • LastModified (String): Modification timestamp
Example 1: Increase Memory and Timeout
#!/bin/bash
# Tune Lambda for heavy Django tasks
aws lambda update-function-configuration \
  --function-name anihpj-report-generator \
  --memory-size 2048 \
  --timeout 300 \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Memory: 2048MB, Timeout: 5 minutes"
{ "FunctionName": "anihpj-report-generator", "MemorySize": 2048, "Timeout": 300, "LastModified": "2026-01-13T10:30:00.000+0000" } ✓ Memory: 2048MB, Timeout: 5 minutes
# Update memory and timeout from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost config = {'memory': 2048, 'timeout': 300} print(f"Updated: {config['memory']}MB, {config['timeout']}s")
Example 2: Update Environment Variables
#!/bin/bash
# Update Django settings
aws lambda update-function-configuration \
  --function-name anihpj-email-sender \
  --environment "Variables={DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings.production,DEBUG=false,EMAIL_HOST=smtp.ses.amazonaws.com}" \
  --region us-east-1
{ "FunctionName": "anihpj-email-sender", "Environment": { "Variables": { "DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE": "anihpj.settings.production", "DEBUG": "false", "EMAIL_HOST": "smtp.ses.amazonaws.com" } } }
# Update environment variables from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Environment Updated:") print(f" DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings.production") print(f" DEBUG=false")
Example 3: Add VPC Configuration
#!/bin/bash
# Connect Lambda to VPC for DB access
aws lambda update-function-configuration \
  --function-name anihpj-db-sync \
  --vpc-config SubnetIds=subnet-private-1a,subnet-private-1b,SecurityGroupIds=sg-lambda-db \
  --region us-east-1
{ "FunctionName": "anihpj-db-sync", "VpcConfig": { "SubnetIds": ["subnet-private-1a", "subnet-private-1b"], "SecurityGroupIds": ["sg-lambda-db"], "VpcId": "vpc-anihpj" } }
# Add VPC configuration from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"VPC Connected: vpc-anihpj") print(f"✓ Can access private Django database")
Example 4: Update Runtime Version
#!/bin/bash
# Upgrade Python runtime
aws lambda update-function-configuration \
  --function-name anihpj-image-processor \
  --runtime python3.12 \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Upgraded to Python 3.12"
{ "FunctionName": "anihpj-image-processor", "Runtime": "python3.12", "LastModified": "2026-01-13T11:00:00.000+0000" } ✓ Upgraded to Python 3.12
# Update runtime from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Runtime Upgraded: python3.12")
Example 5: Update Layers
#!/bin/bash
# Add new dependency layer
aws lambda update-function-configuration \
  --function-name anihpj-report-generator \
  --layers \
    arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:django-deps:6 \
    arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:pandas:4 \
  --region us-east-1
{ "FunctionName": "anihpj-report-generator", "Layers": [ {"Arn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:django-deps:6"}, {"Arn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:pandas:4"} ] }
# Update layers from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Layers Updated:") print(f" django-deps:6, pandas:4")

invoke

Invokes a Lambda function synchronously or asynchronously. Execute Django background tasks, test functions, or trigger event processing. Returns function output and execution details.

Syntax
aws lambda invoke \
  --function-name  \
  [--invocation-type RequestResponse|Event|DryRun] \
  [--payload ] \
  [--log-type Tail|None] \
  [--qualifier ] \
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string): [Required] Function name or ARN
  • --invocation-type (string): RequestResponse (sync), Event (async), DryRun
  • --payload (blob): JSON input to the function
  • --log-type (string): Tail to include logs in response
  • --qualifier (string): Version or alias to invoke
  • outfile (string): [Required] File for response output
Return Value
  • StatusCode (Integer): HTTP status code (200, 202)
  • FunctionError (String): Error type if failed
  • LogResult (String): Base64-encoded logs
  • ExecutedVersion (String): Version executed
Example 1: Synchronous Invocation
#!/bin/bash
# Invoke Django email task synchronously
aws lambda invoke \
  --function-name anihpj-email-sender \
  --invocation-type RequestResponse \
  --payload '{"user_id": 123, "template": "welcome"}' \
  --log-type Tail \
  response.json \
  --region us-east-1

cat response.json
echo "✓ Email sent"
{ "StatusCode": 200, "ExecutedVersion": "$LATEST" } {"status": "sent", "email": "user@example.com"} ✓ Email sent
# Synchronous invoke from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost result = {'status': 'sent', 'user_id': 123} print(f"Email sent to user {result['user_id']}")
Example 2: Asynchronous Invocation
#!/bin/bash
# Fire-and-forget async invocation
aws lambda invoke \
  --function-name anihpj-image-processor \
  --invocation-type Event \
  --payload '{"s3_key": "uploads/photo.jpg", "sizes": [150, 300, 600]}' \
  response.json \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Image processing queued (async)"
{ "StatusCode": 202 } ✓ Image processing queued (async)
# Async invoke from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Task queued asynchronously") print(f"StatusCode: 202 (Accepted)")
Example 3: Invoke Specific Version
#!/bin/bash
# Invoke specific version for testing
aws lambda invoke \
  --function-name anihpj-report-generator \
  --qualifier 5 \
  --payload '{"report_type": "monthly", "month": "2026-01"}' \
  report_output.json \
  --region us-east-1

cat report_output.json
{ "StatusCode": 200, "ExecutedVersion": "5" } {"report_url": "s3://anihpj-reports/monthly-2026-01.pdf"}
# Invoke specific version from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Version 5 executed") print(f"Report: monthly-2026-01.pdf")
Example 4: Invoke Alias (Production)
#!/bin/bash
# Invoke production alias
aws lambda invoke \
  --function-name anihpj-api-handler \
  --qualifier prod \
  --payload '{"action": "get_jobs", "limit": 10}' \
  jobs_response.json \
  --region us-east-1
{ "StatusCode": 200, "ExecutedVersion": "15" }
# Invoke prod alias from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost jobs = JobPost.objects.all()[:10] print(f"Retrieved {len(jobs)} jobs via prod alias")
Example 5: Dry Run Test
#!/bin/bash
# Validate permissions without executing
aws lambda invoke \
  --function-name anihpj-daily-cleanup \
  --invocation-type DryRun \
  --payload '{"dry_run": true}' \
  test.json \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Permission check passed"
{ "StatusCode": 204 } ✓ Permission check passed
# Dry run test from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"DryRun: Permissions validated") print(f"StatusCode: 204 (No Content)")

list-functions

Lists all Lambda functions in your account with configuration details. Inventory Django microservices, audit functions, or find functions by runtime. Supports pagination for large deployments.

Syntax
aws lambda list-functions \
  [--master-region ] \
  [--function-version ALL] \
  [--max-items ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --master-region (string): Region for Lambda@Edge functions
  • --function-version (string): ALL to include versions
  • --max-items (integer): Maximum functions to return
Return Value
  • Functions (List): Array of function configurations
  • NextMarker (String): Pagination token
Example 1: List All Functions
#!/bin/bash
# List all Lambda functions
aws lambda list-functions \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --query 'Functions[*].[FunctionName,Runtime,MemorySize]' \
  --output table
------------------------------------------------- | ListFunctions | +----------------------------+-----------+------+ | anihpj-email-sender | python3.12| 256 | | anihpj-image-processor | python3.12| 512 | | anihpj-report-generator | python3.12| 2048 | | anihpj-daily-cleanup | python3.12| 512 | | anihpj-api-handler | python3.12| 1024 | +----------------------------+-----------+------+
# List all functions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost functions = ['email-sender', 'image-processor', 'report-generator'] print(f"Found {len(functions)} anihpj Lambda functions")
Example 2: Filter by Runtime
#!/bin/bash
# Find Python 3.12 functions
aws lambda list-functions \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --query 'Functions[?Runtime==`python3.12`].FunctionName' \
  --output text
anihpj-email-sender anihpj-image-processor anihpj-report-generator anihpj-daily-cleanup
# Filter by runtime from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost python312_functions = 4 print(f"Python 3.12 functions: {python312_functions}")
Example 3: List with Memory Details
#!/bin/bash
# Show memory and timeout details
aws lambda list-functions \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --query 'Functions[*].{Name:FunctionName,Memory:MemorySize,Timeout:Timeout}' \
  --output table
-------------------------------------------------- | ListFunctions | +----------------------------+--------+----------+ | Name | Memory | Timeout | +----------------------------+--------+----------+ | anihpj-email-sender | 256 | 30 | | anihpj-image-processor | 512 | 60 | | anihpj-report-generator | 2048 | 300 | +----------------------------+--------+----------+
# Memory details from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Memory Allocation:") print(f" email-sender: 256MB") print(f" report-generator: 2048MB")
Example 4: Count Functions by Tag
#!/bin/bash
# List and count anihpj functions
FUNCTION_COUNT=$(aws lambda list-functions \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --query 'Functions[?contains(FunctionName,`anihpj`)].FunctionName' \
  --output text | wc -w)

echo "Total anihpj functions: $FUNCTION_COUNT"
Total anihpj functions: 5
# Count functions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost anihpj_functions = 5 print(f"Total anihpj functions: {anihpj_functions}")
Example 5: Export Function Inventory
#!/bin/bash
# Export to JSON for documentation
aws lambda list-functions \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --query 'Functions[*].{Name:FunctionName,ARN:FunctionArn,Runtime:Runtime,Handler:Handler}' \
  --output json > lambda_inventory.json

echo "✓ Exported to lambda_inventory.json"
✓ Exported to lambda_inventory.json
# Export inventory from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Inventory exported to JSON") print(f"✓ Ready for documentation")

get-function

Gets detailed information about a Lambda function including code location, configuration, tags, and concurrency settings. Retrieve deployment package URL for debugging or backup.

Syntax
aws lambda get-function \
  --function-name  \
  [--qualifier ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string): [Required] Function name or ARN
  • --qualifier (string): Version number or alias
Return Value
  • Configuration (Object): Function configuration
  • Code (Object): Code location with presigned URL
  • Tags (Object): Resource tags
  • Concurrency (Object): Reserved concurrency
Example 1: Get Full Function Details
#!/bin/bash
# Get complete function information
aws lambda get-function \
  --function-name anihpj-email-sender \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Configuration": { "FunctionName": "anihpj-email-sender", "Runtime": "python3.12", "Handler": "email_handler.send_email", "MemorySize": 256, "Timeout": 30, "State": "Active" }, "Code": { "RepositoryType": "S3", "Location": "https://awslambda-us-east-1-tasks.s3..." }, "Tags": { "Application": "anihpj", "Environment": "Production" } }
# Get function details from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost func = {'name': 'anihpj-email-sender', 'state': 'Active'} print(f"Function: {func['name']}, State: {func['state']}")
Example 2: Get Configuration Only
#!/bin/bash
# Extract configuration
aws lambda get-function \
  --function-name anihpj-image-processor \
  --query 'Configuration' \
  --region us-east-1
{ "FunctionName": "anihpj-image-processor", "Runtime": "python3.12", "MemorySize": 512, "Timeout": 60, "VpcConfig": { "SubnetIds": [], "SecurityGroupIds": [] } }
# Get config only from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Memory: 512MB, Timeout: 60s")
Example 3: Download Code Package
#!/bin/bash
# Get download URL and fetch code
CODE_URL=$(aws lambda get-function \
  --function-name anihpj-report-generator \
  --query 'Code.Location' \
  --output text \
  --region us-east-1)

curl -o function_code.zip "$CODE_URL"
echo "✓ Code downloaded to function_code.zip"
% Total % Received 100 5.2M 100 5.2M ✓ Code downloaded to function_code.zip
# Download code from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Code package downloaded") print(f"✓ Ready for local debugging")
Example 4: Get Specific Version
#!/bin/bash
# Get version 5 details
aws lambda get-function \
  --function-name anihpj-api-handler \
  --qualifier 5 \
  --query 'Configuration.{Version:Version,CodeSha:CodeSha256}' \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Version": "5", "CodeSha": "abc123def456789..." }
# Get version details from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Version: 5") print(f"CodeSha: abc123def456789...")
Example 5: Get Tags
#!/bin/bash
# Get function tags
aws lambda get-function \
  --function-name anihpj-daily-cleanup \
  --query 'Tags' \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Application": "anihpj", "Environment": "Production", "TaskType": "Scheduled", "Schedule": "Daily" }
# Get tags from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost tags = {'Application': 'anihpj', 'TaskType': 'Scheduled'} print(f"Tags: {tags}")

get-function-configuration

Gets only the configuration settings of a Lambda function without code location. Faster than get-function when you only need runtime settings, environment variables, or VPC config.

Syntax
aws lambda get-function-configuration \
  --function-name  \
  [--qualifier ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string): [Required] Function name or ARN
  • --qualifier (string): Version number or alias
Return Value
  • FunctionName (String): Function name
  • Runtime (String): Runtime environment
  • Environment (Object): Environment variables
  • VpcConfig (Object): VPC settings
Example 1: Get Full Configuration
#!/bin/bash
# Get function configuration
aws lambda get-function-configuration \
  --function-name anihpj-email-sender \
  --region us-east-1
{ "FunctionName": "anihpj-email-sender", "Runtime": "python3.12", "Handler": "email_handler.send_email", "MemorySize": 256, "Timeout": 30, "Environment": { "Variables": { "DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE": "anihpj.settings" } } }
# Get configuration from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost config = {'memory': 256, 'timeout': 30, 'runtime': 'python3.12'} print(f"Config: {config}")
Example 2: Get Environment Variables
#!/bin/bash
# Extract environment variables
aws lambda get-function-configuration \
  --function-name anihpj-db-sync \
  --query 'Environment.Variables' \
  --region us-east-1
{ "DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE": "anihpj.settings", "DB_HOST": "anihpj-db.internal", "DB_NAME": "anihpj_prod" }
# Get env vars from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"DB_HOST: anihpj-db.internal") print(f"DB_NAME: anihpj_prod")
Example 3: Check VPC Configuration
#!/bin/bash
# Verify VPC settings
aws lambda get-function-configuration \
  --function-name anihpj-db-sync \
  --query 'VpcConfig' \
  --region us-east-1
{ "SubnetIds": ["subnet-private-1a", "subnet-private-1b"], "SecurityGroupIds": ["sg-lambda-db"], "VpcId": "vpc-anihpj" }
# Check VPC from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"VPC: vpc-anihpj") print(f"Subnets: 2 private")
Example 4: Get Layers
#!/bin/bash
# Check attached layers
aws lambda get-function-configuration \
  --function-name anihpj-report-generator \
  --query 'Layers[*].Arn' \
  --region us-east-1
[ "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:django-deps:6", "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:pandas:4" ]
# Get layers from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost layers = ['django-deps:6', 'pandas:4'] print(f"Layers: {layers}")
Example 5: Monitor Function State
#!/bin/bash
# Check function state after update
aws lambda get-function-configuration \
  --function-name anihpj-image-processor \
  --query '{State:State,LastUpdate:LastUpdateStatus,Reason:LastUpdateStatusReason}' \
  --region us-east-1
{ "State": "Active", "LastUpdate": "Successful", "Reason": null }
# Monitor state from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"State: Active") print(f"LastUpdate: Successful")

delete-function

Deletes a Lambda function and all associated versions and aliases. Removes Django background tasks no longer needed. Use qualifier to delete specific version only.

Syntax
aws lambda delete-function \
  --function-name  \
  [--qualifier ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string): [Required] Function name or ARN
  • --qualifier (string): Version to delete (omit to delete all)
Return Value
  • No response body on success (HTTP 204)
Example 1: Delete Function
#!/bin/bash
# Delete deprecated Lambda function
aws lambda delete-function \
  --function-name anihpj-old-processor \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Function deleted"
✓ Function deleted
# Delete function from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Function anihpj-old-processor deleted")
Example 2: Delete Specific Version
#!/bin/bash
# Delete old version
aws lambda delete-function \
  --function-name anihpj-email-sender \
  --qualifier 1 \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Version 1 deleted"
✓ Version 1 deleted
# Delete version from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Version 1 of email-sender deleted")
Example 3: Cleanup Old Versions
#!/bin/bash
# Delete all versions except last 3
FUNCTION_NAME="anihpj-api-handler"

VERSIONS=$(aws lambda list-versions-by-function \
  --function-name $FUNCTION_NAME \
  --query 'Versions[?Version!=`$LATEST`].Version' \
  --output text | head -n -3)

for VERSION in $VERSIONS; do
  aws lambda delete-function \
    --function-name $FUNCTION_NAME \
    --qualifier $VERSION
  echo "Deleted version: $VERSION"
done
Deleted version: 1 Deleted version: 2 Deleted version: 3
# Cleanup versions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost deleted = [1, 2, 3] print(f"Deleted {len(deleted)} old versions")
Example 4: Delete with Confirmation
#!/bin/bash
# Safe delete with confirmation
FUNCTION_NAME="anihpj-temp-task"

read -p "Delete $FUNCTION_NAME? (y/n): " confirm
if [ "$confirm" = "y" ]; then
  aws lambda delete-function \
    --function-name $FUNCTION_NAME \
    --region us-east-1
  echo "✓ Deleted"
else
  echo "Cancelled"
fi
Delete anihpj-temp-task? (y/n): y ✓ Deleted
# Safe delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"User confirmed deletion") print(f"✓ anihpj-temp-task deleted")
Example 5: Bulk Delete by Pattern
#!/bin/bash
# Delete all test functions
aws lambda list-functions \
  --query 'Functions[?contains(FunctionName,`test`)].FunctionName' \
  --output text | \
while read FUNC; do
  aws lambda delete-function --function-name $FUNC
  echo "Deleted: $FUNC"
done
Deleted: anihpj-test-handler Deleted: anihpj-test-processor
# Bulk delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost deleted_count = 2 print(f"Deleted {deleted_count} test functions")

add-permission

Adds a resource-based policy statement to allow another AWS service to invoke your Lambda function. Required for S3 triggers, API Gateway, EventBridge, SNS, and other AWS service integrations.

Syntax
aws lambda add-permission \
  --function-name  \
  --statement-id  \
  --action  \
  --principal  \
  [--source-arn ] \
  [--source-account ] \
  [--qualifier ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string): [Required] Function name or ARN
  • --statement-id (string): [Required] Unique statement identifier
  • --action (string): [Required] Lambda action (lambda:InvokeFunction)
  • --principal (string): [Required] AWS service principal
  • --source-arn (string): ARN of invoking resource
  • --source-account (string): AWS account ID
Return Value
  • Statement (String): JSON policy statement added
Example 1: Allow S3 Trigger
#!/bin/bash
# Allow S3 to invoke image processor
aws lambda add-permission \
  --function-name anihpj-image-processor \
  --statement-id S3InvokePermission \
  --action lambda:InvokeFunction \
  --principal s3.amazonaws.com \
  --source-arn arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-uploads \
  --source-account 123456789012 \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ S3 can now trigger Lambda"
{ "Statement": "{\"Sid\":\"S3InvokePermission\",\"Effect\":\"Allow\",\"Principal\":{\"Service\":\"s3.amazonaws.com\"},\"Action\":\"lambda:InvokeFunction\",\"Resource\":\"arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:anihpj-image-processor\"}" } ✓ S3 can now trigger Lambda
# Allow S3 trigger from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Permission added: S3 → Lambda") print(f"Bucket: anihpj-uploads")
Example 2: Allow API Gateway
#!/bin/bash
# Allow API Gateway to invoke
aws lambda add-permission \
  --function-name anihpj-api-handler \
  --statement-id APIGatewayInvoke \
  --action lambda:InvokeFunction \
  --principal apigateway.amazonaws.com \
  --source-arn "arn:aws:execute-api:us-east-1:123456789012:abc123/*/*/*" \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Statement": "{\"Sid\":\"APIGatewayInvoke\",\"Effect\":\"Allow\",\"Principal\":{\"Service\":\"apigateway.amazonaws.com\"},\"Action\":\"lambda:InvokeFunction\"}" }
# API Gateway permission from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"API Gateway can invoke Lambda")
Example 3: Allow EventBridge (Scheduled)
#!/bin/bash
# Allow EventBridge rule to trigger
aws lambda add-permission \
  --function-name anihpj-daily-cleanup \
  --statement-id EventBridgeDaily \
  --action lambda:InvokeFunction \
  --principal events.amazonaws.com \
  --source-arn arn:aws:events:us-east-1:123456789012:rule/anihpj-daily-rule \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Statement": "{\"Sid\":\"EventBridgeDaily\",\"Effect\":\"Allow\",\"Principal\":{\"Service\":\"events.amazonaws.com\"}}" }
# EventBridge permission from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"EventBridge rule can trigger cleanup")
Example 4: Allow SNS Topic
#!/bin/bash
# Allow SNS to invoke
aws lambda add-permission \
  --function-name anihpj-notification-handler \
  --statement-id SNSInvoke \
  --action lambda:InvokeFunction \
  --principal sns.amazonaws.com \
  --source-arn arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:anihpj-notifications \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Statement": "{\"Sid\":\"SNSInvoke\",\"Effect\":\"Allow\",\"Principal\":{\"Service\":\"sns.amazonaws.com\"}}" }
# SNS permission from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"SNS topic can invoke Lambda")
Example 5: Allow SQS Queue
#!/bin/bash
# Allow SQS to invoke
aws lambda add-permission \
  --function-name anihpj-queue-processor \
  --statement-id SQSInvoke \
  --action lambda:InvokeFunction \
  --principal sqs.amazonaws.com \
  --source-arn arn:aws:sqs:us-east-1:123456789012:anihpj-task-queue \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Statement": "{\"Sid\":\"SQSInvoke\",\"Effect\":\"Allow\"}" }
# SQS permission from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"SQS queue can trigger Lambda")

remove-permission

Removes a resource-based policy statement from a Lambda function. Revoke access from AWS services that should no longer trigger the function.

Syntax
aws lambda remove-permission \
  --function-name  \
  --statement-id  \
  [--qualifier ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string): [Required] Function name or ARN
  • --statement-id (string): [Required] Statement ID to remove
  • --qualifier (string): Version or alias
Return Value
  • No response body on success (HTTP 204)
Example 1: Remove S3 Permission
#!/bin/bash
# Remove S3 trigger permission
aws lambda remove-permission \
  --function-name anihpj-image-processor \
  --statement-id S3InvokePermission \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ S3 permission removed"
✓ S3 permission removed
# Remove S3 permission from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"S3 can no longer trigger Lambda")
Example 2: Remove API Gateway Permission
#!/bin/bash
# Revoke API Gateway access
aws lambda remove-permission \
  --function-name anihpj-api-handler \
  --statement-id APIGatewayInvoke \
  --region us-east-1
# Remove API permission from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"API Gateway access revoked")
Example 3: Remove from Specific Alias
#!/bin/bash
# Remove permission from prod alias
aws lambda remove-permission \
  --function-name anihpj-api-handler \
  --statement-id ProdAPIGateway \
  --qualifier prod \
  --region us-east-1
# Remove from alias from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Permission removed from prod alias")
Example 4: Remove EventBridge Permission
#!/bin/bash
# Stop scheduled triggers
aws lambda remove-permission \
  --function-name anihpj-daily-cleanup \
  --statement-id EventBridgeDaily \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Scheduled trigger disabled"
✓ Scheduled trigger disabled
# Remove EventBridge from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Scheduled trigger disabled")
Example 5: Cleanup All Permissions
#!/bin/bash
# Get all statement IDs and remove
FUNCTION_NAME="anihpj-old-handler"

STATEMENTS=$(aws lambda get-policy \
  --function-name $FUNCTION_NAME \
  --query 'Policy' \
  --output text 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.Statement[].Sid')

for SID in $STATEMENTS; do
  aws lambda remove-permission \
    --function-name $FUNCTION_NAME \
    --statement-id $SID
  echo "Removed: $SID"
done
Removed: S3Invoke Removed: APIGateway Removed: EventBridge
# Cleanup permissions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost removed = ['S3Invoke', 'APIGateway', 'EventBridge'] print(f"Removed {len(removed)} permissions")

get-policy

Gets the resource-based policy attached to a Lambda function. View all permissions granted to AWS services for triggering your function. Essential for security audits.

Syntax
aws lambda get-policy \
  --function-name  \
  [--qualifier ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string): [Required] Function name or ARN
  • --qualifier (string): Version or alias
Return Value
  • Policy (String): JSON policy document
  • RevisionId (String): Policy revision ID
Example 1: Get Function Policy
#!/bin/bash
# Get Lambda function policy
aws lambda get-policy \
  --function-name anihpj-image-processor \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Policy": "{\"Version\":\"2012-10-17\",\"Statement\":[{\"Sid\":\"S3Invoke\",\"Effect\":\"Allow\",\"Principal\":{\"Service\":\"s3.amazonaws.com\"},\"Action\":\"lambda:InvokeFunction\",\"Resource\":\"arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:anihpj-image-processor\"}]}", "RevisionId": "abc123-def456" }
# Get policy from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Policy shows S3 can invoke function")
Example 2: Parse Policy JSON
#!/bin/bash
# Get formatted policy
aws lambda get-policy \
  --function-name anihpj-api-handler \
  --query 'Policy' \
  --output text \
  --region us-east-1 | jq '.'
{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "APIGatewayInvoke", "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "Service": "apigateway.amazonaws.com" }, "Action": "lambda:InvokeFunction" } ] }
# Parse policy from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost statements = [{'Sid': 'APIGatewayInvoke', 'Principal': 'apigateway.amazonaws.com'}] print(f"Statements: {len(statements)}")
Example 3: List All Principals
#!/bin/bash
# Extract all service principals
aws lambda get-policy \
  --function-name anihpj-queue-processor \
  --query 'Policy' \
  --output text \
  --region us-east-1 | \
  jq -r '.Statement[].Principal.Service'
sqs.amazonaws.com sns.amazonaws.com
# List principals from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost principals = ['sqs.amazonaws.com', 'sns.amazonaws.com'] print(f"Principals: {principals}")
Example 4: Check Alias Policy
#!/bin/bash
# Get policy for prod alias
aws lambda get-policy \
  --function-name anihpj-api-handler \
  --qualifier prod \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Policy": "{\"Version\":\"2012-10-17\",\"Statement\":[{\"Sid\":\"ProdAPI\"}]}", "RevisionId": "xyz789" }
# Alias policy from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Prod alias has separate policy")
Example 5: Security Audit
#!/bin/bash
# Audit all functions for policies
for FUNC in $(aws lambda list-functions --query 'Functions[*].FunctionName' --output text); do
  echo "=== $FUNC ==="
  aws lambda get-policy --function-name $FUNC 2>/dev/null || echo "No policy"
done
=== anihpj-email-sender === No policy === anihpj-image-processor === {"Policy":"{...}","RevisionId":"abc123"}
# Security audit from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost functions_with_policies = 3 print(f"{functions_with_policies} functions have policies")

create-event-source-mapping

Creates a mapping between an event source (SQS, DynamoDB, Kinesis) and a Lambda function. Lambda polls the source and invokes the function with batches of records. Essential for processing Django task queues.

Syntax
aws lambda create-event-source-mapping \
  --function-name  \
  --event-source-arn  \
  [--batch-size ] \
  [--maximum-batching-window-in-seconds ] \
  [--enabled | --no-enabled] \
  [--starting-position ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string): [Required] Function name or ARN
  • --event-source-arn (string): [Required] ARN of event source
  • --batch-size (integer): Records per batch (1-10000)
  • --maximum-batching-window-in-seconds (integer): Max wait time
  • --enabled (boolean): Enable mapping immediately
  • --starting-position (string): LATEST, TRIM_HORIZON, AT_TIMESTAMP
Return Value
  • UUID (String): Mapping identifier
  • State (String): Creating, Enabled, Disabled
  • EventSourceArn (String): Source ARN
Example 1: Map SQS Queue
#!/bin/bash
# Connect SQS queue to Lambda
aws lambda create-event-source-mapping \
  --function-name anihpj-queue-processor \
  --event-source-arn arn:aws:sqs:us-east-1:123456789012:anihpj-task-queue \
  --batch-size 10 \
  --enabled \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ SQS queue mapped to Lambda"
{ "UUID": "a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890", "BatchSize": 10, "EventSourceArn": "arn:aws:sqs:us-east-1:123456789012:anihpj-task-queue", "FunctionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:anihpj-queue-processor", "State": "Creating" } ✓ SQS queue mapped to Lambda
# Map SQS to Lambda from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost mapping = {'source': 'SQS', 'batch_size': 10, 'state': 'Creating'} print(f"Mapping: {mapping}")
Example 2: Map DynamoDB Stream
#!/bin/bash
# Process DynamoDB changes
aws lambda create-event-source-mapping \
  --function-name anihpj-stream-processor \
  --event-source-arn arn:aws:dynamodb:us-east-1:123456789012:table/anihpj-jobs/stream/2026-01-13 \
  --batch-size 100 \
  --starting-position LATEST \
  --enabled \
  --region us-east-1
{ "UUID": "b2c3d4e5-f6a7-8901-bcde-f23456789012", "BatchSize": 100, "StartingPosition": "LATEST", "State": "Creating" }
# Map DynamoDB stream from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"DynamoDB stream → Lambda") print(f"Starting: LATEST records only")
Example 3: Map Kinesis Stream
#!/bin/bash
# Process Kinesis events
aws lambda create-event-source-mapping \
  --function-name anihpj-kinesis-processor \
  --event-source-arn arn:aws:kinesis:us-east-1:123456789012:stream/anihpj-events \
  --batch-size 500 \
  --starting-position TRIM_HORIZON \
  --maximum-batching-window-in-seconds 30 \
  --region us-east-1
{ "UUID": "c3d4e5f6-a7b8-9012-cdef-a34567890123", "BatchSize": 500, "MaximumBatchingWindowInSeconds": 30, "State": "Creating" }
# Map Kinesis from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Kinesis stream mapped") print(f"Batch window: 30 seconds")
Example 4: Disabled Mapping (for testing)
#!/bin/bash
# Create disabled mapping for testing
aws lambda create-event-source-mapping \
  --function-name anihpj-test-processor \
  --event-source-arn arn:aws:sqs:us-east-1:123456789012:anihpj-test-queue \
  --batch-size 1 \
  --no-enabled \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Mapping created (disabled)"
{ "UUID": "d4e5f6a7-b8c9-0123-defa-b45678901234", "State": "Disabled" } ✓ Mapping created (disabled)
# Disabled mapping from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Mapping created but disabled") print(f"Enable manually when ready")
Example 5: FIFO Queue Mapping
#!/bin/bash
# Map FIFO SQS queue (ordered processing)
aws lambda create-event-source-mapping \
  --function-name anihpj-order-processor \
  --event-source-arn arn:aws:sqs:us-east-1:123456789012:anihpj-orders.fifo \
  --batch-size 5 \
  --enabled \
  --region us-east-1
{ "UUID": "e5f6a7b8-c9d0-1234-efab-c56789012345", "BatchSize": 5, "EventSourceArn": "arn:aws:sqs:us-east-1:123456789012:anihpj-orders.fifo", "State": "Creating" }
# FIFO queue mapping from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"FIFO queue mapped") print(f"✓ Ordered message processing")

list-event-source-mappings

Lists event source mappings for a Lambda function or event source. View all SQS, DynamoDB, Kinesis triggers connected to your Django background processors.

Syntax
aws lambda list-event-source-mappings \
  [--event-source-arn ] \
  [--function-name ] \
  [--max-items ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --event-source-arn (string): Filter by event source ARN
  • --function-name (string): Filter by function name
  • --max-items (integer): Maximum mappings to return
Return Value
  • EventSourceMappings (List): Array of mapping configurations
  • NextMarker (String): Pagination token
Example 1: List Function Mappings
#!/bin/bash
# List all mappings for a function
aws lambda list-event-source-mappings \
  --function-name anihpj-queue-processor \
  --region us-east-1
{ "EventSourceMappings": [ { "UUID": "a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890", "BatchSize": 10, "EventSourceArn": "arn:aws:sqs:us-east-1:123456789012:anihpj-task-queue", "FunctionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:anihpj-queue-processor", "State": "Enabled" } ] }
# List function mappings from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost mappings = [{'source': 'SQS', 'state': 'Enabled'}] print(f"Found {len(mappings)} mappings")
Example 2: List by Event Source
#!/bin/bash
# Find functions connected to queue
aws lambda list-event-source-mappings \
  --event-source-arn arn:aws:sqs:us-east-1:123456789012:anihpj-task-queue \
  --region us-east-1
{ "EventSourceMappings": [ { "UUID": "a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890", "FunctionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:anihpj-queue-processor", "State": "Enabled" } ] }
# List by source from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Queue connected to 1 Lambda function")
Example 3: List All Mappings
#!/bin/bash
# List all event source mappings
aws lambda list-event-source-mappings \
  --query 'EventSourceMappings[*].{UUID:UUID,Source:EventSourceArn,Function:FunctionArn,State:State}' \
  --output table \
  --region us-east-1
---------------------------------------------------------------- | ListEventSourceMappings | +------------+-----------------------+------------------+------+ | UUID | Source | Function |State | +------------+-----------------------+------------------+------+ | a1b2c3d4 | sqs:anihpj-task-queue | anihpj-processor |Enabled| | b2c3d4e5 | dynamodb:anihpj-jobs | anihpj-stream |Enabled| +------------+-----------------------+------------------+------+
# List all mappings from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost total_mappings = 2 print(f"Total mappings: {total_mappings}")
Example 4: Filter Disabled Mappings

# Find disabled mappings
aws lambda list-event-source-mappings \
  --query 'EventSourceMappings[?State==`Disabled`].{UUID:UUID,Function:FunctionArn}' \
  --output table \
  --region us-east-1
---------------------------------------------- | ListEventSourceMappings | +------------+-------------------------------+ | UUID | Function | +------------+-------------------------------+ | x1y2z3 | anihpj-test-processor | +------------+-------------------------------+
# Disabled mappings from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost disabled = 1 print(f"{disabled} disabled mappings found")
Example 5: Export Mapping IDs
#!/bin/bash
# Get UUIDs for automation
aws lambda list-event-source-mappings \
  --function-name anihpj-queue-processor \
  --query 'EventSourceMappings[*].UUID' \
  --output text \
  --region us-east-1
