[ { "EventId": "evt-090", "EventType": "TaskCompleted", "Timestamp": "2024-01-15T10:45:00.000+0000" }, { "EventId": "evt-091", "EventType": "CheckpointCreated", "Timestamp": "2024-01-15T10:46:00.000+0000" } ]
# Recent events from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost events = ['TaskCompleted', 'CheckpointCreated'] print(f"Recent Events") for event in events: print(f"\n- {event}") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Count Event Types

Analyze event distribution.

#!/bin/bash
# Count event types

aws lambda get-durable-execution-history \
    --execution-id exec-xyz789 \
    --query 'Events[].EventType' --output text | \
    tr '\t' '\n' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn
50 TaskCompleted 50 TaskScheduled 10 CheckpointCreated 1 ExecutionStarted
# Count events from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost counts = { 'TaskCompleted': 50, 'TaskScheduled': 50, 'CheckpointCreated': 10, 'ExecutionStarted': 1 } print(f"Event Distribution") for event, count in counts.items(): print(f"\n{count:4d} {event}") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Find Failed Tasks

Filter for task failure events.

#!/bin/bash
# Find failed tasks

aws lambda get-durable-execution-history \
    --execution-id exec-xyz789 \
    --query 'Events[?EventType==`TaskFailed`]' \
    --output table
---------------------------------------------- | GetDurableExecutionHistory | +----------+-------------+-------------------+ | EventId | EventType | Timestamp | +----------+-------------+-------------------+ | evt-055 | TaskFailed | 2024-01-15T10:30 | +----------+-------------+-------------------+
# Find failures from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost failures = [('evt-055', 'TaskFailed', '10:30')] print(f"Failed Tasks") for evt_id, evt_type, time in failures: print(f"\n{evt_id}: {evt_type} at {time}") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Export Timeline

Save execution timeline for analysis.

#!/bin/bash
# Export timeline

EXEC_ID="exec-xyz789"
OUTPUT="execution-timeline.json"

aws lambda get-durable-execution-history \
    --execution-id "$EXEC_ID" \
    --output json > "$OUTPUT"

COUNT=$(jq '.Events | length' "$OUTPUT")
echo "Exported $COUNT events to $OUTPUT"
Exported 111 events to execution-timeline.json
# Export timeline from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost count = 111 print(f"Timeline Exported") print(f"\nEvents: {count}") print(f"File: execution-timeline.json") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

get-durable-execution-state

Returns the current state of a durable Lambda execution. Get checkpoint data and progress for Django Lambda workflows.

Syntax
aws lambda get-durable-execution-state \
    --execution-id <execution-id>
Parameters Description
  • --execution-id (string, required): ID of the durable execution.
Return Value
  • ExecutionId (string): ID of the execution.
  • State (string): Current state (RUNNING, COMPLETED, etc.).
  • StateData (string): JSON-encoded state data from last checkpoint.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Get Current State

Retrieve execution state and progress.

#!/bin/bash
# Get current state

aws lambda get-durable-execution-state \
    --execution-id exec-xyz789 \
    --region us-east-1
{ "ExecutionId": "exec-xyz789", "State": "RUNNING", "StateData": "{\"step\":3,\"processedJobs\":150}" }
# Get state from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost state = { 'execution_id': 'exec-xyz789', 'state': 'RUNNING', 'step': 3, 'processed_jobs': 150 } print(f"Execution State") print(f"\nStep: {state['step']}") print(f"Processed: {state['processed_jobs']}") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nTotal Jobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Parse State Data

Extract and parse the state JSON.

#!/bin/bash
# Parse state data

RESULT=$(aws lambda get-durable-execution-state \
    --execution-id exec-xyz789)

STATE_DATA=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.StateData')
PROCESSED=$(echo "$STATE_DATA" | jq '.processedJobs')

echo "Jobs processed: $PROCESSED"
Jobs processed: 150
# Parse state from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost processed = 150 print(f"Jobs processed: {processed}") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Monitor Progress

Track workflow progress over time.

#!/bin/bash
# Monitor progress

EXEC_ID="exec-xyz789"
TOTAL_JOBS=500

while true; do
    STATE=$(aws lambda get-durable-execution-state \
        --execution-id "$EXEC_ID")
    
    PROCESSED=$(echo "$STATE" | jq -r '.StateData' | jq '.processedJobs')
    STATUS=$(echo "$STATE" | jq -r '.State')
    
    PERCENT=$((PROCESSED * 100 / TOTAL_JOBS))
    echo "Progress: $PROCESSED/$TOTAL_JOBS ($PERCENT%) - $STATUS"
    
    [ "$STATUS" = "COMPLETED" ] && break
    sleep 10
done
Progress: 150/500 (30%) - RUNNING Progress: 300/500 (60%) - RUNNING Progress: 500/500 (100%) - COMPLETED
# Monitor progress from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost progress = [(150, 30), (300, 60), (500, 100)] print(f"Workflow Progress") for processed, percent in progress: print(f"\n{processed}/500 ({percent}%)") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Check for Errors

Examine state for error information.

#!/bin/bash
# Check for errors

RESULT=$(aws lambda get-durable-execution-state \
    --execution-id exec-xyz789)

STATE_DATA=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.StateData')
ERRORS=$(echo "$STATE_DATA" | jq '.errorsCount // 0')

if [ "$ERRORS" -gt 0 ]; then
    echo "⚠️ Execution has $ERRORS errors"
    echo "$STATE_DATA" | jq '.lastError'
else
    echo "✓ No errors"
fi
⚠️ Execution has 3 errors "Connection timeout on job-123"
# Check errors from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost errors = 3 if errors > 0: print(f"⚠️ Execution has {errors} errors") else: print(f"✓ No errors") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Export State for Analysis

Save current state for debugging.

#!/bin/bash
# Export state

EXEC_ID="exec-xyz789"
OUTPUT="execution-state.json"

aws lambda get-durable-execution-state \
    --execution-id "$EXEC_ID" \
    --output json > "$OUTPUT"

echo "State exported to $OUTPUT"
jq '.StateData | fromjson' "$OUTPUT"
State exported to execution-state.json { "step": 3, "processedJobs": 150, "errorsCount": 0 }
# Export state from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost state = {'step': 3, 'processedJobs': 150, 'errorsCount': 0} print(f"State Exported") for key, value in state.items(): print(f"\n{key}: {value}") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

list-durable-executions-by-function

Lists durable executions for a specific Lambda function. Monitor all long-running Django workflows for a given function.

Syntax
aws lambda list-durable-executions-by-function \
    --function-name <function-name> \
    [--status <status>] \
    [--max-items <number>] \
    [--next-token <token>]
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string, required): Function name or ARN.
  • --status (string, optional): Filter by status (RUNNING, COMPLETED, FAILED).
  • --max-items (integer, optional): Maximum results.
  • --next-token (string, optional): Pagination token.
Return Value
  • Executions (array): List of durable executions.
  • Executions[].ExecutionId (string): Execution identifier.
  • Executions[].Status (string): Execution status.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: List All Executions

Get all durable executions for a function.

#!/bin/bash
# List all executions

aws lambda list-durable-executions-by-function \
    --function-name jobpost-workflow \
    --region us-east-1
{ "Executions": [ { "ExecutionId": "exec-xyz789", "Status": "RUNNING", "StartedAt": "2024-01-15T10:00:00.000+0000" }, { "ExecutionId": "exec-abc123", "Status": "COMPLETED", "StartedAt": "2024-01-14T08:00:00.000+0000" } ] }
# List executions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost executions = [ {'id': 'exec-xyz789', 'status': 'RUNNING'}, {'id': 'exec-abc123', 'status': 'COMPLETED'} ] print(f"Durable Executions") for e in executions: print(f"\n{e['id']}: {e['status']}") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Filter Running Only

List only running executions.

#!/bin/bash
# Running only

aws lambda list-durable-executions-by-function \
    --function-name jobpost-workflow \
    --status RUNNING
{ "Executions": [ { "ExecutionId": "exec-xyz789", "Status": "RUNNING" } ] }
# Running from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost running = ['exec-xyz789'] print(f"Running Executions: {len(running)}") for e in running: print(f"\n{e}") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Count by Status

Count executions by status.

#!/bin/bash
# Count by status

for status in RUNNING COMPLETED FAILED; do
    COUNT=$(aws lambda list-durable-executions-by-function \
        --function-name jobpost-workflow \
        --status "$status" \
        --query 'length(Executions)' --output text)
    echo "$status: $COUNT"
done
RUNNING: 2 COMPLETED: 15 FAILED: 1
# Count by status from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost counts = {'RUNNING': 2, 'COMPLETED': 15, 'FAILED': 1} print(f"Execution Counts") for status, count in counts.items(): print(f"\n{status}: {count}") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Export List

Save executions to file.

#!/bin/bash
# Export list

aws lambda list-durable-executions-by-function \
    --function-name jobpost-workflow \
    --output json > executions.json

echo "Exported to executions.json"
Exported to executions.json
# Export from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Executions Exported") print(f"\nFile: executions.json") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Get IDs Only

Extract just execution IDs.

#!/bin/bash
# IDs only

aws lambda list-durable-executions-by-function \
    --function-name jobpost-workflow \
    --query 'Executions[].ExecutionId' \
    --output text
exec-xyz789 exec-abc123 exec-def456
# IDs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost ids = ['exec-xyz789', 'exec-abc123', 'exec-def456'] print(f"Execution IDs") for i in ids: print(f"\n{i}") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

stop-durable-execution

Stops a running durable Lambda execution. Cancel long-running Django workflows that need to be terminated.

Syntax
aws lambda stop-durable-execution \
    --execution-id <execution-id> \
    [--reason <reason>]
Parameters Description
  • --execution-id (string, required): ID of the durable execution.
  • --reason (string, optional): Reason for stopping.
Return Value
  • ExecutionId (string): Stopped execution ID.
  • Status (string): New status (STOPPING).
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Stop Execution

Stop a running execution.

#!/bin/bash
# Stop execution

aws lambda stop-durable-execution \
    --execution-id exec-xyz789 \
    --region us-east-1
{ "ExecutionId": "exec-xyz789", "Status": "STOPPING" }
# Stop execution from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Execution Stopped") print(f"\nID: exec-xyz789") print(f"Status: STOPPING") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Stop with Reason

Provide reason for stopping.

#!/bin/bash
# Stop with reason

aws lambda stop-durable-execution \
    --execution-id exec-xyz789 \
    --reason "Manual termination - timeout exceeded"
{ "ExecutionId": "exec-xyz789", "Status": "STOPPING" }
# With reason from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Execution Stopped") print(f"\nReason: Manual termination") print(f"Status: STOPPING") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Stop All Running

Stop all running executions.

#!/bin/bash
# Stop all running

EXECS=$(aws lambda list-durable-executions-by-function \
    --function-name jobpost-workflow \
    --status RUNNING \
    --query 'Executions[].ExecutionId' --output text)

for exec_id in $EXECS; do
    aws lambda stop-durable-execution \
        --execution-id "$exec_id" \
        --reason "Batch stop"
    echo "Stopped: $exec_id"
done
Stopped: exec-xyz789 Stopped: exec-abc123
# Stop all from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost stopped = ['exec-xyz789', 'exec-abc123'] print(f"Batch Stop") for e in stopped: print(f"\nStopped: {e}") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Verify Stop

Stop and verify status.

#!/bin/bash
# Verify stop

EXEC_ID="exec-xyz789"

aws lambda stop-durable-execution --execution-id "$EXEC_ID"

# Verify
STATUS=$(aws lambda get-durable-execution \
    --execution-id "$EXEC_ID" \
    --query 'Status' --output text)

echo "Verified status: $STATUS"
Verified status: STOPPING
# Verify from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Stop Verified") print(f"\nStatus: STOPPING") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Safe Stop

Check before stopping.

#!/bin/bash
# Safe stop

EXEC_ID="exec-xyz789"

STATUS=$(aws lambda get-durable-execution \
    --execution-id "$EXEC_ID" \
    --query 'Status' --output text 2>/dev/null)

if [ "$STATUS" = "RUNNING" ]; then
    aws lambda stop-durable-execution --execution-id "$EXEC_ID"
    echo "Stopped running execution"
else
    echo "Execution not running: $STATUS"
fi
Stopped running execution
# Safe stop from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Safe Stop") print(f"\nStatus: Running - Stopped") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

send-durable-execution-callback-success

Sends a success callback to resume a waiting durable execution. Complete async tasks in Django workflows.

Syntax
aws lambda send-durable-execution-callback-success \
    --task-token <token> \
    --output <output-data>
Parameters Description
  • --task-token (string, required): Task token from the waiting execution.
  • --output (string, required): Output data to return.
Return Value
  • HTTP 200: No content returned on success.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Send Success Callback

Resume waiting execution with success.

#!/bin/bash
# Send success

aws lambda send-durable-execution-callback-success \
    --task-token "token-abc123xyz" \
    --output '{"status": "approved", "jobId": 12345}' \
    --region us-east-1

echo "Success callback sent"
Success callback sent
# Success callback from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Success Callback Sent") print(f"\nStatus: approved") print(f"Job ID: 12345") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: With Job Data

Return processed job data.

#!/bin/bash
# With job data

OUTPUT=$(cat << 'EOF'
{"processedJobs": 100, "errors": 0, "duration": "5m"}
EOF
)

aws lambda send-durable-execution-callback-success \
    --task-token "token-abc123xyz" \
    --output "$OUTPUT"

echo "Callback with data sent"
Callback with data sent
# With data from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Callback Sent") print(f"\nProcessed: 100 jobs") print(f"Errors: 0") print(f"Duration: 5m") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: From File

Load output from file.

#!/bin/bash
# From file

aws lambda send-durable-execution-callback-success \
    --task-token "token-abc123xyz" \
    --output file://result.json

echo "Callback sent from file"
Callback sent from file
# From file from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Callback from File") print(f"\nSource: result.json") print(f"Status: Sent") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Approval Workflow

Complete approval task.

#!/bin/bash
# Approval workflow

TOKEN="$1"
APPROVER="$2"

aws lambda send-durable-execution-callback-success \
    --task-token "$TOKEN" \
    --output "{\"approved\": true, \"approver\": \"$APPROVER\"}"

echo "Approval completed by $APPROVER"
Approval completed by admin@example.com
# Approval from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Approval Workflow") print(f"\nApproved: True") print(f"Approver: admin@example.com") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Batch Callbacks

Send multiple success callbacks.

#!/bin/bash
# Batch callbacks

TOKENS=("token-1" "token-2" "token-3")

for token in "${TOKENS[@]}"; do
    aws lambda send-durable-execution-callback-success \
        --task-token "$token" \
        --output '{"status": "complete"}'
    echo "Callback sent: $token"
done
Callback sent: token-1 Callback sent: token-2 Callback sent: token-3
# Batch callbacks from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost tokens = ['token-1', 'token-2', 'token-3'] print(f"Batch Callbacks") for t in tokens: print(f"\nSent: {t}") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

send-durable-execution-callback-failure

Sends a failure callback to a waiting durable execution. Signal errors in async Django workflow tasks.

Syntax
aws lambda send-durable-execution-callback-failure \
    --task-token <token> \
    --error <error-code> \
    --cause <error-message>
Parameters Description
  • --task-token (string, required): Task token from the waiting execution.
  • --error (string, required): Error code.
  • --cause (string, optional): Error message or cause.
Return Value
  • HTTP 200: No content returned on success.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Send Failure Callback

Signal task failure.

#!/bin/bash
# Send failure

aws lambda send-durable-execution-callback-failure \
    --task-token "token-abc123xyz" \
    --error "ValidationError" \
    --cause "Invalid job data format" \
    --region us-east-1

echo "Failure callback sent"
Failure callback sent
# Failure callback from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Failure Callback Sent") print(f"\nError: ValidationError") print(f"Cause: Invalid job data format") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Timeout Failure

Report timeout error.

#!/bin/bash
# Timeout failure

aws lambda send-durable-execution-callback-failure \
    --task-token "token-abc123xyz" \
    --error "TimeoutError" \
    --cause "Task exceeded 300 second limit"

echo "Timeout failure reported"
Timeout failure reported
# Timeout from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Timeout Failure") print(f"\nError: TimeoutError") print(f"Limit: 300 seconds") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Database Error

Report database failure.

#!/bin/bash
# Database error

aws lambda send-durable-execution-callback-failure \
    --task-token "token-abc123xyz" \
    --error "DatabaseError" \
    --cause "Connection to PostgreSQL failed"

echo "Database error reported"
Database error reported
# Database error from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Database Error") print(f"\nError: DatabaseError") print(f"Cause: Connection failed") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Conditional Failure

Report failure based on condition.

#!/bin/bash
# Conditional failure

TOKEN="token-abc123xyz"
VALID=false

if [ "$VALID" = "false" ]; then
    aws lambda send-durable-execution-callback-failure \
        --task-token "$TOKEN" \
        --error "ValidationFailed" \
        --cause "Job data validation failed"
    echo "Validation failure sent"
fi
Validation failure sent
# Conditional from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Conditional Failure") print(f"\nValidation: Failed") print(f"Callback: Sent") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Error with Details

Include detailed error information.

#!/bin/bash
# Detailed error

ERROR_DETAILS=$(cat << 'EOF'
Job processing failed at step 3. Records processed: 50/100. 
Error on record ID 67: Missing required field 'title'.
EOF
)

aws lambda send-durable-execution-callback-failure \
    --task-token "token-abc123xyz" \
    --error "ProcessingError" \
    --cause "$ERROR_DETAILS"

echo "Detailed failure sent"
Detailed failure sent
# Detailed from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Detailed Error") print(f"\nStep: 3") print(f"Processed: 50/100") print(f"Error: Missing field") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

send-durable-execution-callback-heartbeat

Sends a heartbeat to keep a durable execution task alive. Prevent timeout for long-running Django async tasks.

Syntax
aws lambda send-durable-execution-callback-heartbeat \
    --task-token <token>
Parameters Description
  • --task-token (string, required): Task token from the waiting execution.
Return Value
  • HTTP 200: No content returned on success.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Send Heartbeat

Keep task alive.

#!/bin/bash
# Send heartbeat

aws lambda send-durable-execution-callback-heartbeat \
    --task-token "token-abc123xyz" \
    --region us-east-1

echo "Heartbeat sent"
Heartbeat sent
# Heartbeat from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Heartbeat Sent") print(f"\nToken: token-abc123xyz") print(f"Status: Active") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Periodic Heartbeats

Send heartbeats at intervals.

#!/bin/bash
# Periodic heartbeats

TOKEN="token-abc123xyz"
INTERVAL=60

while true; do
    aws lambda send-durable-execution-callback-heartbeat \
        --task-token "$TOKEN"
    echo "Heartbeat sent at $(date)"
    sleep $INTERVAL
done
Heartbeat sent at Mon Jan 15 10:00:00 UTC 2024 Heartbeat sent at Mon Jan 15 10:01:00 UTC 2024 ...
# Periodic from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost import datetime print(f"Periodic Heartbeats") print(f"\nInterval: 60 seconds") print(f"Last: {datetime.datetime.now()}") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Heartbeat During Processing

Send heartbeats while processing.

#!/bin/bash
# During processing

TOKEN="token-abc123xyz"
RECORDS=(1 2 3 4 5)

for record in "${RECORDS[@]}"; do
    # Process record
    echo "Processing record $record"
    sleep 30
    
    # Send heartbeat
    aws lambda send-durable-execution-callback-heartbeat \
        --task-token "$TOKEN"
    echo "Heartbeat sent after record $record"
done
Processing record 1 Heartbeat sent after record 1 Processing record 2 Heartbeat sent after record 2 ...
# During processing from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost records = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] print(f"Processing with Heartbeats") for r in records: print(f"\nRecord {r}: Processed") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Background Heartbeat

Run heartbeats in background.

#!/bin/bash
# Background heartbeat

TOKEN="token-abc123xyz"

# Start heartbeat in background
(
    while true; do
        aws lambda send-durable-execution-callback-heartbeat \
            --task-token "$TOKEN" 2>/dev/null
        sleep 60
    done
) &
HEARTBEAT_PID=$!

echo "Heartbeat running in background (PID: $HEARTBEAT_PID)"

# Do main work here...

# When done, stop heartbeat
kill $HEARTBEAT_PID 2>/dev/null
Heartbeat running in background (PID: 12345)
# Background from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Background Heartbeat") print(f"\nPID: 12345") print(f"Status: Running") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Heartbeat with Error Handling

Handle heartbeat failures.

#!/bin/bash
# With error handling

TOKEN="token-abc123xyz"

send_heartbeat() {
    if aws lambda send-durable-execution-callback-heartbeat \
        --task-token "$TOKEN" 2>/dev/null; then
        echo "Heartbeat OK"
        return 0
    else
        echo "Heartbeat failed - task may have completed or expired"
        return 1
    fi
}

# Try to send heartbeat
if ! send_heartbeat; then
    echo "Exiting due to heartbeat failure"
    exit 1
fi
Heartbeat OK
# Error handling from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Heartbeat with Handling") print(f"\nStatus: OK") print(f"Errors: None") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

AWS Elastic Beanstalk

Platform-as-a-Service for deploying Django applications with auto-scaling

📋 Elastic Beanstalk Overview

AWS Elastic Beanstalk is a fully managed Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS) that automatically handles deployment, capacity provisioning, load balancing, auto-scaling, and health monitoring for your anihpj Django application. Simply upload your code and Beanstalk deploys it with best-practice configurations for JobPost, Skills, User, Author, and Location data handling.

Core Elastic Beanstalk Operations for Django (48 Commands)

abort-environment-update - Cancel update
apply-environment-managed-action - Apply managed action
associate-environment-operations-role - Associate ops role
check-dns-availability - Check CNAME availability
compose-environments - Compose linked envs
create-application - Create new EB application
create-application-version - Upload new version
create-configuration-template - Create config template
create-environment - Deploy environment
create-platform-version - Create custom platform
create-storage-location - Create S3 storage
delete-application - Delete application
delete-application-version - Delete version
delete-configuration-template - Delete config template
delete-environment-configuration - Delete env config
delete-platform-version - Delete platform version
describe-account-attributes - Get account limits
describe-application-versions - List versions
describe-applications - List applications
describe-configuration-options - List config options
describe-configuration-settings - Get config settings
describe-environment-health - Health monitoring
describe-environment-managed-action-history - Action history
describe-environment-managed-actions - List managed actions
describe-environment-resources - List env resources
describe-environments - Get environment status
describe-events - List EB events
describe-instances-health - Instance health
describe-platform-version - Get platform details
disassociate-environment-operations-role - Remove ops role
list-available-solution-stacks - List stacks
list-platform-branches - List platform branches
list-platform-versions - List platforms
list-tags-for-resource - List tags
rebuild-environment - Rebuild environment
request-environment-info - Request logs
restart-app-server - Restart app server
retrieve-environment-info - Get requested info
swap-environment-cnames - Blue/green deployment
terminate-environment - Remove environment
update-application - Update application
update-application-resource-lifecycle - Update lifecycle
update-application-version - Update version
update-configuration-template - Update config template
update-environment - Update configuration
update-tags-for-resource - Update tags
validate-configuration-settings - Validate config
wait - Wait for condition

create-application

Creates a new Elastic Beanstalk application container. An application is a logical collection of environments, versions, and configurations for your Django project.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk create-application \
  --application-name <value> \
  [--description <value>] \
  [--resource-lifecycle-config <value>] \
  [--tags <value>] \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string, required): Application name (1-100 chars).
  • --description (string, optional): Description of the application.
  • --resource-lifecycle-config (structure, optional): Version lifecycle settings.
  • --tags (list, optional): Key-value tags for the application.
Return Value
  • Application (object): Created application details.
  • Application.ApplicationName (string): Name of the application.
  • Application.DateCreated (timestamp): Creation date.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Create Basic Application

Create a new EB application.

#!/bin/bash
# Create basic application

aws elasticbeanstalk create-application \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --description "Django JobPost Application" \
    --region us-east-1
{ "Application": { "ApplicationName": "jobpost-django", "Description": "Django JobPost Application", "DateCreated": "2024-01-15T10:00:00.000Z", "DateUpdated": "2024-01-15T10:00:00.000Z" } }
# Create application from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Application Created") print(f"\nName: jobpost-django") print(f"Description: Django JobPost Application") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: With Lifecycle Config

Create with version lifecycle.

#!/bin/bash
# With lifecycle

aws elasticbeanstalk create-application \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --description "Django JobPost Application" \
    --resource-lifecycle-config '{
        "ServiceRole": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/eb-service",
        "VersionLifecycleConfig": {
            "MaxCountRule": {
                "Enabled": true,
                "MaxCount": 10,
                "DeleteSourceFromS3": true
            }
        }
    }'
{ "Application": { "ApplicationName": "jobpost-django", "ResourceLifecycleConfig": { "VersionLifecycleConfig": { "MaxCountRule": { "Enabled": true, "MaxCount": 10 } } } } }
# Lifecycle config from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Application with Lifecycle") print(f"\nMax versions: 10") print(f"Delete from S3: True") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: With Tags

Create with resource tags.

#!/bin/bash
# With tags

aws elasticbeanstalk create-application \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --tags Key=Environment,Value=Production \
           Key=Project,Value=JobPost \
           Key=Team,Value=Backend
{ "Application": { "ApplicationName": "jobpost-django", "DateCreated": "2024-01-15T10:00:00.000Z" } }
# With tags from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost tags = { 'Environment': 'Production', 'Project': 'JobPost', 'Team': 'Backend' } print(f"Application with Tags") for k, v in tags.items(): print(f"\n{k}: {v}") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Multiple Applications

Create apps for different environments.

#!/bin/bash
# Multiple apps

for env in dev staging prod; do
    aws elasticbeanstalk create-application \
        --application-name "jobpost-django-$env" \
        --description "JobPost $env environment"
    echo "Created: jobpost-django-$env"
done
Created: jobpost-django-dev Created: jobpost-django-staging Created: jobpost-django-prod
# Multiple apps from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost envs = ['dev', 'staging', 'prod'] print(f"Multiple Applications") for env in envs: print(f"\nCreated: jobpost-django-{env}") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Verify Creation

Create and verify application.

#!/bin/bash
# Verify creation

APP_NAME="jobpost-django"

aws elasticbeanstalk create-application \
    --application-name "$APP_NAME"

# Verify
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-applications \
    --application-names "$APP_NAME" \
    --query 'Applications[0].ApplicationName'

echo "Application verified"
"jobpost-django" Application verified
# Verify from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Creation Verified") print(f"\nApplication: jobpost-django") print(f"Status: Created") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")
Return Value
{
    "Application": {
        "ApplicationName": "anihpj-jobpost",
        "ApplicationArn": "arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1:123456789012:application/anihpj-jobpost",
        "DateCreated": "2026-01-13T10:00:00.000Z",
        "DateUpdated": "2026-01-13T10:00:00.000Z"
    }
}
Example 1: Create Django Application
#!/bin/bash
# Create Elastic Beanstalk application for Django
aws elasticbeanstalk create-application \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --description "Django job posting application with Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost models" \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Application": { "ApplicationName": "anihpj-jobpost", "ApplicationArn": "arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1:123456789012:application/anihpj-jobpost", "Description": "Django job posting application with Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost models", "DateCreated": "2026-01-13T10:00:00.000Z", "DateUpdated": "2026-01-13T10:00:00.000Z" } }
# Create EB application from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost app_name = "anihpj-jobpost" print(f"EB Application created: {app_name}")
Example 2: Create with Tags
#!/bin/bash
# Create application with cost allocation tags
aws elasticbeanstalk create-application \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --description "Production Django app" \
  --tags Key=Project,Value=anihpj Key=Environment,Value=production Key=Team,Value=backend \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Application": { "ApplicationName": "anihpj-jobpost", "ApplicationArn": "arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1:123456789012:application/anihpj-jobpost", "DateCreated": "2026-01-13T10:00:00.000Z" } }
# Create with tags from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost tags = {'Project': 'anihpj', 'Team': 'backend'} print(f"Application tagged: {tags}")
Example 3: Create with Lifecycle Policy
#!/bin/bash
# Create with version lifecycle to auto-delete old versions
aws elasticbeanstalk create-application \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --resource-lifecycle-config '{
    "ServiceRole": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/aws-elasticbeanstalk-service-role",
    "VersionLifecycleConfig": {
      "MaxCountRule": {
        "Enabled": true,
        "MaxCount": 10,
        "DeleteSourceFromS3": true
      }
    }
  }' \
  --region us-east-1
{ "Application": { "ApplicationName": "anihpj-jobpost", "ResourceLifecycleConfig": { "VersionLifecycleConfig": { "MaxCountRule": { "Enabled": true, "MaxCount": 10 } } } } }
# Lifecycle policy from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Keep only 10 versions, auto-delete old")
Example 4: Create Staging Application
#!/bin/bash
# Create separate staging application
aws elasticbeanstalk create-application \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost-staging \
  --description "Staging environment for testing" \
  --tags Key=Environment,Value=staging \
  --region us-east-1

echo "✓ Staging application created"
{ "Application": { "ApplicationName": "anihpj-jobpost-staging", "Description": "Staging environment for testing" } } ✓ Staging application created
# Staging app from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Staging application for testing")
Example 5: Create and Verify
#!/bin/bash
# Create and immediately verify
APP_NAME="anihpj-jobpost"

aws elasticbeanstalk create-application \
  --application-name $APP_NAME \
  --description "Django jobpost app" \
  --region us-east-1

# Verify creation
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-applications \
  --application-names $APP_NAME \
  --query 'Applications[0].ApplicationName' \
  --output text
anihpj-jobpost
# Create and verify from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost app = "anihpj-jobpost" print(f"Verified: {app}")

describe-applications

Returns descriptions of existing Elastic Beanstalk applications. Use to list all applications or get details about specific ones for your Django project.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-applications \
  [--application-names <value>...] \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --application-names (list, optional): Specific application names to describe.
  • --region (string, optional): AWS region.
Return Value
  • Applications (array): List of application objects.
  • Applications[].ApplicationName (string): Application name.
  • Applications[].DateCreated (timestamp): Creation date.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: List All Applications

Get all EB applications.

#!/bin/bash
# List all applications

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-applications \
    --region us-east-1
{ "Applications": [ { "ApplicationName": "jobpost-django", "Description": "Django JobPost Application", "DateCreated": "2024-01-15T10:00:00.000Z" }, { "ApplicationName": "jobpost-api", "Description": "JobPost API Service", "DateCreated": "2024-01-14T08:00:00.000Z" } ] }
# List applications from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost apps = ['jobpost-django', 'jobpost-api'] print(f"EB Applications") for app in apps: print(f"\n{app}") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Get Specific Application

Describe one application.

#!/bin/bash
# Specific application

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-applications \
    --application-names jobpost-django
{ "Applications": [ { "ApplicationName": "jobpost-django", "ApplicationArn": "arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1:123456789012:application/jobpost-django", "Description": "Django JobPost Application" } ] }
# Specific app from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Application Details") print(f"\nName: jobpost-django") print(f"Description: Django JobPost Application") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Get Names Only

Extract just application names.

#!/bin/bash
# Names only

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-applications \
    --query 'Applications[].ApplicationName' \
    --output table
------------------- | ApplicationName | +------------------+ | jobpost-django | | jobpost-api | +------------------+
# Names only from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost names = ['jobpost-django', 'jobpost-api'] print(f"Application Names") for n in names: print(f"\n{n}") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Count Applications

Get total application count.

#!/bin/bash
# Count apps

COUNT=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-applications \
    --query 'length(Applications)' \
    --output text)

echo "Total applications: $COUNT"
Total applications: 3
# Count from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost count = 3 print(f"Total Applications: {count}") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Export List

Save applications to file.

#!/bin/bash
# Export list

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-applications \
    --output json > eb-applications.json

echo "Applications exported to eb-applications.json"
Applications exported to eb-applications.json
# Export from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Applications Exported") print(f"\nFile: eb-applications.json") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

update-application

Updates the description and resource lifecycle settings for an existing application. Modify Django app metadata or version cleanup policies.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk update-application \
  --application-name <value> \
  [--description <value>] \
  [--resource-lifecycle-config <value>] \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string, required): Name of application to update.
  • --description (string, optional): New description.
  • --resource-lifecycle-config (structure, optional): Version lifecycle settings.
Return Value
  • Application (object): Updated application details.
  • Application.ApplicationName (string): Application name.
  • Application.DateUpdated (timestamp): Update timestamp.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Update Description

Change application description.

#!/bin/bash
# Update description

aws elasticbeanstalk update-application \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --description "Django JobPost Application v2.0" \
    --region us-east-1
{ "Application": { "ApplicationName": "jobpost-django", "Description": "Django JobPost Application v2.0", "DateUpdated": "2024-01-15T12:00:00.000Z" } }
# Update description from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Application Updated") print(f"\nDescription: Django JobPost Application v2.0") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Set Max Versions

Configure version lifecycle.

#!/bin/bash
# Set max versions

aws elasticbeanstalk update-application \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --resource-lifecycle-config '{
        "ServiceRole": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/eb-service",
        "VersionLifecycleConfig": {
            "MaxCountRule": {
                "Enabled": true,
                "MaxCount": 20,
                "DeleteSourceFromS3": true
            }
        }
    }'
{ "Application": { "ApplicationName": "jobpost-django", "ResourceLifecycleConfig": { "VersionLifecycleConfig": { "MaxCountRule": {"Enabled": true, "MaxCount": 20} } } } }
# Max versions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Lifecycle Updated") print(f"\nMax Versions: 20") print(f"Delete from S3: True") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Age-Based Cleanup

Delete old versions automatically.

#!/bin/bash
# Age-based cleanup

aws elasticbeanstalk update-application \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --resource-lifecycle-config '{
        "ServiceRole": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/eb-service",
        "VersionLifecycleConfig": {
            "MaxAgeRule": {
                "Enabled": true,
                "MaxAgeInDays": 180,
                "DeleteSourceFromS3": true
            }
        }
    }'
{ "Application": { "ResourceLifecycleConfig": { "VersionLifecycleConfig": { "MaxAgeRule": {"Enabled": true, "MaxAgeInDays": 180} } } } }
# Age cleanup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Age Cleanup Configured") print(f"\nMax Age: 180 days") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Batch Update

Update multiple applications.

#!/bin/bash
# Batch update

for app in jobpost-django jobpost-api; do
    aws elasticbeanstalk update-application \
        --application-name "$app" \
        --description "Updated $(date +%Y-%m-%d)"
    echo "Updated: $app"
done
Updated: jobpost-django Updated: jobpost-api
# Batch update from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost apps = ['jobpost-django', 'jobpost-api'] print(f"Batch Update") for app in apps: print(f"\nUpdated: {app}") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Verify Update

Update and verify changes.

#!/bin/bash
# Verify update

APP="jobpost-django"

aws elasticbeanstalk update-application \
    --application-name "$APP" \
    --description "Production v3.0"

# Verify
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-applications \
    --application-names "$APP" \
    --query 'Applications[0].Description'

echo "Update verified"
"Production v3.0" Update verified
# Verify from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Update Verified") print(f"\nDescription: Production v3.0") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")
# Age-based cleanup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Delete versions older than 180 days")
Example 4: Disable Lifecycle
#!/bin/bash
# Disable automatic version cleanup
aws elasticbeanstalk update-application \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --resource-lifecycle-config '{
    "VersionLifecycleConfig": {
      "MaxCountRule": {"Enabled": false},
      "MaxAgeRule": {"Enabled": false}
    }
  }'

echo "✓ Lifecycle rules disabled"
✓ Lifecycle rules disabled
# Disable lifecycle from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Lifecycle rules disabled")
Example 5: Update Both Settings
#!/bin/bash
# Update description and lifecycle together
aws elasticbeanstalk update-application \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --description "Django jobpost - Updated $(date +%Y-%m-%d)" \
  --resource-lifecycle-config '{
    "ServiceRole": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/aws-elasticbeanstalk-service-role",
    "VersionLifecycleConfig": {
      "MaxCountRule": {"Enabled": true, "MaxCount": 15}
    }
  }'
{ "Application": { "ApplicationName": "anihpj-jobpost", "Description": "Django jobpost - Updated 2026-01-13" } }
# Update both from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Description and lifecycle updated")

delete-application

Deletes the specified application along with all associated versions and configurations. Cannot delete if environments are running unless forced.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk delete-application \
  --application-name <value> \
  [--terminate-env-by-force] \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string, required): Name of application to delete.
  • --terminate-env-by-force (boolean, optional): Force terminate running environments.
Return Value
  • HTTP 200: No content returned on successful deletion.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Delete Application

Remove application safely.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete application

aws elasticbeanstalk delete-application \
    --application-name jobpost-old \
    --region us-east-1

echo "Application deleted"
Application deleted
# Delete app from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Application Deleted") print(f"\nName: jobpost-old") print(f"Status: Removed") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Force Delete

Force delete with running environments.

#!/bin/bash
# Force delete

aws elasticbeanstalk delete-application \
    --application-name jobpost-test \
    --terminate-env-by-force

echo "App and environments terminated"
App and environments terminated
# Force delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Forced Deletion") print(f"\nEnvironments: Terminated") print(f"Application: Deleted") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Safe Delete

Check environments before deleting.

#!/bin/bash
# Safe delete

APP="jobpost-staging"

ENVS=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --application-name "$APP" \
    --query 'Environments[?Status!=`Terminated`].EnvironmentName' \
    --output text)

if [ -z "$ENVS" ]; then
    aws elasticbeanstalk delete-application --application-name "$APP"
    echo "Deleted safely"
else
    echo "Active environments: $ENVS"
fi
Deleted safely
# Safe delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Safe Delete") print(f"\nNo active environments") print(f"Status: Deleted") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Batch Delete

Delete multiple old applications.

#!/bin/bash
# Batch delete

for app in jobpost-old jobpost-deprecated jobpost-test; do
    aws elasticbeanstalk delete-application \
        --application-name "$app" 2>/dev/null && \
        echo "Deleted: $app" || echo "Not found: $app"
done
Deleted: jobpost-old Deleted: jobpost-deprecated Not found: jobpost-test
# Batch delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost deleted = ['jobpost-old', 'jobpost-deprecated'] print(f"Batch Delete") for app in deleted: print(f"\nDeleted: {app}") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Confirm Delete

Prompt before deletion.

#!/bin/bash
# Confirm delete

APP="jobpost-staging"

read -p "Delete $APP? (y/n): " confirm
if [ "$confirm" = "y" ]; then
    aws elasticbeanstalk delete-application \
        --application-name "$APP" \
        --terminate-env-by-force
    echo "Deleted: $APP"
else
    echo "Cancelled"
fi
Delete jobpost-staging? (y/n): y Deleted: jobpost-staging
# Confirm delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Deletion Confirmed") print(f"\nApplication: jobpost-staging") print(f"Status: Deleted") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

create-application-version

Creates a new application version from source bundle in S3. Each version represents a deployable iteration of your Django code.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk create-application-version \
  --application-name <value> \
  --version-label <value> \
  [--description <value>] \
  [--source-bundle S3Bucket=<bucket>,S3Key=<key>] \
  [--auto-create-application] \
  [--process] \
  [--tags <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string, required): Application name.
  • --version-label (string, required): Unique version label.
  • --source-bundle (structure, optional): S3 location of source ZIP.
  • --description (string, optional): Version description.
  • --auto-create-application (boolean, optional): Create app if not exists.
  • --process (boolean, optional): Pre-process and validate.
Return Value
  • ApplicationVersion (object): Created version details.
  • ApplicationVersion.VersionLabel (string): Version identifier.
  • ApplicationVersion.Status (string): Version status.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Create Version from S3

Create version from S3 bundle.

#!/bin/bash
# Create version from S3

aws elasticbeanstalk create-application-version \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --version-label v2.1.0 \
    --description "Added JobPost filtering features" \
    --source-bundle S3Bucket=jobpost-deployments,S3Key=releases/v2.1.0.zip \
    --region us-east-1
{ "ApplicationVersion": { "ApplicationName": "jobpost-django", "VersionLabel": "v2.1.0", "Description": "Added JobPost filtering features", "DateCreated": "2024-01-15T10:00:00.000Z", "Status": "Unprocessed" } }
# Create version from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Version Created") print(f"\nLabel: v2.1.0") print(f"Status: Unprocessed") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Upload and Create

Package and upload Django app.

#!/bin/bash
# Upload and create

VERSION="v$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)"
BUCKET="jobpost-deployments"

# Create package
zip -r deploy.zip . -x "*.git*" -x "__pycache__/*"

# Upload to S3
aws s3 cp deploy.zip "s3://$BUCKET/releases/$VERSION.zip"

# Create version
aws elasticbeanstalk create-application-version \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --version-label "$VERSION" \
    --source-bundle "S3Bucket=$BUCKET,S3Key=releases/$VERSION.zip"

echo "Created version: $VERSION"
upload: deploy.zip to s3://jobpost-deployments/releases/v20240115-100000.zip { "ApplicationVersion": { "VersionLabel": "v20240115-100000", "Status": "Unprocessed" } } Created version: v20240115-100000
# Upload and create from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Upload and Create") print(f"\nPackage: deploy.zip") print(f"Version: v20240115-100000") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: With Auto-Create

Create app and version together.

#!/bin/bash
# With auto-create

aws elasticbeanstalk create-application-version \
    --application-name jobpost-new \
    --version-label v1.0.0 \
    --source-bundle S3Bucket=jobpost-deployments,S3Key=releases/v1.0.0.zip \
    --auto-create-application

echo "Application and version created"
{ "ApplicationVersion": { "ApplicationName": "jobpost-new", "VersionLabel": "v1.0.0", "Status": "Unprocessed" } } Application and version created
# Auto-create from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Auto-Create") print(f"\nApp: jobpost-new") print(f"Version: v1.0.0") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: With Processing

Pre-process and validate version.

#!/bin/bash
# With processing

aws elasticbeanstalk create-application-version \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --version-label v2.2.0 \
    --source-bundle S3Bucket=jobpost-deployments,S3Key=releases/v2.2.0.zip \
    --process

echo "Version created and processing"
{ "ApplicationVersion": { "VersionLabel": "v2.2.0", "Status": "Processing" } } Version created and processing
# Processing from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Version Processing") print(f"\nLabel: v2.2.0") print(f"Status: Processing") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: With Tags

Create version with tags.

#!/bin/bash
# With tags

aws elasticbeanstalk create-application-version \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --version-label v2.3.0 \
    --source-bundle S3Bucket=jobpost-deployments,S3Key=releases/v2.3.0.zip \
    --tags Key=Release,Value=Stable Key=Sprint,Value=23

echo "Tagged version created"
{ "ApplicationVersion": { "VersionLabel": "v2.3.0", "Status": "Unprocessed" } } Tagged version created
# With tags from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost tags = {'Release': 'Stable', 'Sprint': '23'} print(f"Tagged Version") for k, v in tags.items(): print(f"\n{k}: {v}") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")
# Upload to S3 aws s3 cp deploy.zip s3://$BUCKET/releases/$VERSION.zip # Create version aws elasticbeanstalk create-application-version \ --application-name anihpj-jobpost \ --version-label $VERSION \ --source-bundle S3Bucket=$BUCKET,S3Key=releases/$VERSION.zip \ --process echo "✓ Version $VERSION created"
✓ Version v20260113-100000 created
# Upload and create from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Packaged and uploaded to S3")
Example 3: Git-Based Versioning
#!/bin/bash
# Use git commit as version label
COMMIT=$(git rev-parse --short HEAD)
VERSION="git-$COMMIT"

aws elasticbeanstalk create-application-version \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --version-label $VERSION \
  --description "Commit: $(git log -1 --pretty=%B)" \
  --source-bundle S3Bucket=anihpj-deployments,S3Key=releases/$VERSION.zip

echo "✓ Created version for commit $COMMIT"
✓ Created version for commit abc1234
# Git versioning from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost commit = "abc1234" print(f"Version from git: {commit}")
Example 4: Auto-Create Application
#!/bin/bash
# Create app and first version in one command
aws elasticbeanstalk create-application-version \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost-new \
  --version-label v1.0.0 \
  --auto-create-application \
  --source-bundle S3Bucket=anihpj-deployments,S3Key=releases/v1.0.0.zip

echo "✓ App and version created"
✓ App and version created
# Auto-create from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"App auto-created with v1.0.0")
Example 5: Create with Tags
#!/bin/bash
# Create version with deployment tags
aws elasticbeanstalk create-application-version \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --version-label v2.2.0 \
  --description "Production release" \
  --source-bundle S3Bucket=anihpj-deployments,S3Key=releases/v2.2.0.zip \
  --tags Key=ReleaseType,Value=production Key=DeployedBy,Value=CI

echo "✓ Tagged version created"
✓ Tagged version created
# Version with tags from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost tags = {'ReleaseType': 'production'} print(f"Version tagged: {tags}")

describe-application-versions

Returns descriptions of application versions. List all versions or filter by labels to track Django deployment history.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-application-versions \
  [--application-name <value>] \
  [--version-labels <value>...] \
  [--max-records <value>] \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string, optional): Filter by application name.
  • --version-labels (list, optional): Specific version labels.
  • --max-records (integer, optional): Max results (1-1000).
Return Value
  • ApplicationVersions (array): List of version objects.
  • ApplicationVersions[].VersionLabel (string): Version identifier.
  • ApplicationVersions[].Status (string): Version status.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: List All Versions

Get all application versions.

#!/bin/bash
# List all versions

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-application-versions \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --region us-east-1
{ "ApplicationVersions": [ {"VersionLabel": "v2.1.0", "Status": "Processed", "DateCreated": "2024-01-15T10:00:00Z"}, {"VersionLabel": "v2.0.0", "Status": "Processed", "DateCreated": "2024-01-10T10:00:00Z"}, {"VersionLabel": "v1.0.0", "Status": "Processed", "DateCreated": "2024-01-01T10:00:00Z"} ] }
# List versions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost versions = ['v2.1.0', 'v2.0.0', 'v1.0.0'] print(f"Application Versions") for v in versions: print(f"\n{v}") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Get Specific Version

Describe one version.

#!/bin/bash
# Specific version

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-application-versions \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --version-labels v2.1.0
{ "ApplicationVersions": [ { "VersionLabel": "v2.1.0", "Description": "Added JobPost filtering", "Status": "Processed" } ] }
# Specific version from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Version Details") print(f"\nLabel: v2.1.0") print(f"Status: Processed") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: List Labels Only

Extract just version labels.

#!/bin/bash
# Labels only

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-application-versions \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --query 'ApplicationVersions[].VersionLabel' \
    --output table
--------------- | VersionLabel | +--------------+ | v2.1.0 | | v2.0.0 | | v1.0.0 | +--------------+
# Labels only from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost labels = ['v2.1.0', 'v2.0.0', 'v1.0.0'] print(f"Version Labels") for l in labels: print(f"\n{l}") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Count Versions

Get total version count.

#!/bin/bash
# Count versions

COUNT=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-application-versions \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --query 'length(ApplicationVersions)' \
    --output text)

echo "Total versions: $COUNT"
Total versions: 5
# Count from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost count = 5 print(f"Total Versions: {count}") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Latest Version

Get most recent version.

#!/bin/bash
# Latest version

LATEST=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-application-versions \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --max-records 1 \
    --query 'ApplicationVersions[0].VersionLabel' \
    --output text)

echo "Latest version: $LATEST"
Latest version: v2.1.0
# Latest from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Latest Version") print(f"\nLabel: v2.1.0") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")
| VersionLabel | ---------------- | v2.1.0 | | v2.0.0 | | v1.0.0 | ----------------
# Version labels from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost labels = ['v2.1.0', 'v2.0.0', 'v1.0.0'] print(f"Labels: {labels}")
Example 4: Get Latest Version
#!/bin/bash
# Get most recent version
LATEST=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-application-versions \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --max-records 1 \
  --query 'ApplicationVersions[0].VersionLabel' \
  --output text)

echo "Latest version: $LATEST"
Latest version: v2.1.0
# Latest version from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost latest = "v2.1.0" print(f"Latest: {latest}")
Example 5: Check Version Status
#!/bin/bash
# Check processing status
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-application-versions \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
  --version-labels v2.1.0 \
  --query 'ApplicationVersions[0].Status' \
  --output text
Processed
# Version status from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost status = "Processed" print(f"Status: {status}")

update-application-version

Updates the description for an existing application version. Add release notes or mark Django versions.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk update-application-version \
  --application-name <value> \
  --version-label <value> \
  [--description <value>] \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string, required): Application name.
  • --version-label (string, required): Version to update.
  • --description (string, optional): New description.
Return Value
  • ApplicationVersion (object): Updated version details.
  • ApplicationVersion.VersionLabel (string): Version identifier.
  • ApplicationVersion.DateUpdated (timestamp): Update time.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Update Description

Change version description.

#!/bin/bash
# Update description

aws elasticbeanstalk update-application-version \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --version-label v2.1.0 \
    --description "Production release - JobPost filtering + Skills autocomplete" \
    --region us-east-1
{ "ApplicationVersion": { "VersionLabel": "v2.1.0", "Description": "Production release - JobPost filtering + Skills autocomplete", "DateUpdated": "2024-01-15T12:00:00.000Z" } }
# Update description from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Version Updated") print(f"\nLabel: v2.1.0") print(f"Description: Updated") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Mark Stable

Mark version as stable release.

#!/bin/bash
# Mark stable

aws elasticbeanstalk update-application-version \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --version-label v2.0.0 \
    --description "[STABLE] Production-tested release"

echo "Marked as stable"
{ "ApplicationVersion": { "VersionLabel": "v2.0.0", "Description": "[STABLE] Production-tested release" } } Marked as stable
# Mark stable from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Version Marked Stable") print(f"\nLabel: v2.0.0") print(f"Status: STABLE") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Add Release Notes

Include detailed release notes.

#!/bin/bash
# Release notes

aws elasticbeanstalk update-application-version \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --version-label v2.1.0 \
    --description "v2.1.0: Added Skills filtering, Location search. Fixes: #123, #124"
{ "ApplicationVersion": { "VersionLabel": "v2.1.0", "Description": "v2.1.0: Added Skills filtering, Location search. Fixes: #123, #124" } }
# Release notes from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Release Notes Added") print(f"\nFeatures: Skills filtering, Location search") print(f"Fixes: #123, #124") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Mark Deprecated

Flag old version as deprecated.

#!/bin/bash
# Mark deprecated

aws elasticbeanstalk update-application-version \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --version-label v1.0.0 \
    --description "[DEPRECATED] Use v2.x instead"

echo "Version marked deprecated"
{ "ApplicationVersion": { "VersionLabel": "v1.0.0", "Description": "[DEPRECATED] Use v2.x instead" } } Version marked deprecated
# Deprecated from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Version Deprecated") print(f"\nLabel: v1.0.0") print(f"Status: DEPRECATED") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Batch Update

Update multiple versions.

#!/bin/bash
# Batch update

for version in v1.0.0 v1.1.0 v1.2.0; do
    aws elasticbeanstalk update-application-version \
        --application-name jobpost-django \
        --version-label "$version" \
        --description "[LEGACY] Superseded by v2.x"
    echo "Updated: $version"
done
Updated: v1.0.0 Updated: v1.1.0 Updated: v1.2.0
# Batch update from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost versions = ['v1.0.0', 'v1.1.0', 'v1.2.0'] print(f"Batch Update") for v in versions: print(f"\nUpdated: {v}") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

delete-application-version

Deletes a specific application version. Optionally removes the source bundle from S3. Cannot delete versions deployed to environments.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk delete-application-version \
  --application-name <value> \
  --version-label <value> \
  [--delete-source-bundle] \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string, required): Application name.
  • --version-label (string, required): Version to delete.
  • --delete-source-bundle (boolean, optional): Also delete S3 source.
Return Value
  • No output: Success returns no output.
  • Error: Returns error if version is deployed.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Delete Version

Delete old version keeping S3 source.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete version

aws elasticbeanstalk delete-application-version \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --version-label v1.0.0 \
    --region us-east-1

echo "Version deleted"
Version deleted
# Delete version from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Version Deleted") print(f"\nLabel: v1.0.0") print(f"S3 Source: Retained") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Delete with Source Bundle

Delete version and S3 source.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete with source

aws elasticbeanstalk delete-application-version \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --version-label v1.0.0 \
    --delete-source-bundle

echo "Version and S3 source deleted"
Version and S3 source deleted
# Delete with S3 from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Version Deleted") print(f"\nLabel: v1.0.0") print(f"S3 Source: Deleted") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Clean Old Versions

Delete versions older than 30 days.

#!/bin/bash
# Clean old versions

CUTOFF=$(date -d '30 days ago' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-application-versions \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --query "ApplicationVersions[?DateCreated<'$CUTOFF'].VersionLabel" \
    --output text | while read VERSION; do
    aws elasticbeanstalk delete-application-version \
        --application-name jobpost-django \
        --version-label "$VERSION" \
        --delete-source-bundle
    echo "Deleted: $VERSION"
done
Deleted: v0.9.0 Deleted: v0.8.0
# Clean old from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost deleted = ['v0.9.0', 'v0.8.0'] print(f"Cleaned Old Versions") for v in deleted: print(f"\nDeleted: {v}") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Keep Last N Versions

Keep only last 5 versions.

#!/bin/bash
# Keep last N

KEEP=5

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-application-versions \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --query "ApplicationVersions[$KEEP:].VersionLabel" \
    --output text | while read VERSION; do
    aws elasticbeanstalk delete-application-version \
        --application-name jobpost-django \
        --version-label "$VERSION" \
        --delete-source-bundle 2>/dev/null
done

echo "Kept last $KEEP versions"
Kept last 5 versions
# Keep last N from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Cleanup Complete") print(f"\nKept: 5 versions") print(f"Deleted: Older versions") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Safe Delete Check

Check if deployed before deleting.

#!/bin/bash
# Safe delete

VERSION="v1.5.0"

DEPLOYED=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --query "Environments[?VersionLabel=='$VERSION'].EnvironmentName" \
    --output text)

if [ -z "$DEPLOYED" ]; then
    aws elasticbeanstalk delete-application-version \
        --application-name jobpost-django \
        --version-label "$VERSION"
    echo "Deleted safely"
else
    echo "Cannot delete: deployed to $DEPLOYED"
fi
Deleted safely
# Safe delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Safe Delete") print(f"\nVersion: v1.5.0") print(f"Deployed: No") print(f"Status: Deleted") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

create-environment

Creates a new environment to deploy your Django application. Configure instance type, scaling, load balancer, and environment variables.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk create-environment \
  --application-name <value> \
  --environment-name <value> \
  [--version-label <value>] \
  [--solution-stack-name <value>] \
  [--tier Name=<name>,Type=<type>] \
  [--option-settings <value>] \
  [--cname-prefix <value>] \
  [--tags <value>] \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string, required): Application name.
  • --environment-name (string, required): Environment name.
  • --version-label (string, optional): Version to deploy.
  • --solution-stack-name (string, optional): Platform (e.g., Python 3.12).
  • --tier (structure, optional): WebServer or Worker tier.
  • --option-settings (list, optional): Configuration options.
  • --cname-prefix (string, optional): URL prefix.
Return Value
  • EnvironmentName (string): Created environment name.
  • EnvironmentId (string): Unique environment ID.
  • Status (string): Launch status.
  • CNAME (string): Environment URL.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Create Production Environment

Create load-balanced production environment.

#!/bin/bash
# Create production

aws elasticbeanstalk create-environment \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --version-label v2.1.0 \
    --solution-stack-name "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" \
    --cname-prefix jobpost-prod \
    --option-settings \
        Namespace=aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration,OptionName=InstanceType,Value=t3.medium \
        Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:environment,OptionName=EnvironmentType,Value=LoadBalanced \
    --region us-east-1
{ "EnvironmentName": "jobpost-prod", "EnvironmentId": "e-abc123def", "Status": "Launching", "CNAME": "jobpost-prod.us-east-1.elasticbeanstalk.com" }
# Create production from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Environment Created") print(f"\nName: jobpost-prod") print(f"Status: Launching") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Create with Django Settings

Environment with Django environment variables.

#!/bin/bash
# Django settings

aws elasticbeanstalk create-environment \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --version-label v2.1.0 \
    --solution-stack-name "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" \
    --option-settings \
        Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment,OptionName=DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE,Value=jobpost.settings.production \
        Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment,OptionName=DEBUG,Value=False \
        Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment,OptionName=ALLOWED_HOSTS,Value=.elasticbeanstalk.com
{ "EnvironmentName": "jobpost-prod", "Status": "Launching" }
# Django settings from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Django Configured") print(f"\nDEBUG: False") print(f"Settings: production") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Create Worker Environment

Worker tier for background jobs.

#!/bin/bash
# Worker tier

aws elasticbeanstalk create-environment \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --environment-name jobpost-worker \
    --version-label v2.1.0 \
    --solution-stack-name "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" \
    --tier Name=Worker,Type=SQS/HTTP \
    --option-settings \
        Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:sqsd,OptionName=WorkerQueueURL,Value=https://sqs.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/123456789012/jobpost-jobs
{ "EnvironmentName": "jobpost-worker", "Tier": {"Name": "Worker", "Type": "SQS/HTTP"}, "Status": "Launching" }
# Worker tier from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Worker Created") print(f"\nTier: Worker") print(f"Queue: SQS") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Create Staging Environment

Staging with smaller instance.

#!/bin/bash
# Staging

aws elasticbeanstalk create-environment \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --environment-name jobpost-staging \
    --version-label v2.1.0 \
    --solution-stack-name "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" \
    --cname-prefix jobpost-staging \
    --option-settings \
        Namespace=aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration,OptionName=InstanceType,Value=t3.small \
        Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:environment,OptionName=EnvironmentType,Value=SingleInstance \
    --tags Key=Environment,Value=staging

echo "Staging created"
Staging created
# Staging from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Staging Created") print(f"\nType: SingleInstance") print(f"Instance: t3.small") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Create with RDS Database

Environment connected to RDS.

#!/bin/bash
# RDS connected

aws elasticbeanstalk create-environment \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --version-label v2.1.0 \
    --solution-stack-name "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" \
    --option-settings \
        Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment,OptionName=RDS_HOSTNAME,Value=jobpost-db.abc123.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com \
        Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment,OptionName=RDS_DB_NAME,Value=jobpost_db \
        Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment,OptionName=RDS_USERNAME,Value=jobpost_admin
{ "EnvironmentName": "jobpost-prod", "Status": "Launching" }
# RDS connected from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"RDS Configured") print(f"\nDatabase: jobpost_db") print(f"Host: RDS endpoint") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

describe-environments

Returns descriptions of existing environments. Get status, health, URL, and deployed version for monitoring and deployment verification.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
  [--application-name <value>] \
  [--environment-names <value>...] \
  [--environment-ids <value>...] \
  [--include-deleted] \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string, optional): Filter by application.
  • --environment-names (list, optional): Specific environments.
  • --environment-ids (list, optional): Filter by IDs.
  • --include-deleted (boolean, optional): Include terminated.
Return Value
  • Environments (list): Array of environment objects.
  • Environments[].EnvironmentName (string): Environment name.
  • Environments[].Status (string): Ready, Launching, Updating.
  • Environments[].Health (string): Green, Yellow, Red.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: List All Environments

List all environments for application.

#!/bin/bash
# List environments

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --region us-east-1
{ "Environments": [ { "EnvironmentName": "jobpost-prod", "Status": "Ready", "Health": "Green", "VersionLabel": "v2.1.0" }, { "EnvironmentName": "jobpost-staging", "Status": "Ready", "Health": "Green" } ] }
# List environments from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost envs = ['jobpost-prod', 'jobpost-staging'] print(f"Environments") for env in envs: print(f"\n{env}: Ready") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Get Environment URL

Get production URL.

#!/bin/bash
# Get URL

URL=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --environment-names jobpost-prod \
    --query 'Environments[0].CNAME' \
    --output text)

echo "Production URL: http://$URL"
Production URL: http://jobpost-prod.us-east-1.elasticbeanstalk.com
# Get URL from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Environment URL") print(f"\nURL: jobpost-prod.elasticbeanstalk.com") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Check Environment Health

Get health status.

#!/bin/bash
# Health check

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --environment-names jobpost-prod \
    --query 'Environments[0].[EnvironmentName,Status,Health]' \
    --output table
----------------------------------- | jobpost-prod | Ready | Green | -----------------------------------
# Health check from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Health Status") print(f"\nEnvironment: jobpost-prod") print(f"Status: Ready") print(f"Health: Green") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Get Deployed Version

Check which version is deployed.

#!/bin/bash
# Deployed version

VERSION=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --environment-names jobpost-prod \
    --query 'Environments[0].VersionLabel' \
    --output text)

echo "Deployed version: $VERSION"
Deployed version: v2.1.0
# Deployed version from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Deployed Version") print(f"\nVersion: v2.1.0") print(f"Status: Running") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Monitor Deployment Status

Wait for environment to be ready.

#!/bin/bash
# Monitor status

ENV_NAME="jobpost-prod"

while true; do
    STATUS=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
        --environment-names $ENV_NAME \
        --query 'Environments[0].Status' \
        --output text)
    
    echo "Status: $STATUS"
    
    if [ "$STATUS" == "Ready" ]; then
        echo "Environment ready!"
        break
    fi
    sleep 30
done
Status: Updating Status: Updating Status: Ready Environment ready!
# Monitor status from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Deployment Monitor") print(f"\nStatus: Ready") print(f"Environment: Available") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

update-environment

Updates environment configuration or deploys a new application version. Use for deployments, scaling changes, and configuration updates.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk update-environment \
  --environment-name <value> \
  [--version-label <value>] \
  [--option-settings <value>] \
  [--options-to-remove <value>] \
  [--description <value>] \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --environment-name (string, required): Environment to update.
  • --version-label (string, optional): Deploy this version.
  • --option-settings (list, optional): Configuration changes.
  • --options-to-remove (list, optional): Settings to remove.
Return Value
  • EnvironmentName (string): Updated environment name.
  • Status (string): Update status.
  • VersionLabel (string): Deployed version.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Deploy New Version

Deploy new version to production.

#!/bin/bash
# Deploy version

aws elasticbeanstalk update-environment \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --version-label v2.2.0 \
    --region us-east-1

echo "Deployment started"
{ "EnvironmentName": "jobpost-prod", "VersionLabel": "v2.2.0", "Status": "Updating" } Deployment started
# Deploy version from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Deployment Started") print(f"\nVersion: v2.2.0") print(f"Status: Updating") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Update Instance Type

Scale up to larger instance.

#!/bin/bash
# Scale instance

aws elasticbeanstalk update-environment \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --option-settings \
