        Namespace=aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration,OptionName=InstanceType,Value=t3.large

echo "Scaling to t3.large"
{ "EnvironmentName": "jobpost-prod", "Status": "Updating" } Scaling to t3.large
# Scale instance from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Instance Scaling") print(f"\nNew Type: t3.large") print(f"Status: Updating") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Update Environment Variables

Update Django environment variables.

#!/bin/bash
# Update env vars

aws elasticbeanstalk update-environment \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --option-settings \
        Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment,OptionName=DEBUG,Value=False \
        Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment,OptionName=SECRET_KEY,Value=new-secret-key-here
{ "EnvironmentName": "jobpost-prod", "Status": "Updating" }
# Update env vars from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Environment Variables") print(f"\nDEBUG: False") print(f"SECRET_KEY: Updated") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Configure Auto Scaling

Set auto-scaling min/max instances.

#!/bin/bash
# Auto-scaling

aws elasticbeanstalk update-environment \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --option-settings \
        Namespace=aws:autoscaling:asg,OptionName=MinSize,Value=2 \
        Namespace=aws:autoscaling:asg,OptionName=MaxSize,Value=10

echo "Auto-scaling: 2-10 instances"
Auto-scaling: 2-10 instances
# Auto-scaling from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Auto-Scaling Configured") print(f"\nMin: 2 instances") print(f"Max: 10 instances") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Deploy with Rolling Update

Deploy with rolling update (no downtime).

#!/bin/bash
# Rolling deploy

aws elasticbeanstalk update-environment \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --version-label v2.2.0 \
    --option-settings \
        Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:command,OptionName=DeploymentPolicy,Value=Rolling \
        Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:command,OptionName=BatchSize,Value=30

echo "Rolling deployment: 30% at a time"
Rolling deployment: 30% at a time
# Rolling deploy from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Rolling Deployment") print(f"\nPolicy: Rolling") print(f"Batch: 30%") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

terminate-environment

Terminates the specified environment and releases all associated AWS resources. Use for cleanup or decommissioning environments.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk terminate-environment \
  --environment-name <value> \
  [--terminate-resources] \
  [--force-terminate] \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --environment-name (string, required): Environment to terminate.
  • --terminate-resources (boolean, optional): Delete AWS resources.
  • --force-terminate (boolean, optional): Force immediate termination.
Return Value
  • EnvironmentName (string): Terminated environment name.
  • Status (string): Terminating status.
  • DateUpdated (timestamp): Termination time.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Terminate Environment

Terminate staging environment.

#!/bin/bash
# Terminate staging

aws elasticbeanstalk terminate-environment \
    --environment-name jobpost-staging \
    --region us-east-1

echo "Termination started"
{ "EnvironmentName": "jobpost-staging", "Status": "Terminating", "DateUpdated": "2024-01-15T14:00:00.000Z" } Termination started
# Terminate staging from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Environment Terminating") print(f"\nName: jobpost-staging") print(f"Status: Terminating") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Force Terminate

Force immediate termination.

#!/bin/bash
# Force terminate

aws elasticbeanstalk terminate-environment \
    --environment-name jobpost-test \
    --force-terminate

echo "Force terminated"
Force terminated
# Force terminate from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Force Termination") print(f"\nEnvironment: Removed") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Wait for Termination

Terminate and wait for completion.

#!/bin/bash
# Wait for termination

ENV_NAME="jobpost-staging"

aws elasticbeanstalk terminate-environment \
    --environment-name $ENV_NAME

echo "Waiting for termination..."
aws elasticbeanstalk wait environment-terminated \
    --environment-names $ENV_NAME

echo "Environment terminated"
Waiting for termination... Environment terminated
# Wait termination from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Termination Wait") print(f"\nStatus: Complete") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Terminate Old Environment

Terminate after blue-green deploy.

#!/bin/bash
# Blue-green cleanup

OLD_ENV="jobpost-prod-blue"

HEALTH=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --environment-names jobpost-prod-green \
    --query 'Environments[0].Health' \
    --output text)

if [ "$HEALTH" == "Green" ]; then
    aws elasticbeanstalk terminate-environment \
        --environment-name $OLD_ENV
    echo "Old environment terminated"
else
    echo "New env not healthy"
fi
Old environment terminated
# Blue-green cleanup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Blue-Green Cleanup") print(f"\nOld Env: Terminated") print(f"New Env: Active") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Cleanup Test Environments

Terminate all test environments.

#!/bin/bash
# Cleanup tests

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --query "Environments[?contains(EnvironmentName,'test')].EnvironmentName" \
    --output text | while read ENV; do
    aws elasticbeanstalk terminate-environment \
        --environment-name "$ENV"
    echo "Terminating: $ENV"
done
Terminating: jobpost-test-1 Terminating: jobpost-test-2
# Cleanup tests from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost terminated = ['test-1', 'test-2'] print(f"Test Cleanup") for t in terminated: print(f"\nTerminated: {t}") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

restart-app-server

Restarts the application server running on each instance. Quick way to apply configuration changes or recover from issues without full redeployment.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk restart-app-server \
  --environment-name <value> \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --environment-name (string, required): Environment to restart.
  • --region (string, optional): AWS region.
Return Value
  • No output: Success returns no output.
  • Note: Restart initiated on all instances.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Restart Production

Restart Django app servers.

#!/bin/bash
# Restart production

aws elasticbeanstalk restart-app-server \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --region us-east-1

echo "App servers restarting"
App servers restarting
# Restart production from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Server Restart") print(f"\nEnvironment: jobpost-prod") print(f"Status: Restarting") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Restart After Config Change

Restart to pick up new environment variables.

#!/bin/bash
# Config change restart

ENV="jobpost-prod"

aws elasticbeanstalk update-environment \
    --environment-name $ENV \
    --option-settings \
        Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment,OptionName=CACHE_TTL,Value=3600

aws elasticbeanstalk restart-app-server \
    --environment-name $ENV

echo "Config applied with restart"
Config applied with restart
# Config restart from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Config Applied") print(f"\nCACHE_TTL: 3600") print(f"Servers: Restarted") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Recovery Restart

Restart to recover from memory issues.

#!/bin/bash
# Recovery restart

ENV="jobpost-prod"

HEALTH=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-health \
    --environment-name $ENV \
    --attribute-names HealthStatus \
    --query 'HealthStatus' \
    --output text)

if [ "$HEALTH" != "Ok" ]; then
    aws elasticbeanstalk restart-app-server \
        --environment-name $ENV
    echo "Recovery restart initiated"
fi
Recovery restart initiated
# Recovery restart from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Recovery Restart") print(f"\nHealth: Degraded") print(f"Action: Restart") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Restart All Environments

Restart all environments in application.

#!/bin/bash
# Restart all

for ENV in $(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --application-name jobpost-django \
    --query 'Environments[?Status==`Ready`].EnvironmentName' \
    --output text); do
    aws elasticbeanstalk restart-app-server \
        --environment-name "$ENV"
    echo "Restarted: $ENV"
done
Restarted: jobpost-prod Restarted: jobpost-staging
# Restart all from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost envs = ['jobpost-prod', 'jobpost-staging'] print(f"All Restarted") for env in envs: print(f"\n{env}: Restarted") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Restart with Verification

Restart and verify health.

#!/bin/bash
# Restart with verify

ENV="jobpost-prod"

aws elasticbeanstalk restart-app-server \
    --environment-name $ENV

echo "Waiting for restart..."
sleep 60

HEALTH=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-health \
    --environment-name $ENV \
    --query 'HealthStatus' \
    --output text)

echo "Health after restart: $HEALTH"
Waiting for restart... Health after restart: Ok
# Restart verify from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Restart Verified") print(f"\nHealth: Ok") print(f"Status: Running") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

rebuild-environment

Deletes and recreates all AWS resources in the environment. Use when deep infrastructure issues require a fresh start while keeping configuration.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk rebuild-environment \
  --environment-name <value> \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --environment-name (string, required): Environment to rebuild.
  • --region (string, optional): AWS region.
Return Value
  • No output: Success returns no output.
  • Note: Rebuild initiated, takes 5-10 minutes.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Rebuild Environment

Full rebuild of environment.

#!/bin/bash
# Rebuild environment

aws elasticbeanstalk rebuild-environment \
    --environment-name jobpost-staging \
    --region us-east-1

echo "Rebuild started (5-10 minutes)"
Rebuild started (5-10 minutes)
# Rebuild environment from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Environment Rebuilding") print(f"\nName: jobpost-staging") print(f"Duration: 5-10 min") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Rebuild After Corruption

Rebuild when environment is corrupted.

#!/bin/bash
# Rebuild corrupted

ENV="jobpost-prod"

echo "Warning: This will cause downtime"
read -p "Continue? (y/n): " CONFIRM

if [ "$CONFIRM" == "y" ]; then
    aws elasticbeanstalk rebuild-environment \
        --environment-name $ENV
    echo "Rebuild initiated"
fi
Warning: This will cause downtime Continue? (y/n): y Rebuild initiated
# Rebuild corrupted from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Corruption Recovery") print(f"\nAction: Rebuild") print(f"Status: In Progress") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Rebuild and Monitor

Rebuild with status monitoring.

#!/bin/bash
# Monitor rebuild

ENV="jobpost-staging"

aws elasticbeanstalk rebuild-environment \
    --environment-name $ENV

while true; do
    STATUS=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
        --environment-names $ENV \
        --query 'Environments[0].Status' \
        --output text)
    echo "Status: $STATUS"
    [ "$STATUS" == "Ready" ] && break
    sleep 30
done

echo "Rebuild complete"
Status: Updating Status: Updating Status: Ready Rebuild complete
# Monitor rebuild from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Rebuild Monitor") print(f"\nStatus: Ready") print(f"Complete: Yes") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Rebuild with Health Check

Rebuild only if unhealthy.

#!/bin/bash
# Health-based rebuild

ENV="jobpost-prod"

HEALTH=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-health \
    --environment-name $ENV \
    --query 'HealthStatus' \
    --output text)

if [ "$HEALTH" == "Severe" ] || [ "$HEALTH" == "Degraded" ]; then
    echo "Health: $HEALTH - Rebuilding..."
    aws elasticbeanstalk rebuild-environment \
        --environment-name $ENV
else
    echo "Health: $HEALTH - No rebuild needed"
fi
Health: Severe - Rebuilding...
# Health-based rebuild from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Health-Based Rebuild") print(f"\nHealth: Severe") print(f"Action: Rebuilding") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Scheduled Rebuild

Rebuild during maintenance window.

#!/bin/bash
# Scheduled rebuild

ENV="jobpost-staging"
HOUR=$(date +%H)

if [ "$HOUR" -ge 2 ] && [ "$HOUR" -lt 4 ]; then
    aws elasticbeanstalk rebuild-environment \
        --environment-name $ENV
    echo "Maintenance rebuild started"
else
    echo "Outside maintenance window"
fi
Maintenance rebuild started
# Scheduled rebuild from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Scheduled Rebuild") print(f"\nWindow: 2-4 AM") print(f"Status: Started") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

swap-environment-cnames

Swaps the CNAMEs between two environments for blue-green deployments. Instantly redirect traffic from one environment to another with zero downtime.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk swap-environment-cnames \
  --source-environment-name <value> \
  --destination-environment-name <value> \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --source-environment-name (string, required): First environment.
  • --destination-environment-name (string, required): Second environment.
  • --region (string, optional): AWS region.
Return Value
  • No output: Success returns no output.
  • Note: CNAMEs swapped instantly.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Blue-Green Swap

Swap production traffic to new environment.

#!/bin/bash
# Blue-green swap

aws elasticbeanstalk swap-environment-cnames \
    --source-environment-name jobpost-prod-blue \
    --destination-environment-name jobpost-prod-green \
    --region us-east-1

echo "Traffic swapped to green environment"
Traffic swapped to green environment
# Blue-green swap from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"CNAME Swap") print(f"\nFrom: blue") print(f"To: green") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Promote Staging to Prod

Swap staging URL with production.

#!/bin/bash
# Promote staging

aws elasticbeanstalk swap-environment-cnames \
    --source-environment-name jobpost-staging \
    --destination-environment-name jobpost-prod

echo "Staging promoted to production URL"
Staging promoted to production URL
# Promote staging from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Staging Promoted") print(f"\nStaging: Now production URL") print(f"Production: Now staging URL") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Rollback with Swap

Quick rollback by swapping back.

#!/bin/bash
# Rollback swap

OLD_ENV="jobpost-prod-v1"
NEW_ENV="jobpost-prod-v2"

echo "Rolling back to previous version..."
aws elasticbeanstalk swap-environment-cnames \
    --source-environment-name $NEW_ENV \
    --destination-environment-name $OLD_ENV

echo "Rollback complete"
Rolling back to previous version... Rollback complete
# Rollback swap from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Rollback Complete") print(f"\nActive: v1") print(f"Standby: v2") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Safe Swap with Health Check

Only swap if new environment is healthy.

#!/bin/bash
# Safe swap

NEW_ENV="jobpost-prod-green"
OLD_ENV="jobpost-prod-blue"

HEALTH=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-health \
    --environment-name $NEW_ENV \
    --query 'HealthStatus' \
    --output text)

if [ "$HEALTH" == "Ok" ]; then
    aws elasticbeanstalk swap-environment-cnames \
        --source-environment-name $OLD_ENV \
        --destination-environment-name $NEW_ENV
    echo "Swap complete"
else
    echo "New env unhealthy: $HEALTH"
fi
Swap complete
# Safe swap from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Safe Swap") print(f"\nHealth: Verified") print(f"Swap: Complete") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Verify Swap

Swap and verify URLs changed.

#!/bin/bash
# Verify swap

ENV1="jobpost-prod-blue"
ENV2="jobpost-prod-green"

URL1_BEFORE=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --environment-names $ENV1 \
    --query 'Environments[0].CNAME' --output text)

aws elasticbeanstalk swap-environment-cnames \
    --source-environment-name $ENV1 \
    --destination-environment-name $ENV2

URL1_AFTER=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --environment-names $ENV1 \
    --query 'Environments[0].CNAME' --output text)

echo "Before: $URL1_BEFORE"
echo "After: $URL1_AFTER"
Before: jobpost-prod.elasticbeanstalk.com After: jobpost-staging.elasticbeanstalk.com
# Verify swap from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Swap Verified") print(f"\nURLs: Exchanged") print(f"Status: Success") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

describe-environment-health

Returns detailed health information for the environment including status, causes, and request statistics. Essential for monitoring and alerting.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-health \
  --environment-name <value> \
  [--attribute-names <value>...] \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --environment-name (string, required): Environment name.
  • --attribute-names (list, optional): Attributes: Status, Color, Causes, ApplicationMetrics, InstancesHealth, All, HealthStatus, RefreshedAt.
Return Value
  • HealthStatus (string): Ok, Warning, Degraded, Severe.
  • Color (string): Green, Yellow, Red, Grey.
  • Causes (list): Health issue descriptions.
  • ApplicationMetrics (object): Request counts and latency.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Get Health Status

Get environment health status.

#!/bin/bash
# Health status

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-health \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --attribute-names HealthStatus Color \
    --region us-east-1
{ "EnvironmentName": "jobpost-prod", "HealthStatus": "Ok", "Color": "Green" }
# Health status from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Health Status") print(f"\nStatus: Ok") print(f"Color: Green") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Get All Health Data

Get comprehensive health info.

#!/bin/bash
# All health data

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-health \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --attribute-names All
{ "EnvironmentName": "jobpost-prod", "HealthStatus": "Ok", "Color": "Green", "ApplicationMetrics": { "RequestCount": 1250, "StatusCodes": {"Status2xx": 1200, "Status5xx": 5}, "Latency": {"P99": 0.5, "P50": 0.05} } }
# All health data from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"All Health Data") print(f"\nRequests: 1250") print(f"P99 Latency: 0.5s") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Check for Issues

Get health causes (issues).

#!/bin/bash
# Check issues

CAUSES=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-health \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --attribute-names Causes \
    --query 'Causes' \
    --output text)

if [ -n "$CAUSES" ]; then
    echo "Issues found:"
    echo "$CAUSES"
else
    echo "No health issues"
fi
No health issues
# Check issues from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Issue Check") print(f"\nIssues: None") print(f"Status: Healthy") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Get Request Metrics

Get application request metrics.

#!/bin/bash
# Request metrics

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-health \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --attribute-names ApplicationMetrics \
    --query 'ApplicationMetrics.{Requests:RequestCount,P99:Latency.P99,Errors:StatusCodes.Status5xx}'
{ "Requests": 1250, "P99": 0.5, "Errors": 5 }
# Request metrics from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Request Metrics") print(f"\nRequests: 1250") print(f"Errors: 5") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Health Monitoring Script

Continuous health monitoring.

#!/bin/bash
# Health monitoring

ENV="jobpost-prod"

while true; do
    HEALTH=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-health \
        --environment-name $ENV \
        --attribute-names HealthStatus Color \
        --query '[HealthStatus,Color]' \
        --output text)
    
    echo "$(date): $HEALTH"
    
    if echo "$HEALTH" | grep -q "Severe\|Red"; then
        echo "ALERT: Environment unhealthy!"
    fi
    
    sleep 60
done
2024-01-15 14:00:00: Ok Green 2024-01-15 14:01:00: Ok Green
# Health monitoring from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Health Monitor") print(f"\nStatus: Ok") print(f"Continuous: Running") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

describe-instances-health

Returns health information for individual EC2 instances in the environment. Identify problematic instances for troubleshooting.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-instances-health \
  --environment-name <value> \
  [--attribute-names <value>...] \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --environment-name (string, required): Environment name.
  • --attribute-names (list, optional): Attributes: HealthStatus, Color, Causes, ApplicationMetrics, System, All.
Return Value
  • InstanceHealthList (list): Array of instance health objects.
  • InstanceId (string): EC2 instance ID.
  • HealthStatus (string): Ok, Warning, Degraded, Severe.
  • Color (string): Green, Yellow, Red, Grey.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: List Instance Health

Get health for all instances.

#!/bin/bash
# Instance health

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-instances-health \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --attribute-names All \
    --region us-east-1
{ "InstanceHealthList": [ {"InstanceId": "i-0abc123", "HealthStatus": "Ok", "Color": "Green"}, {"InstanceId": "i-0def456", "HealthStatus": "Ok", "Color": "Green"} ] }
# Instance health from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Instance Health") print(f"\nInstances: 2") print(f"Status: All Ok") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Get Instance IDs

List all instance IDs.

#!/bin/bash
# Instance IDs

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-instances-health \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --query 'InstanceHealthList[].InstanceId' \
    --output table
--------------- | InstanceId | --------------- | i-0abc123 | | i-0def456 | ---------------
# Instance IDs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Instance IDs") print(f"\ni-0abc123") print(f"i-0def456") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Find Unhealthy Instances

Find instances with issues.

#!/bin/bash
# Unhealthy instances

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-instances-health \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --attribute-names HealthStatus \
    --query "InstanceHealthList[?HealthStatus!='Ok'].[InstanceId,HealthStatus]" \
    --output table
----------------------------- | InstanceId | HealthStatus | ----------------------------- | i-0problem | Degraded | -----------------------------
# Unhealthy instances from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Unhealthy Instances") print(f"\ni-0problem: Degraded") print(f"Action: Investigate") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Get Instance Metrics

Get CPU and request metrics per instance.

#!/bin/bash
# Instance metrics

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-instances-health \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --attribute-names ApplicationMetrics System \
    --query 'InstanceHealthList[].{ID:InstanceId,CPU:System.CPUUtilization.User,Requests:ApplicationMetrics.RequestCount}'
[ {"ID": "i-0abc123", "CPU": 25.5, "Requests": 450}, {"ID": "i-0def456", "CPU": 30.2, "Requests": 410} ]
# Instance metrics from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Instance Metrics") print(f"\ni-0abc123: CPU 25.5%") print(f"i-0def456: CPU 30.2%") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Count Healthy vs Unhealthy

Summarize instance health counts.

#!/bin/bash
# Health count

ENV="jobpost-prod"

HEALTHY=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-instances-health \
    --environment-name $ENV \
    --query "length(InstanceHealthList[?HealthStatus=='Ok'])" \
    --output text)

TOTAL=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-instances-health \
    --environment-name $ENV \
    --query "length(InstanceHealthList)" \
    --output text)

echo "Healthy: $HEALTHY / $TOTAL"
Healthy: 3 / 3
# Health count from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Health Count") print(f"\nHealthy: 3/3") print(f"Percentage: 100%") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

describe-environment-resources

Returns AWS resources associated with the environment including Auto Scaling groups, load balancers, instances, and queues.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-resources \
  --environment-name <value> \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --environment-name (string, required): Environment name.
Return Value
  • EnvironmentResources (object): Container for all resources.
  • AutoScalingGroups (list): ASG names.
  • Instances (list): EC2 instance IDs.
  • LoadBalancers (list): Load balancer names.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: List All Resources

Get all AWS resources in environment.

#!/bin/bash
# All resources

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-resources \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --region us-east-1
{ "EnvironmentResources": { "EnvironmentName": "jobpost-prod", "AutoScalingGroups": [{"Name": "awseb-e-abc123-ASG"}], "Instances": [{"Id": "i-0abc123"}, {"Id": "i-0def456"}], "LoadBalancers": [{"Name": "awseb-e-a-LB-123"}] } }
# All resources from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Environment Resources") print(f"\nInstances: 2") print(f"Load Balancers: 1") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Get Load Balancer

Get load balancer name.

#!/bin/bash
# Load balancer

LB=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-resources \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --query 'EnvironmentResources.LoadBalancers[0].Name' \
    --output text)

echo "Load Balancer: $LB"
Load Balancer: awseb-e-a-LB-123ABC
# Load balancer from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Load Balancer") print(f"\nName: awseb-e-a-LB-123ABC") print(f"Type: Application LB") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Get Auto Scaling Group

Get ASG for scaling operations.

#!/bin/bash
# Auto Scaling Group

ASG=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-resources \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --query 'EnvironmentResources.AutoScalingGroups[0].Name' \
    --output text)

echo "ASG: $ASG"

# Use with autoscaling commands
aws autoscaling describe-auto-scaling-groups \
    --auto-scaling-group-names $ASG
ASG: awseb-e-abc123-stack-ASG
# Auto Scaling Group from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Auto Scaling Group") print(f"\nASG: awseb-stack-ASG") print(f"Scaling: Enabled") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: List Instance IDs

Get EC2 instance IDs for SSH.

#!/bin/bash
# Instance IDs

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-resources \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --query 'EnvironmentResources.Instances[].Id' \
    --output table
------------- | Id | ------------- | i-0abc123 | | i-0def456 | -------------
# Instance IDs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Instance IDs") print(f"\ni-0abc123") print(f"i-0def456") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Get Queue URL (Worker)

Get SQS queue for worker environment.

#!/bin/bash
# Worker queue

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-resources \
    --environment-name jobpost-worker \
    --query 'EnvironmentResources.Queues[0].URL' \
    --output text
https://sqs.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/123456789012/awseb-worker-queue
# Worker queue from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Worker Queue") print(f"\nQueue: awseb-worker-queue") print(f"Type: SQS") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

describe-events

Returns events for the environment including deployments, configuration changes, and health transitions. Essential for debugging and audit trails.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-events \
  [--application-name <value>] \
  [--environment-name <value>] \
  [--severity <value>] \
  [--start-time <value>] \
  [--end-time <value>] \
  [--max-records <value>] \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --environment-name (string, optional): Filter by environment.
  • --severity (string, optional): TRACE, DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR, FATAL.
  • --start-time (timestamp, optional): Events after this time.
  • --max-records (integer, optional): Max events to return.
Return Value
  • Events (list): Array of event objects.
  • EventDate (timestamp): When event occurred.
  • Message (string): Event description.
  • Severity (string): Event severity level.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Get Recent Events

Get recent events for environment.

#!/bin/bash
# Recent events

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-events \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --max-records 10 \
    --region us-east-1
{ "Events": [ {"EventDate": "2026-01-14T14:00:00Z", "Message": "Health Ok", "Severity": "INFO"}, {"EventDate": "2026-01-14T13:55:00Z", "Message": "Version deployed", "Severity": "INFO"} ] }
# Recent events from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Recent Events") print(f"\nHealth Ok") print(f"Version deployed") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Get Errors Only

Filter for error events.

#!/bin/bash
# Error events

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-events \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --severity ERROR \
    --max-records 20
{ "Events": [ {"EventDate": "2026-01-13T10:00:00Z", "Message": "Deployment failed", "Severity": "ERROR"} ] }
# Error events from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Error Events") print(f"\nDeployment failed") print(f"Action: Investigate") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Events in Time Range

Get events from last hour.

#!/bin/bash
# Time range events

START=$(date -d '1 hour ago' --iso-8601=seconds)

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-events \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --start-time $START \
    --query 'Events[].[EventDate,Severity,Message]' \
    --output table
---------------------------------------------------------- | EventDate | Severity | Message | ---------------------------------------------------------- | 2026-01-14T14:00:00Z | INFO | Health Ok | | 2026-01-14T13:55:00Z | INFO | Deployed v2.1.0 | ----------------------------------------------------------
# Time range events from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Time Range Events") print(f"\nLast hour: 2 events") print(f"Status: INFO") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Deployment Events

Track deployment progress.

#!/bin/bash
# Deployment events

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-events \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --max-records 50 \
    --query "Events[?contains(Message,'deploy') || contains(Message,'version')].[EventDate,Message]" \
    --output table
------------------------------------------------------ | EventDate | Message | ------------------------------------------------------ | 2026-01-14T13:55:00Z | New version deployed | | 2026-01-14T13:50:00Z | Deploying version v2.1.0 | ------------------------------------------------------
# Deployment events from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Deployment Events") print(f"\nDeployed: v2.1.0") print(f"Status: Success") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Monitor Events Live

Tail events during deployment.

#!/bin/bash
# Live events

ENV="jobpost-prod"
LAST_TIME=$(date --iso-8601=seconds)

while true; do
    aws elasticbeanstalk describe-events \
        --environment-name $ENV \
        --start-time $LAST_TIME \
        --query 'Events[].Message' \
        --output text
    
    LAST_TIME=$(date --iso-8601=seconds)
    sleep 10
done
Deploying new version v2.1.0 Instance deployment completed Environment health Ok
# Live events from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Live Event Monitor") print(f"\nPolling: 10s") print(f"Status: Active") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

create-configuration-template

Creates a reusable configuration template from an existing environment or from scratch. Use to standardize environment settings across deployments.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk create-configuration-template \
  --application-name <value> \
  --template-name <value> \
  [--solution-stack-name <value>] \
  [--source-configuration ApplicationName=<>,TemplateName=<>] \
  [--environment-id <value>] \
  [--description <value>] \
  [--option-settings <value>] \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string, required): Application name.
  • --template-name (string, required): Template name.
  • --solution-stack-name (string, optional): Platform stack.
  • --environment-id (string, optional): Copy from environment.
  • --option-settings (list, optional): Configuration options.
Return Value
  • TemplateName (string): Template identifier.
  • ApplicationName (string): Application name.
  • Description (string): Template description.
  • SolutionStackName (string): Platform stack.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Create from Environment

Create template from production environment.

#!/bin/bash
# From environment

ENV_ID=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --environment-names jobpost-prod \
    --query 'Environments[0].EnvironmentId' \
    --output text)

aws elasticbeanstalk create-configuration-template \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --template-name prod-config-v1 \
    --environment-id $ENV_ID \
    --description "Production snapshot"
{ "TemplateName": "prod-config-v1", "ApplicationName": "jobpost-app", "Description": "Production snapshot", "SolutionStackName": "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" }
# From environment from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Template from Environment") print(f"\nName: prod-config-v1") print(f"Source: Production") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Create Django Template

Create standard Django template.

#!/bin/bash
# Django template

aws elasticbeanstalk create-configuration-template \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --template-name django-standard \
    --solution-stack-name "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" \
    --option-settings \
        Namespace=aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration,OptionName=InstanceType,Value=t3.medium \
        Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment,OptionName=DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE,Value=jobpost.settings.production
{ "TemplateName": "django-standard", "SolutionStackName": "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" }
# Django template from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Django Template") print(f"\nName: django-standard") print(f"Instance: t3.medium") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: High-Availability Template

Create HA production template.

#!/bin/bash
# HA template

aws elasticbeanstalk create-configuration-template \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --template-name ha-production \
    --solution-stack-name "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" \
    --option-settings \
        Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:environment,OptionName=EnvironmentType,Value=LoadBalanced \
        Namespace=aws:autoscaling:asg,OptionName=MinSize,Value=2 \
        Namespace=aws:autoscaling:asg,OptionName=MaxSize,Value=10 \
    --description "HA with auto-scaling"
{ "TemplateName": "ha-production", "Description": "HA with auto-scaling" }
# HA template from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"HA Template") print(f"\nMin: 2, Max: 10") print(f"Type: LoadBalanced") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Development Template

Create low-cost dev template.

#!/bin/bash
# Dev template

aws elasticbeanstalk create-configuration-template \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --template-name dev-single \
    --solution-stack-name "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" \
    --option-settings \
        Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:environment,OptionName=EnvironmentType,Value=SingleInstance \
        Namespace=aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration,OptionName=InstanceType,Value=t3.micro \
        Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment,OptionName=DEBUG,Value=True
{ "TemplateName": "dev-single", "Description": "Development single instance" }
# Dev template from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Dev Template") print(f"\nInstance: t3.micro") print(f"Debug: True") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Copy Existing Template

Create new template based on existing.

#!/bin/bash
# Copy template

aws elasticbeanstalk create-configuration-template \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --template-name prod-config-v2 \
    --source-configuration ApplicationName=jobpost-app,TemplateName=prod-config-v1 \
    --description "Updated production config"

echo "Template copied successfully"
Template copied successfully
# Copy template from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Template Copied") print(f"\nFrom: v1") print(f"To: v2") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

describe-configuration-settings

Returns configuration settings for an environment or configuration template. Shows all configurable options and their current values.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-configuration-settings \
  --application-name <value> \
  [--template-name <value>] \
  [--environment-name <value>] \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string, required): Application name.
  • --template-name (string, optional): Template to describe (mutually exclusive with environment-name).
  • --environment-name (string, optional): Environment to describe.
Return Value
  • ConfigurationSettings (list): Array of configuration objects.
  • OptionSettings (list): Name-value pairs of settings.
  • Namespace (string): Option namespace.
  • Value (string): Option value.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Get Environment Settings

Get current environment configuration.

#!/bin/bash
# Environment settings

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-configuration-settings \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --query 'ConfigurationSettings[0].OptionSettings' \
    --output table
{ "ConfigurationSettings": [{ "ApplicationName": "jobpost-app", "EnvironmentName": "jobpost-prod", "OptionSettings": [ {"Namespace": "aws:elasticbeanstalk:environment", "OptionName": "EnvironmentType", "Value": "LoadBalanced"} ] }] }
# Environment settings from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Environment Settings") print(f"\nType: LoadBalanced") print(f"Retrieved: Success") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Get Template Settings

Get template configuration.

#!/bin/bash
# Template settings

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-configuration-settings \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --template-name prod-config-v1
{ "ConfigurationSettings": [{ "TemplateName": "prod-config-v1", "SolutionStackName": "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" }] }
# Template settings from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Template Settings") print(f"\nTemplate: prod-config-v1") print(f"Stack: Python 3.12") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Extract Instance Type

Get current instance type.

#!/bin/bash
# Instance type

INSTANCE_TYPE=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-configuration-settings \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --query "ConfigurationSettings[0].OptionSettings[?OptionName=='InstanceType'].Value" \
    --output text)

echo "Instance Type: $INSTANCE_TYPE"
Instance Type: t3.medium
# Instance type from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Instance Type") print(f"\nType: t3.medium") print(f"Memory: 4GB") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Get Environment Variables

Get app environment variables.

#!/bin/bash
# Environment variables

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-configuration-settings \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --query "ConfigurationSettings[0].OptionSettings[?Namespace=='aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment']" \
    --output table
--------------------------------------------------- | Namespace | OptionName | Value | --------------------------------------------------- | ...environment| DJANGO_SETTINGS_... | jobpost...| | ...environment| DATABASE_URL | postgres://| ---------------------------------------------------
# Environment variables from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Environment Variables") print(f"\nDJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE") print(f"DATABASE_URL") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Export Settings to JSON

Export full config for backup.

#!/bin/bash
# Export config

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-configuration-settings \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    > prod-config-backup.json

echo "Configuration exported"
Configuration exported
# Export config from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Config Export") print(f"\nFile: prod-config-backup.json") print(f"Status: Complete") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

describe-configuration-options

Lists all available configuration options for a solution stack or saved configuration. Use to discover what settings can be modified.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-configuration-options \
  [--application-name <value>] \
  [--template-name <value>] \
  [--environment-name <value>] \
  [--solution-stack-name <value>] \
  [--options <value>] \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string, optional): Application name.
  • --solution-stack-name (string, optional): Platform stack to query.
  • --environment-name (string, optional): Environment name.
  • --options (list, optional): Specific options to describe.
Return Value
  • SolutionStackName (string): Platform stack name.
  • Options (list): Available configuration options.
  • Namespace (string): Option namespace.
  • DefaultValue (string): Default value if any.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: List All Options for Platform

Get all options for Python platform.

#!/bin/bash
# Platform options

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-configuration-options \
    --solution-stack-name "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" \
    --query 'Options[].{Namespace:Namespace,Name:Name}' \
    --output table
{ "SolutionStackName": "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12", "Options": [ {"Namespace": "aws:autoscaling:asg", "Name": "MinSize", "DefaultValue": "1"}, {"Namespace": "aws:autoscaling:asg", "Name": "MaxSize", "DefaultValue": "4"} ] }
# Platform options from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Platform Options") print(f"\nMinSize: Default 1") print(f"MaxSize: Default 4") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Get Instance Type Options

Get valid instance types.

#!/bin/bash
# Instance types

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-configuration-options \
    --solution-stack-name "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" \
    --options Namespace=aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration,OptionName=InstanceType \
    --query 'Options[0].ValueOptions'
[ "t3.micro", "t3.small", "t3.medium", "t3.large", "m5.large" ]
# Instance types from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Instance Types") print(f"\nt3.micro, t3.small") print(f"t3.medium, t3.large") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Get Autoscaling Options

Get autoscaling config options.

#!/bin/bash
# ASG options

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-configuration-options \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --query "Options[?Namespace=='aws:autoscaling:asg']"
[ {"Namespace": "aws:autoscaling:asg", "Name": "MinSize", "MinValue": 1, "MaxValue": 10000}, {"Namespace": "aws:autoscaling:asg", "Name": "MaxSize", "MinValue": 1, "MaxValue": 10000}, {"Namespace": "aws:autoscaling:asg", "Name": "Cooldown", "DefaultValue": "360"} ]
# ASG options from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"ASG Options") print(f"\nMinSize: 1-10000") print(f"Cooldown: 360s") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Get Environment Variable Options

Check environment variable namespace.

#!/bin/bash
# Env var options

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-configuration-options \
    --solution-stack-name "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" \
    --query "Options[?Namespace=='aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment']"
[ { "Namespace": "aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment", "Name": "*", "UserDefined": true, "ValueType": "Scalar" } ]
# Env var options from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Env Var Options") print(f"\nUserDefined: true") print(f"Type: Scalar") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: List All Namespaces

Get unique namespaces.

#!/bin/bash
# All namespaces

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-configuration-options \
    --solution-stack-name "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12" \
    --query 'Options[].Namespace' \
    --output text | tr '\t' '\n' | sort -u
aws:autoscaling:asg aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration aws:autoscaling:trigger aws:elasticbeanstalk:application aws:elasticbeanstalk:environment aws:elb:loadbalancer
# All namespaces from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Config Namespaces") print(f"\nautoscaling:asg") print(f"elasticbeanstalk:environment") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

update-configuration-template

Modifies an existing configuration template with new option settings. Use to update templates without recreating them.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk update-configuration-template \
  --application-name <value> \
  --template-name <value> \
  [--description <value>] \
  [--option-settings <value>] \
  [--options-to-remove <value>] \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string, required): Application name.
  • --template-name (string, required): Template to update.
  • --option-settings (list, optional): Options to add or change.
  • --options-to-remove (list, optional): Options to remove.
Return Value
  • TemplateName (string): Updated template name.
  • ApplicationName (string): Application name.
  • DateUpdated (timestamp): When updated.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Update Instance Type

Upgrade instance type in template.

#!/bin/bash
# Update instance type

aws elasticbeanstalk update-configuration-template \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --template-name prod-config-v1 \
    --option-settings \
        Namespace=aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration,OptionName=InstanceType,Value=t3.large
{ "TemplateName": "prod-config-v1", "ApplicationName": "jobpost-app", "DateUpdated": "2026-01-14T10:30:00Z" }
# Update instance type from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Instance Type Updated") print(f"\nFrom: t3.medium") print(f"To: t3.large") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Update Autoscaling Limits

Scale up autoscaling limits.

#!/bin/bash
# Update ASG limits

aws elasticbeanstalk update-configuration-template \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --template-name ha-production \
    --option-settings \
        Namespace=aws:autoscaling:asg,OptionName=MinSize,Value=3 \
        Namespace=aws:autoscaling:asg,OptionName=MaxSize,Value=15
{ "TemplateName": "ha-production", "Description": "Updated autoscaling limits" }
# Update ASG limits from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"ASG Updated") print(f"\nMin: 3") print(f"Max: 15") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Add Environment Variable

Add new Django setting.

#!/bin/bash
# Add env var

aws elasticbeanstalk update-configuration-template \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --template-name prod-config-v1 \
    --option-settings \
        Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment,OptionName=CACHE_TTL,Value=3600
{ "TemplateName": "prod-config-v1", "DateUpdated": "2026-01-14T10:35:00Z" }
# Add env var from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Env Var Added") print(f"\nCACHE_TTL: 3600") print(f"Status: Added") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Remove Option

Remove deprecated setting.

#!/bin/bash
# Remove option

aws elasticbeanstalk update-configuration-template \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --template-name prod-config-v1 \
    --options-to-remove \
        Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment,OptionName=LEGACY_MODE
{ "TemplateName": "prod-config-v1", "DateUpdated": "2026-01-14T10:40:00Z" }
# Remove option from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Option Removed") print(f"\nLEGACY_MODE: Removed") print(f"Status: Complete") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Update Description

Update template metadata.

#!/bin/bash
# Update description

aws elasticbeanstalk update-configuration-template \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --template-name prod-config-v1 \
    --description "Production config v1.2 - Updated autoscaling and cache settings"

echo "Template description updated"
Template description updated
# Update description from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Description Updated") print(f"\nVersion: v1.2") print(f"Changes: ASG, Cache") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

delete-configuration-template

Permanently removes a configuration template from an application. This action cannot be undone.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk delete-configuration-template \
  --application-name <value> \
  --template-name <value> \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string, required): Application name.
  • --template-name (string, required): Template to delete.
Return Value
  • No output: Success returns no content.
  • Error: Returns error if template not found.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Delete Old Template

Delete deprecated template.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete template

aws elasticbeanstalk delete-configuration-template \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --template-name old-config-v1

echo "Template deleted"
Template deleted
# Delete template from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Template Deleted") print(f"\nName: old-config-v1") print(f"Status: Removed") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Delete with Confirmation

Delete with safety check.

#!/bin/bash
# Confirm delete

TEMPLATE="dev-test-config"
read -p "Delete template $TEMPLATE? (y/n): " confirm

if [ "$confirm" = "y" ]; then
    aws elasticbeanstalk delete-configuration-template \
        --application-name jobpost-app \
        --template-name $TEMPLATE
    echo "Template deleted"
fi
Delete template dev-test-config? (y/n): y Template deleted
# Confirm delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Confirmed Deletion") print(f"\nTemplate: dev-test-config") print(f"Confirmed: Yes") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Cleanup Old Templates

Delete multiple templates.

#!/bin/bash
# Batch cleanup

for template in old-v1 old-v2 deprecated-config; do
    aws elasticbeanstalk delete-configuration-template \
        --application-name jobpost-app \
        --template-name $template 2>/dev/null && \
    echo "Deleted: $template"
done
Deleted: old-v1 Deleted: old-v2 Deleted: deprecated-config
# Batch cleanup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Batch Cleanup") print(f"\nDeleted: 3 templates") print(f"Status: Complete") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Delete Before Recreate

Delete and recreate template.

#!/bin/bash
# Recreate template

aws elasticbeanstalk delete-configuration-template \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --template-name prod-config-v1 2>/dev/null

aws elasticbeanstalk create-configuration-template \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --template-name prod-config-v1 \
    --environment-id $ENV_ID

echo "Template recreated"
Template recreated
# Recreate template from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Template Recreated") print(f"\nOld: Deleted") print(f"New: Created") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Check Before Delete

Verify template exists before deletion.

#!/bin/bash
# Safe delete

TEMPLATE="staging-config"

if aws elasticbeanstalk describe-configuration-settings \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --template-name $TEMPLATE &>/dev/null; then
    aws elasticbeanstalk delete-configuration-template \
        --application-name jobpost-app \
        --template-name $TEMPLATE
    echo "Template $TEMPLATE deleted"
else
    echo "Template not found"
fi
Template staging-config deleted
# Safe delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Safe Delete") print(f"\nChecked: Exists") print(f"Deleted: Yes") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

validate-configuration-settings

Validates configuration settings before applying them to an environment. Checks for errors and warnings to prevent deployment failures.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk validate-configuration-settings \
  --application-name <value> \
  [--template-name <value>] \
  [--environment-name <value>] \
  --option-settings <value> \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string, required): Application name.
  • --template-name (string, optional): Template context.
  • --environment-name (string, optional): Environment context.
  • --option-settings (list, required): Settings to validate.
Return Value
  • Messages (list): Validation messages.
  • Message (string): Error or warning text.
  • Severity (string): error or warning.
  • Namespace (string): Setting namespace.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Validate Instance Type

Validate before applying.

#!/bin/bash
# Validate instance type

aws elasticbeanstalk validate-configuration-settings \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --option-settings \
        Namespace=aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration,OptionName=InstanceType,Value=t3.large
{ "Messages": [] }
# Validate instance type from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Validation Result") print(f"\nMessages: None") print(f"Status: Valid") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Detect Invalid Value

Test invalid instance type.

#!/bin/bash
# Invalid value test

aws elasticbeanstalk validate-configuration-settings \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --option-settings \
        Namespace=aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration,OptionName=InstanceType,Value=t3.fake
{ "Messages": [ {"Message": "Invalid value for InstanceType: t3.fake", "Severity": "error"} ] }
# Invalid value from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Validation Error") print(f"\nError: Invalid InstanceType") print(f"Value: t3.fake") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Validate Multiple Settings

Validate batch of settings.

#!/bin/bash
# Batch validate

aws elasticbeanstalk validate-configuration-settings \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --option-settings \
        Namespace=aws:autoscaling:asg,OptionName=MinSize,Value=2 \
        Namespace=aws:autoscaling:asg,OptionName=MaxSize,Value=10 \
        Namespace=aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration,OptionName=InstanceType,Value=t3.medium
{ "Messages": [] }
# Batch validate from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Batch Validation") print(f"\nSettings: 3") print(f"Status: All Valid") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Check Template Settings

Validate against template.

#!/bin/bash
# Template validate

aws elasticbeanstalk validate-configuration-settings \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --template-name prod-config-v1 \
    --option-settings \
        Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment,OptionName=DATABASE_URL,Value=postgres://localhost/db
{ "Messages": [ {"Message": "Check DATABASE_URL format", "Severity": "warning"} ] }
# Template validate from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Template Validation") print(f"\nWarning: DB URL format") print(f"Status: Warning") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Validation Script

Validate and only apply if valid.

#!/bin/bash
# Safe apply

RESULT=$(aws elasticbeanstalk validate-configuration-settings \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --option-settings \
        Namespace=aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration,OptionName=InstanceType,Value=t3.large \
    --query 'Messages[?Severity==`error`]' \
    --output text)

if [ -z "$RESULT" ]; then
    echo "Validation passed - applying..."
else
    echo "Validation failed: $RESULT"
fi
Validation passed - applying...
# Safe apply from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Safe Apply") print(f"\nValidated: Yes") print(f"Applied: Yes") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

list-available-solution-stacks

Returns a list of available platform versions (solution stacks) that can be used to create environments. Use to find Python/Django-compatible platforms.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk list-available-solution-stacks \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --region (string, optional): AWS region.
Return Value
  • SolutionStacks (list): Available platform names.
  • SolutionStackDetails (list): Detailed platform info.
  • PermittedFileTypes (list): Allowed deployment types.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: List Python Platforms

Find Python platforms for Django.

#!/bin/bash
# Python platforms

aws elasticbeanstalk list-available-solution-stacks \
    --query "SolutionStacks[?contains(@, 'Python')]" \
    --output table
[ "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12", "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.11", "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.9" ]
# Python platforms from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Python Platforms") print(f"\nPython 3.12") print(f"Python 3.11") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Get Latest Python 3.12

Get latest Python 3.12 stack.

#!/bin/bash
# Latest Python

STACK=$(aws elasticbeanstalk list-available-solution-stacks \
    --query "SolutionStacks[?contains(@, 'Python 3.12')] | [0]" \
    --output text)

echo "Latest: $STACK"
Latest: 64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12
# Latest Python from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Latest Python") print(f"\nVersion: 3.12") print(f"OS: Amazon Linux 2023") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: List All Docker Platforms

Find Docker platforms.

#!/bin/bash
# Docker platforms

aws elasticbeanstalk list-available-solution-stacks \
    --query "SolutionStacks[?contains(@, 'Docker')]"
[ "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Docker", "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running ECS", "64bit Amazon Linux 2 v3.5.0 running Docker" ]
# Docker platforms from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Docker Platforms") print(f"\nDocker") print(f"ECS") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: List Amazon Linux 2023 Stacks

Get all AL2023 platforms.

#!/bin/bash
# AL2023 platforms

aws elasticbeanstalk list-available-solution-stacks \
    --query "SolutionStacks[?contains(@, 'Amazon Linux 2023')]" \
    --output text | tr '\t' '\n'
64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12 64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.11 64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Node.js 20 64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Docker
# AL2023 platforms from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"AL2023 Platforms") print(f"\nPython, Node.js") print(f"Docker available") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Export All Stacks

Export full list for reference.

#!/bin/bash
# Export platforms

aws elasticbeanstalk list-available-solution-stacks \
    --query 'SolutionStacks' \
    > available-platforms.json

echo "Platforms exported"
Platforms exported
# Export platforms from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Platforms Exported") print(f"\nFile: available-platforms.json") print(f"Count: 45") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

check-dns-availability

Checks if a CNAME prefix is available for use when creating a new Elastic Beanstalk environment. The CNAME creates a subdomain under elasticbeanstalk.com.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk check-dns-availability \
  --cname-prefix <value> \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --cname-prefix (string) [Required]: Desired CNAME prefix (subdomain) to check for availability
  • --region (string) [Optional]: AWS region for the DNS availability check
Return Value
  • Available (boolean): Whether the CNAME prefix is available for use
  • FullyQualifiedCNAME (string): Full domain name if available (prefix.region.elasticbeanstalk.com)
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Check DNS Availability for Production

Check if anihpj-prod CNAME is available for Django deployment.

#!/bin/bash
# Check production CNAME availability

PREFIX="anihpj-prod"

RESULT=$(aws elasticbeanstalk check-dns-availability \
    --cname-prefix "$PREFIX" \
    --output json)

AVAILABLE=$(echo $RESULT | jq -r '.Available')
CNAME=$(echo $RESULT | jq -r '.FullyQualifiedCNAME')

if [ "$AVAILABLE" = "true" ]; then
    echo "CNAME available: $CNAME"
else
    echo "CNAME not available"
fi
CNAME available: anihpj-prod.us-east-1.elasticbeanstalk.com
# Check production DNS from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Production DNS Check") print(f"\nPrefix: anihpj-prod") print(f"Available: True") print(f"CNAME: anihpj-prod.us-east-1.elasticbeanstalk.com") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Check Unavailable DNS Name

Handle case when CNAME is already taken.

#!/bin/bash
# Check common name availability

PREFIX="production"

AVAILABLE=$(aws elasticbeanstalk check-dns-availability \
    --cname-prefix "$PREFIX" \
    --query 'Available' \
    --output text)

if [ "$AVAILABLE" = "False" ]; then
    echo "Name '$PREFIX' is already taken"
    echo "Try a more unique prefix"
fi
Name 'production' is already taken Try a more unique prefix
# Check unavailable DNS from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"DNS Check Result") print(f"\nPrefix: production") print(f"Available: False") print(f"Status: Already taken") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Find Available DNS Variation

Loop through variations to find available CNAME.

#!/bin/bash
# Find available CNAME variation

BASE="anihpj-django"
SUFFIXES=("" "-app" "-web" "-api" "-prod")

for SUFFIX in "${SUFFIXES[@]}"; do
    PREFIX="${BASE}${SUFFIX}"
    RESULT=$(aws elasticbeanstalk check-dns-availability \
        --cname-prefix "$PREFIX")
    
    AVAILABLE=$(echo $RESULT | jq -r '.Available')
    
    if [ "$AVAILABLE" = "true" ]; then
        CNAME=$(echo $RESULT | jq -r '.FullyQualifiedCNAME')
        echo "Found available: $PREFIX"
        echo "URL: http://$CNAME"
        break
    else
        echo "Taken: $PREFIX"
    fi
done
Taken: anihpj-django Found available: anihpj-django-app URL: http://anihpj-django-app.us-east-1.elasticbeanstalk.com
# Find available variation from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"DNS Variation Search") print(f"\nBase: anihpj-django") print(f"Found: anihpj-django-app") print(f"Available: True") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Check Multiple Environment CNAMEs

Validate all environment names before deployment.

#!/bin/bash
# Check all environment CNAMEs

ENVIRONMENTS=("anihpj-dev" "anihpj-staging" "anihpj-prod")
ALL_AVAILABLE=true

echo "Checking environment CNAMEs..."
for ENV in "${ENVIRONMENTS[@]}"; do
    AVAILABLE=$(aws elasticbeanstalk check-dns-availability \
        --cname-prefix "$ENV" \
        --query 'Available' \
        --output text)
    
    if [ "$AVAILABLE" = "True" ]; then
        echo "$ENV: Available"
    else
        echo "$ENV: Not available"
        ALL_AVAILABLE=false
    fi
done

if [ "$ALL_AVAILABLE" = true ]; then
    echo "All environment CNAMEs are available!"
fi
Checking environment CNAMEs... anihpj-dev: Available anihpj-staging: Available anihpj-prod: Available All environment CNAMEs are available!
# Check multiple environments from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Multi-Environment Check") print(f"\nanihpj-dev: Available") print(f"anihpj-staging: Available") print(f"anihpj-prod: Available") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Export DNS Check to Configuration

Save available CNAME to environment configuration file.

#!/bin/bash
# Export DNS check result to config

APP_NAME="anihpj-jobpost"
ENV_TYPE="prod"

PREFIX="${APP_NAME}-${ENV_TYPE}"

RESULT=$(aws elasticbeanstalk check-dns-availability \
    --cname-prefix "$PREFIX")

AVAILABLE=$(echo $RESULT | jq -r '.Available')
FQDN=$(echo $RESULT | jq -r '.FullyQualifiedCNAME')

if [ "$AVAILABLE" = "true" ]; then
    echo "CNAME_PREFIX=$PREFIX" > env.config
    echo "FQDN=$FQDN" >> env.config
    echo "DNS_AVAILABLE=true" >> env.config
    echo "Configuration saved to env.config"
    cat env.config
else
    echo "CNAME not available, cannot proceed"
    exit 1
fi
Configuration saved to env.config CNAME_PREFIX=anihpj-jobpost-prod FQDN=anihpj-jobpost-prod.us-east-1.elasticbeanstalk.com DNS_AVAILABLE=true
# Export DNS config from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"DNS Configuration Exported") print(f"\nPrefix: anihpj-jobpost-prod") print(f"FQDN: anihpj-jobpost-prod.us-east-1.elasticbeanstalk.com") print(f"Config: env.config") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

create-storage-location

Creates an S3 bucket for Elastic Beanstalk to store application versions, logs, and other artifacts. Called automatically when needed, but can be invoked manually.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk create-storage-location \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --region (string) [Optional]: AWS region for the storage bucket creation
Return Value
  • S3Bucket (string): Name of the S3 bucket created or already existing (elasticbeanstalk-region-account-id format)
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Create Storage Bucket for Django App

Initialize Elastic Beanstalk storage bucket for application deployment.

#!/bin/bash
# Create Elastic Beanstalk storage bucket

echo "Creating EB storage location..."

RESULT=$(aws elasticbeanstalk create-storage-location \
    --output json)

BUCKET=$(echo $RESULT | jq -r '.S3Bucket')

echo "Storage bucket: $BUCKET"
echo "Ready for Django application uploads"
Creating EB storage location... Storage bucket: elasticbeanstalk-us-east-1-123456789012 Ready for Django application uploads
# Create storage bucket from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"EB Storage Created") print(f"\nBucket: elasticbeanstalk-us-east-1-123456789012") print(f"Region: us-east-1") print(f"Status: Ready") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Create Storage in Specific Region

Create storage bucket in EU region for compliance.

#!/bin/bash
# Create storage in EU region

REGION="eu-west-1"

BUCKET=$(aws elasticbeanstalk create-storage-location \
    --region "$REGION" \
    --query 'S3Bucket' \
    --output text)

echo "EU Storage bucket: $BUCKET"
echo "Region: $REGION"
EU Storage bucket: elasticbeanstalk-eu-west-1-123456789012 Region: eu-west-1
# Create EU storage from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"EU Storage Created") print(f"\nBucket: elasticbeanstalk-eu-west-1-123456789012") print(f"Region: eu-west-1") print(f"GDPR Compliant: Yes") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Get or Create Storage and List Contents

Ensure storage exists and view existing artifacts.

#!/bin/bash
# Get or create storage and list contents

BUCKET=$(aws elasticbeanstalk create-storage-location \
    --query 'S3Bucket' \
    --output text)

echo "Using bucket: $BUCKET"
echo ""
echo "Current contents:"
aws s3 ls "s3://$BUCKET/" --human-readable

echo ""
echo "Application versions:"
aws s3 ls "s3://$BUCKET/resources/" --recursive 2>/dev/null || echo "No versions yet"
Using bucket: elasticbeanstalk-us-east-1-123456789012 Current contents: PRE resources/ PRE environments/ Application versions: 2024-01-15 10:30:00 5.2 MiB resources/anihpj-v1.zip
# List storage contents from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Storage Contents") print(f"\nBucket: elasticbeanstalk-us-east-1-123456789012") print(f"Folders: resources, environments") print(f"Versions: 1") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Upload Django App to Storage

Create storage and upload application bundle.

#!/bin/bash
# Get bucket and upload Django application

APP_NAME="anihpj-django"
VERSION="v1.0.0"

# Get or create storage bucket
BUCKET=$(aws elasticbeanstalk create-storage-location \
    --query 'S3Bucket' \
    --output text)

echo "Storage bucket: $BUCKET"

# Create application bundle
zip -r "${APP_NAME}-${VERSION}.zip" . -x "*.git*" -x "__pycache__/*"

# Upload to EB storage
aws s3 cp "${APP_NAME}-${VERSION}.zip" \
    "s3://${BUCKET}/${APP_NAME}/versions/${VERSION}.zip"

echo "Django app uploaded: s3://${BUCKET}/${APP_NAME}/versions/${VERSION}.zip"
Storage bucket: elasticbeanstalk-us-east-1-123456789012 adding: application.py (deflated 65%) adding: requirements.txt (deflated 45%) upload: anihpj-django-v1.0.0.zip to s3://elasticbeanstalk-us-east-1-123456789012/anihpj-django/versions/v1.0.0.zip Django app uploaded: s3://elasticbeanstalk-us-east-1-123456789012/anihpj-django/versions/v1.0.0.zip
# Upload Django app from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Django App Uploaded") print(f"\nApp: anihpj-django") print(f"Version: v1.0.0") print(f"Location: s3://elasticbeanstalk-us-east-1-123456789012/") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Export Bucket Name for CI/CD Pipeline

Save bucket name for use in GitHub Actions deployment.

#!/bin/bash
# Export bucket name for CI/CD pipeline

# Get or create storage bucket
BUCKET=$(aws elasticbeanstalk create-storage-location \
    --query 'S3Bucket' \
    --output text)

# Save to file for local use
echo "$BUCKET" > .eb-bucket

# Export for GitHub Actions
if [ -n "$GITHUB_ENV" ]; then
    echo "EB_BUCKET=$BUCKET" >> $GITHUB_ENV
    echo "Exported EB_BUCKET to GitHub Actions"
fi

# Export for GitLab CI
if [ -n "$CI_PROJECT_DIR" ]; then
    echo "export EB_BUCKET=$BUCKET" >> .env
    echo "Exported EB_BUCKET for GitLab CI"
fi

echo "Bucket name saved: $BUCKET"
Exported EB_BUCKET to GitHub Actions Bucket name saved: elasticbeanstalk-us-east-1-123456789012
# Export bucket for CI/CD from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"CI/CD Configuration") print(f"\nBucket: elasticbeanstalk-us-east-1-123456789012") print(f"GitHub Env: EB_BUCKET set") print(f"Local File: .eb-bucket created") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

request-environment-info

Initiates compilation of environment logs or bundle info. The request is asynchronous - use retrieve-environment-info to get results.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk request-environment-info \
  --info-type <value> \
  [--environment-id <value>] \
  [--environment-name <value>] \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --info-type (string) [Required]: Type of information to request - tail (last 100 lines) or bundle (full logs zipped)
  • --environment-id (string) [Optional]: Environment ID to request info from
  • --environment-name (string) [Optional]: Environment name to request info from
  • --region (string) [Optional]: AWS region of the environment
Return Value
  • No return value - request is initiated asynchronously
  • Use retrieve-environment-info after ~30 seconds to get the results
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Request Tail Logs for Django Environment

Request the last 100 lines of logs for debugging Django errors.

#!/bin/bash
# Request tail logs for Django environment

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"

echo "Requesting tail logs for $ENV_NAME..."

aws elasticbeanstalk request-environment-info \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --info-type tail

echo "Log request initiated successfully"
echo "Wait 30 seconds, then use retrieve-environment-info to get logs"
Requesting tail logs for anihpj-prod... Log request initiated successfully Wait 30 seconds, then use retrieve-environment-info to get logs
# Request tail logs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Log Request Initiated") print(f"\nEnvironment: anihpj-prod") print(f"Type: tail (last 100 lines)") print(f"Status: Pending") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Request Full Log Bundle

Request complete log bundle with all application logs.

#!/bin/bash
# Request full log bundle for comprehensive debugging

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"

echo "Requesting full log bundle for $ENV_NAME..."
echo "This may take 1-2 minutes to compile"

aws elasticbeanstalk request-environment-info \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --info-type bundle

echo "Bundle request submitted"
echo "Contains: Apache logs, Django logs, deployment logs"
Requesting full log bundle for anihpj-prod... This may take 1-2 minutes to compile Bundle request submitted Contains: Apache logs, Django logs, deployment logs
# Request full bundle from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Bundle Request Initiated") print(f"\nEnvironment: anihpj-prod") print(f"Type: bundle (full logs)") print(f"Includes: Apache, Django, deployment") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Request Logs by Environment ID

Request logs using environment ID for precise targeting.

#!/bin/bash
# Request logs using environment ID

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"

# Get environment ID first
ENV_ID=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --environment-names "$ENV_NAME" \
    --query 'Environments[0].EnvironmentId' \
    --output text)

echo "Environment ID: $ENV_ID"

# Request logs by ID
aws elasticbeanstalk request-environment-info \
    --environment-id "$ENV_ID" \
    --info-type tail

echo "Log request submitted for environment $ENV_ID"
Environment ID: e-abcd1234ef Log request submitted for environment e-abcd1234ef
# Request by environment ID from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Request by ID") print(f"\nEnvironment ID: e-abcd1234ef") print(f"Type: tail") print(f"Status: Submitted") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Request Logs and Wait for Retrieval

Complete workflow to request and retrieve logs.

#!/bin/bash
# Request logs and wait for availability

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"
INFO_TYPE="tail"

echo "Step 1: Requesting $INFO_TYPE logs..."
aws elasticbeanstalk request-environment-info \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --info-type "$INFO_TYPE"

echo "Step 2: Waiting 30 seconds for compilation..."
sleep 30

echo "Step 3: Retrieving logs..."
aws elasticbeanstalk retrieve-environment-info \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --info-type "$INFO_TYPE" \
    --query 'EnvironmentInfo[0].Message' \
    --output text
Step 1: Requesting tail logs... Step 2: Waiting 30 seconds for compilation... Step 3: Retrieving logs... https://elasticbeanstalk-us-east-1-123456789012.s3.amazonaws.com/resources/environments/logs/tail/...
# Request and wait workflow from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Log Retrieval Workflow") print(f"\nStep 1: Request initiated") print(f"Step 2: Waited 30 seconds") print(f"Step 3: Logs retrieved") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Request Logs for Multiple Environments

Request logs from all Django environments for comparison.

#!/bin/bash
# Request logs from multiple environments

ENVIRONMENTS=("anihpj-dev" "anihpj-staging" "anihpj-prod")
INFO_TYPE="tail"

echo "Requesting $INFO_TYPE logs from all environments..."
